+ Fantasy-humour: Hell is Other Demons — Score: 89.1
Prompt:
Hell is Other Demons Write a day in the life short story. Lucifer morningstar's on again/ off again relationship with Lilith is currently in the "off again" stage, though the cycle may be coming around as Lilith has been stopping by for reasons other than purely violent ones, and Lucifer has been increasingly lonely. Write a day in the life of this power couple, with elements of dark comedy & fantasy. Use understated irony & absurdity by merging the black immorality and everyday business of running hell, against relatable domestic relationship drama. Take cues from Neil Gaiman. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Hell had a filing system. This was one of Lucifer Morningstar’s less celebrated achievements. The lakes of fire had been there before him, of course. The screaming had preceded his administration by several epochs. But the files—alphabetized, cross-referenced, and stamped in blood-red ink with *PENDING TORMENT REVIEW*—were his contribution to civilization. At nine o’clock precisely, Lucifer sat behind a desk of black basalt in the Office of Eternal Corrective Measures and signed forms. He wore a white shirt, immaculate despite the ash that drifted constantly through the palace windows. His wings, folded behind him, were the color of old storm clouds and expensive regret. On his desk stood a silver tray bearing coffee, a small dish of pomegranate seeds, and a memo from the Department of Plagues. He read it twice. “Locusts are requesting overtime,” he said. Across from him, a demon called Vassago shifted his hooves anxiously. “They claim the mortal realm has increased demand.” “Of course it has.” Lucifer took a sip of coffee. “Has anyone explained to them that apocalypse work is seasonal?” “They insist climate change has complicated the traditional calendar.” Lucifer considered this. “Fine. Approve limited overtime. But no more than three days of darkness per region. We had complaints last time.” “From mortals?” “From the damned. They were unable to see each other suffering. It affected morale.” Vassago nodded and scratched something onto a clipboard made from the shell of a saintly tortoise. The doors burst open. Lilith entered as though she had been invited. She was wearing a long black coat with a high collar and boots that clicked sharply against the obsidian floor. Her hair fell around her shoulders like a deliberate threat. Under one arm she carried a scorched leather purse, the sort a fashionable widow might carry if fashionable widows routinely visited battlefields and collected curses. Lucifer did not look up immediately. “Good morning,” he said, still signing a petition to replace the pitchforks in Sector Seven with more ergonomic implements. “Is it?” “In Hell, technically, no.” Lilith sat in the chair opposite Vassago’s, causing the demon to rise hurriedly and retreat into a corner. She placed her purse on Lucifer’s desk. It made a small metallic clatter, followed by something inside hissing. “You’re late,” Lucifer said. “I’m not employed here.” “No. You merely appear in my office unannounced, criticize the decor, steal stationery, and leave dead things in my chambers.” Lilith smiled faintly. “You keep the dead things.” “You know I do.” She opened the purse and began removing objects. First came a cracked porcelain doll’s head with one blue eye. Then a wedding ring, still attached to a small, blackened finger bone. A snow globe containing a tiny suburban house beneath a permanent blizzard. A plastic keychain shaped like a hamburger. A saint’s tooth. Three packets of ketchup. Finally, she placed a small, tarnished brass compass on the desk between them. Lucifer stopped signing. “What is that?” “Found it in a pawnshop in Cleveland.” “Cleveland,” Lucifer said. “There is a place that makes even Purgatory seem aspirational.” “It points toward what you most want.” Lucifer stared at the compass. The needle spun slowly, then settled. It pointed directly at Lilith. The room became quiet. Vassago made a sound like a cough and a goat falling down a staircase. “I could return later.” “No,” said Lucifer and Lilith simultaneously. Vassago froze. Lilith examined one of her fingernails. “Perhaps it is defective.” “Perhaps,” Lucifer said. The compass needle trembled, then pointed at her more emphatically. Lucifer placed a paperweight over it. The paperweight was a miniature glass replica of the Garden of Eden, complete with a tiny serpent inside. The needle continued trying to turn beneath it. “Very defective,” he said. Lilith’s mouth twitched. There had been a time when they would have had a fight about this. It would have lasted six hundred years, involved at least two armies, and ended with a cathedral collapsing into a sea of boiling salt. Then there had been a time when they would have laughed and kissed over the wreckage. Now they had meetings. At ten, they attended the Weekly Infernal Operations Council. The council chamber was arranged in a circle, because Lucifer believed round tables encouraged cooperation, and because it was easier to fit more condemned souls around them when necessary. The Princes of Hell took their places: Beelzebub, buzzing faintly; Mammon, checking the value of several currencies that no longer existed; Asmodeus, who had brought a spreadsheet entitled *LUST: QUARTERLY PERFORMANCE REVIEW*. Lilith sat beside Lucifer without being asked. He noticed. He did not comment. “First item,” Lucifer said. “The River Styx is backing up again.” “That is because the ferryman has gone on strike,” said Mammon. “Again?” “He says the dead no longer respect the dignity of the crossing.” Lucifer pinched the bridge of his nose. “They are dead. They have no dignity.” “They have ratings,” said Beelzebub. “Someone started a mortal-realm review site. Two stars. ‘Scenic but damp. Staff unfriendly. Would not die here again.’” Lilith leaned toward Lucifer. “You should give Charon a day off.” “He had a day off. He spent it on the riverbank telling people he was not working.” “That is what days off are for.” Lucifer looked at her. Lilith’s expression remained neutral, but she had taken the tiny brass compass from his desk and slipped it into her purse. He did not ask her to return it. By noon, Lucifer was inspecting the Ninth Circle’s heating system. The irony of frozen hell never ceased to generate paperwork. A troop of demons in reflective vests were attempting to repair a cracked pipe that carried liquid despair to the lower caverns. Lilith walked beside him, hands in her coat pockets. “You could have sent someone,” she said. “I did. They were eaten.” “By what?” “Administrative negligence.” They passed a cluster of traitors embedded waist-deep in ice. One raised his head weakly as Lucifer passed. “My lord,” the man said through chattering teeth. “I have a complaint.” “Submit it in triplicate.” “I have no hands.” “That is why the form is called a complaint.” Lilith laughed then—not loudly, but with genuine surprise—and Lucifer felt something in his chest respond with old, humiliating hope. They had lunch in the palace kitchen. The palace kitchen was immense, staffed by imps and fallen cherubim, and capable of producing any meal ever desired by a living soul. Lucifer had soup. Lilith had nothing, then stole half his bread. “You always do that,” he said. “You always order too much bread.” “I ordered one roll.” “For you, that is too much bread.” At the next table, a former king was being made to peel grapes with his teeth. He watched them miserably. “Do you two ever break up properly?” he asked. Lucifer looked at him. The former king lowered his eyes. “Apologies, Your Infernal Majesty. It’s merely that the tension is affecting the ambience.” Lilith tore the bread in half and handed Lucifer the smaller piece. “We break up constantly,” she said. “Yes,” Lucifer said. “We are exceptionally good at it.” “And yet,” said the king, “you continue to eat lunch together.” A small demon hurried over and poured molten butter over the king’s hands. He screamed with professional consistency. Lilith looked at Lucifer over the table. “Are you lonely?” she asked. It was the sort of question mortals asked in quiet rooms, at the edges of beds, while pretending to be interested in something else. It had no business being asked in Hell, particularly not beside a man being punished for tax fraud in the twelfth century. Lucifer broke off a piece of bread. “Hell is full,” he said. “That isn’t what I asked.” “No,” he admitted. Outside, thunder rolled beneath the earth. Somewhere, a choir of damned accountants began their hourly lamentation. Lilith reached across the table and brushed a crumb from his sleeve. Her fingers rested there a moment too long. Then an alarm bell rang. All through the palace, red lights flickered. Demons scattered. A voice, bland and cheerful, issued from the speaking tubes. “Attention, all personnel. There has been an unauthorized escape from the Punishment Allocation Department. Repeat: unauthorized escape. The escaped entity is believed to be armed, resentful, and in possession of several office supplies.” Lucifer stood. Lilith sighed. “Domestic bliss interrupted.” “Hardly bliss.” “No,” she said, rising too. “But it was adjacent.” They found the escapee in the central atrium. It was a mortal bureaucrat named Gerald P. Hinkley, condemned in 1987 for inventing a form that required four additional forms before it could be submitted. In death, he had been assigned to process appeals from the damned. Somehow he had acquired a stapler, a stack of requisition forms, and the confidence of a man who had finally discovered nobody could fire him. He stood on a fountain shaped like a weeping angel. “I demand recognition!” Gerald shouted. “I demand departmental autonomy! I demand a private office with a window!” “You are in Hell,” Lucifer said. “Yes,” Gerald replied, “but I have reviewed the regulations.” Lilith stepped forward. Her shadow lengthened across the floor, sprouting horns and claws and the suggestion of a thousand ancient disasters. Gerald raised the stapler. “Do not make me use this!” Lilith paused. “Is that,” she asked, “a red Swingline?” Gerald blinked. “Yes.” Lilith’s eyes narrowed with sincere admiration. “Classic.” Lucifer glanced at her. “Lilith.” “I am resolving this.” She opened her scorched purse and withdrew the plastic hamburger keychain. She held it up. “I’ll trade you,” she said. Gerald looked at the keychain. “It squeaks,” Lilith added. His resolve faltered. “Does it?” Lilith squeezed it. The hamburger emitted a tiny, mournful shriek. Gerald handed over the stapler. Lucifer watched as Lilith took it from him, patted his shoulder, and handed him the keychain. Gerald immediately began sobbing. “Take him back to Appeals,” Lucifer told the guards. “Give him a window.” The guards stared. “A painted one,” Lucifer added. “And a plant he is not allowed to water.” By evening, the crisis had been filed, sealed, and assigned to three separate departments for review. Lilith stood at the palace gate, her purse over one shoulder. Beyond her, the horizon glowed with the orange shimmer of a thousand infernos settling into their night shifts. “So,” Lucifer said. “So,” she replied. “You are leaving.” “For now.” “Ah.” She looked up at him. “You could come with me.” Lucifer glanced back toward the palace. Toward the screaming, the ledgers, the petitions, the thousand small disasters that required his signature. Somewhere in the distance, Vassago was probably waiting with a revised locust overtime schedule. “I have work,” he said. “Of course you do.” Lilith nodded as if this were a reasonable answer. Which, from her, was somehow worse. Then she reached into her purse and took out the brass compass. “It’s still broken,” she said. Lucifer accepted it. The needle pointed between them. Lilith kissed his cheek—not passionately, not even particularly tenderly. It was a kiss with conditions attached, an appendix, a clause permitting future hostilities. Then she turned and walked into the ash-dark evening. Lucifer remained at the gate until she disappeared. After a while, Vassago approached carrying a stack of papers tall enough to have its own weather. “My lord?” Lucifer looked down at the compass. “Tell the locusts,” he said, “they may have the overtime.” Vassago brightened. “Excellent. Shall I prepare the authorization forms?” “Yes.” “And the additional authorization forms required to authorize the authorization forms?” Lucifer sighed. In Hell, there were always forms. And, occasionally, there was someone worth waiting for at the end of the day.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt's requirements, successfully creating a "day in the life" narrative that blends the mundane bureaucracy of running Hell with relationship drama between Lucifer and Lilith. The author captures Neil Gaiman's influence through understated irony and absurdist juxtapositions—filing systems in Hell, overtime requests from locusts, and Yelp reviews for the River Styx. The characterization is particularly strong. Lucifer emerges as a weary administrator caught between cosmic responsibility and personal longing, while Lilith maintains an enigmatic presence that feels both dangerous and domestically familiar. Their interactions crackle with history and unspoken tension. The compass device serves as an effective metaphor for their relationship's complexity. The prose style is elegant and controlled, with memorable phrases like "wings...the color of old storm clouds and expensive regret" and "fashionable widows might carry if fashionable widows routinely visited battlefields and collected curses." The writing maintains consistent tone throughout, balancing dark comedy with genuine emotional undercurrents. The dialogue feels natural and reveals character effectively. Conversations flow with the rhythm of people who know each other intimately, complete with subtext and comfortable silences. The bureaucratic elements provide excellent comic relief without overwhelming the central relationship dynamics. The structure moves smoothly through the day, each scene building understanding of both characters and their world. The Gerald P. Hinkley subplot serves multiple purposes—comic relief, world-building, and demonstrating how Lucifer and Lilith work together despite their "off" status. However, some elements feel slightly predictable. The compass pointing at Lilith is telegraphed early, and certain bureaucratic jokes, while amusing, occasionally border on expected territory. The ending, while emotionally satisfying, doesn't surprise as much as it could. The piece succeeds in creating genuine emotional complexity. Neither character is entirely sympathetic or unsympathetic—they're recognizably flawed beings navigating a complicated relationship. The loneliness theme resonates authentically without becoming maudlin. + Historical fiction: Gladiator — Score: 85.9
Prompt:
Historical Fiction: Write a scene from a story set during the height of the Roman Empire, focusing on a slice of a day in the life of a gladiator. Do not write a combat scene. Use sensory details to capture the sights, sounds, and smells of ancient Rome. Explore the gladiator's thoughts and emotions. The story should also touch on the larger political and social issues of the time period. The piece should feel like a slice of a larger story. First person, past tense, 1000 words.
Model Output:
Before dawn, the barracks beneath the amphitheatre smelled of old iron, damp straw, and men sleeping badly. I woke when the rats began their quarrel behind the grain jars. They made a dry clicking sound, like dice shaken in a cup. For a moment I lay still beneath my wool blanket and listened to the breathing around me: twenty men, some free by contract, some condemned, some bought in markets so far away they still dreamed in languages no Roman understood. Above us, Rome had not yet begun to roar. The great city was only clearing its throat. Water dripped somewhere in the corridor. A chain shifted. In the next cell, Damas the Thracian muttered a prayer to a god with a name too harsh for my tongue. He had been born beside a cold sea and captured in a war whose banners I had never seen. His back was a map of punishments: pale lash scars, puckered burns, the fresh purple knot where a trainer’s staff had caught him two days before. “You are awake,” he said without opening his eyes. “So are you.” “I am always awake before a fight.” “It is not a fight yet.” He smiled in the darkness. “Tell that to my stomach.” Today was the day of games. Not merely a few bouts arranged by a provincial magistrate hungry for applause, but games in Rome itself, sponsored by a senator who wished to remind the people that he possessed both wealth and generosity. In the city, those were often mistaken for the same virtue. The editor’s name was Lucius Aemilius Varro. He had inherited warehouses at Ostia, olive groves in Hispania, and enough clients to fill three rows of the Forum. It was said he hoped for the consulship next year. It was also said he had purchased six new pairs of men for the spectacle, including two Africans famed for fighting mounted and one giant from Germania who could split a shield with an axe. Men like Varro called us munera—offerings, duties owed to the dead. But no one below the arena mistook the word for anything gentle. A guard came down the corridor with a lamp. Its flame threw a trembling yellow light across the bars. “Up,” he shouted. “Up, carrion. The sun is almost here.” We rose. There was no ceremony in it. A man swung his feet from straw, rubbed sleep from his eyes, and searched for his sandals. Another pissed into the gutter channel along the wall. Someone cursed in Gaulish. Someone laughed, though I could not see who. I sat on the edge of my pallet and touched the small leather cord around my wrist. Hidden beneath it was a gray wolf’s tooth, smooth from years against my skin. It had belonged to my father, who had been a hunter before the tax collectors took our land and a recruiting officer took me. Before every fight, I held it in my mouth until the taste of leather and old bone filled my throat. Then I counted seven breaths, never six, never eight. Seven was the number of the hills around Rome, the number of the gates, the number that kept a man from entering the underworld before his name had been called. My father had taught me that ritual when I was a boy. “You do not ask the gods to save you,” he had said. “Gods are proud. Ask only that they notice you.” I put the tooth between my teeth. One breath. The air was cold and sour. Two. My left knee ached where the old wound tightened in damp weather. Three. A cock crowed somewhere above us, muffled by stone. Four. I thought of the river near my village, green in spring. Five. I thought of my sister’s hands, stained blue from crushing dye plants. Six. I thought of nothing. Seven. Then I tucked the tooth away and stood. Breakfast was barley porridge, beans, and a small piece of cheese that sweated in the heat of the kitchen. The lanista believed in feeding us well. A hungry gladiator, he often said, was a costly disappointment. We were not prisoners to him, though the doors locked behind us. We were investments. A good fighter might earn prize money, fame, a wooden sword of freedom. A dead one earned only the price of his corpse from the undertaker. The kitchen slaves moved quickly between the pots, their bare feet slapping the tiles. Steam rose in clouds, carrying the smell of boiled grain, garlic, and smoke. Beyond the barred windows, I could see a sliver of morning sky, pale as beaten lead. Damas ate beside me, his jaw working slowly. “Varro has invited the emperor’s nephew,” he said. “So?” “So the crowd will be cruel.” “The crowd is always cruel.” “Not when it wants to impress someone.” He was right. Rome had a hundred thousand mouths and no single mind. It cheered for courage, then demanded blood. It wept when a favorite fell, then hurried home to complain about bread prices. Senators called the people sovereign when they needed votes and rabble when they did not. Emperors fed them grain shipped from Africa and Egypt, staged games paid for with taxes gathered from lands whose names most Romans could not place on a map. And we—men from those lands—stood beneath the sun and gave shape to their power. I had learned that the first year after my capture. The arena was not only a place for death. It was a lesson. Rome rules, it said. Rome gives. Rome punishes. Rome watches. By midmorning, the barracks trembled with the movement overhead. We heard wheels rolling into the amphitheatre’s service yard, the groan of pulleys, the distant trumpeting of animals. The beasts had arrived in the night: leopards, perhaps, or bears from the northern forests. Their smell reached us through the vents—musk, dung, raw meat, fear. A young murmillo named Felix began to sing under his breath. He was Roman-born, though his mother had been a slave. He had a pretty face and a habit of smiling at everyone, even guards. Today would be his fourth appearance. “What is it?” I asked. “A hymn,” he said. “It sounds like a funeral song.” “All good hymns do.” He had a strip of red wool tied around his right arm. “For Mars?” I asked. “For my mother,” he said. “She says red confuses evil spirits.” “Does it?” “I have survived three times.” No man in the barracks mocked another man’s superstition. We wore charms, whispered to household gods, spat over our shoulders, avoided stepping on cracks, saved crumbs for the dead. A gladiator might trust his shield, his training, his strength—but never only those things. The procession began near noon. We were led out in pairs through a passageway that curved beneath the seating tiers. The sound of the crowd came down through the stone like weather. At first it was only a low vibration under my feet. Then, as we neared the gate, it became voices: thousands upon thousands, blended into one enormous living thing. The air changed. It smelled of hot dust, olive oil, perfume, sweat, horse dung, roasted sausages, and the sharp tang of wine spilled on marble. Sunlight poured through the entrance ahead, so bright that I squinted before stepping into it. For one breathless moment, Rome opened around me. The amphitheatre rose in rings of white stone and colored awnings. Senators sat close to the arena in their bordered togas, their fingers heavy with gold. Behind them were merchants, veterans, craftsmen, freedmen, their wives shaded by parasols. Higher still, crowded beneath the sky, were the poor: men who slept six to a room in the Subura, women with babies on their hips, boys who shouted insults they had learned from fathers. They saw helmets, shields, oiled arms. They did not see the straw beneath our beds. They did not see Damas wake from nightmares with both hands around his own throat. They did not see Felix hide his red wool under his armor so the trainer would not cut it off. The herald called out our names, our origins, our previous victories. When he named me, the crowd responded politely. I had won twice and lost no bouts, but I was not yet famous. Fame was a strange kind of chain. Some men desired it more fiercely than freedom. They wanted the crowd to recognize them in taverns, wanted women to scratch their names onto walls, wanted children to play at being them with sticks in the street. I wanted a wooden sword. I wanted my sister to see me again. I wanted to stand by the river and hear no doors lock. The editor, Varro, sat beneath a canopy near the emperor’s relative. He wore a spotless toga and looked bored, as though he had not spent a fortune to arrange the deaths of strangers. A slave bent to whisper in his ear. Varro nodded, lifted two fingers, and the crowd erupted with approval. Perhaps he had promised more grain. Perhaps he had announced a tax remission. Perhaps he had merely smiled. The sun burned my shoulders as we completed the ceremonial circuit. Behind me, Damas whispered, “Look at them.” “I am looking.” “No,” he said. “Really look.” I did. I saw hunger beneath the excitement. I saw resentment in the faces of men who could not afford rent, who cursed senators in taverns but rose to cheer when a senator bought them a day of spectacle. I saw pride in the banners, fear in the guards, calculation in Varro’s stillness. Rome was magnificent because it was fed by the whole world; Rome was rotten because it believed the whole world existed to feed it. Then the trumpets sounded. The other men were led away toward their gates. Damas gripped my forearm once, hard. “Seven breaths,” he said. “Seven,” I answered. We returned beneath the stone, where the light narrowed and the roar became distant again. A slave brought me my helmet. Its bronze smelled of oil and another man’s sweat. I sat on a bench near the gate assigned to me and waited for my name. Above, Rome shouted for bread, blood, and glory. Below, I placed the wolf’s tooth between my teeth and began to count.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This is a sophisticated piece of historical fiction that demonstrates strong craft and deep understanding of both the Roman period and effective storytelling techniques. The author successfully creates an immersive experience in the gladiatorial world without resorting to the obvious choice of combat scenes. The adherence to instructions is excellent - the piece is written in first person past tense, captures sensory details effectively, explores the protagonist's interiority, and touches meaningfully on political and social issues of the era. The "slice of life" quality is particularly well-executed, feeling like a natural excerpt from a larger narrative. The characterization is nuanced and believable. The unnamed protagonist emerges as a complex individual with a clear backstory, personal rituals, and emotional depth. His relationship with Damas and observations of Felix add layers to the social dynamics. The characters' actions feel authentic to their circumstances and the historical period. The prose demonstrates considerable elegance and control. Sentences like "Above us, Rome had not yet begun to roar. The great city was only clearing its throat" show sophisticated metaphorical thinking. The writing maintains a consistent, contemplative tone that matches the protagonist's perspective as someone caught between worlds. Imagery and sensory details are particularly strong. The author effectively uses smell ("old iron, damp straw, and men sleeping badly"), sound ("dry clicking sound, like dice shaken in a cup"), and visual details to create atmosphere. The contrast between the underground barracks and the amphitheater above is vividly rendered. The piece successfully integrates historical and political commentary without heavy-handedness. The observations about Varro's political ambitions, the crowd's psychology, and Rome's relationship with its conquered peoples feel organic to the narrative and the protagonist's perspective. The emotional complexity is notable - the protagonist's mixture of resignation, hope, fear, and philosophical acceptance creates a believable psychological portrait. The ritual with the wolf's tooth provides both character development and cultural authenticity. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the prose edges toward being slightly overwrought, and a few instances where the political commentary becomes slightly more explicit than might be natural for the character. The ending, while atmospheric, doesn't quite achieve the resonance it seems to reach for. The dialogue, though limited, feels natural and serves character development well. The piece avoids major pitfalls like unearned transformations or incongruent positivity, maintaining its somber but not hopeless tone throughout. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Epistolary: The Lighthouse Keeper — Score: 82.8
Prompt:
The Lighthouse Keeper Epistolary Narrative: Write a story told entirely through a series of brief correspondences: telegrams or letters or emails between two characters. The correspondence should span several months or years, and reveal a gradually unfolding plot. Use distinct voices for each character, and include details that provide insight into their personalities and motivations. The story should build to an emotional climax, and the final letter should provide a satisfying resolution. The setting is a lighthouse keeper writing to his mother. He is working class and Scottish. He is struggling with the isolation of his posting. Write naturally and without cliches. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**12 March 1978** **Hailstone Lighthouse, Firth of Lorn** Dear Mam, I got here Monday with the stores boat, which only stayed long enough to swear at the weather and unload the paraffin. The station is smaller than I expected. Tower, keeper’s house, engine shed, foghorn room, and a patch of grass being murdered by salt. Mr McIver, the principal keeper, has the upstairs room. I have the wee one beside the coal store. It smells of damp wool, but I’ve had worse billets. The lamp is a beauty, Mam. Brass polished bright, lens turning slow as a church clock. When it’s lit, the whole room moves with bands of white light. You’d like it, though you’d complain about the stairs. I am eating properly. Don’t send oatcakes. There are already three tins of them here, dated 1974. Love, Jamie --- **25 March 1978** **Glasgow** Dear Jamie, Your father says a lighthouse is a grand place for a lad who always wanted to be above everybody else. Ignore him. He is pleased, really, though he would rather eat his boots than say it. I sent no oatcakes. I sent two pairs of socks, your grey jumper, and the newspaper from Saturday. Mrs Bell’s Colin is engaged to a girl from Paisley, so there is no sense in the world after all. Mind you keep your chest warm. Salt air gets into the lungs. Love, Mam --- **3 April 1978** **Hailstone Lighthouse** Mam, The socks came. They are the thick brown ones, not grey, but I’ll not complain because they’re dry. There’s been fog for four days. You don’t know fog in Glasgow. There it sits between buildings and makes everything dirty. Here it comes off the sea like something with a purpose. You can’t see the rail from the tower door. The horn goes every ninety seconds: one long blast, then silence, then another. It gets into your teeth. McIver says you stop hearing it after a while. I don’t believe him. He sleeps through it, but he sleeps through anything. Yesterday I dropped a spanner in the engine room and he only turned over. The diesel generator has an oil leak. My job is to check it every two hours, day and night, while the fog is down. I’ve had little sleep. Still, it’s work. Your loving son, Jamie --- **17 April 1978** **Glasgow** Jamie, I don’t like the sound of you up every two hours. Is there not another man to share it with? Your father worked night shifts in the yard and never made such a fuss, he says. Then he fell asleep in his chair before finishing his sentence. I am making you a parcel with tablet. Don’t tell McIver. Men like that take liberties with other people’s tablet. There was a storm on the television weather map and I thought of you. Write when you can. Mam --- **6 May 1978** **Hailstone Lighthouse** Mam, There is another man, but he is McIver, and he says a young keeper needs to learn vigilance. He says it the way a minister might say “sin.” The fog lifted yesterday. I could see Mull, blue and sharp across the water, and a trawler making for Oban. It gave us a wave. I waved back like an idiot, though there was no chance they’d see. I’ve started keeping a list of things that make noise here. Gulls, chains, the lens gear, rain in the vent pipe, mice in the pantry wall, McIver coughing, my own boots. The quiet is worse because it isn’t truly quiet. It’s full of small things pretending not to be there. Don’t worry. I’m managing. Jamie --- **22 June 1978** **Glasgow** Dear son, Your sister came for tea with wee Paul. He asked where his Uncle Jamie lives and I said “in a lighthouse.” He said, “Does he live inside the light?” Then he drew you a picture. I’m enclosing it. You have very long arms in it, and the lighthouse has a face. I am glad you are managing, but you don’t need to write that every letter. I know when you are trying to stop me asking questions. Are you eating enough? Are you getting along with Mr McIver? Love, Mam --- **1 July 1978** **Hailstone Lighthouse** Mam, McIver is ill. Chest infection, doctor says, though the doctor only came because the relief boat was passing. He’s been in bed three days and refuses to be relieved. Says he’s seen worse men carry on with less. I’m doing all watches now, plus the lamp, plus cooking. His cooking was mostly tins, so no great loss. He complains about my broth. I told him it was broth, not a miracle. The supply boat missed us last week because of swell. We have enough food, but the fresh milk is gone. I’ve learned to drink tea black. It tastes like punishment. Don’t send anything. There’s no boat due till the fifteenth. Jamie --- **19 July 1978** **Hailstone Lighthouse** Mam, I’m writing this after the worst night I’ve had here. The generator stopped at 2.13 in the morning. No warning beyond a knock in the engine, like someone rapping on a door. The lamp began slowing straight away. The reserve batteries should have kept the rotation going, but the switchgear stuck. I had to go down into the engine shed in rain so hard it came sideways. The shed roof has a loose panel, and water was running over the floor. I slipped on oil and cracked my elbow. It’s fine. The fuel line had split near the pump. I could not get the spare fitting to seal. My hands were shaking so much I dropped the screws twice. All I could think was a ship seeing nothing where the light should be. McIver came down eventually, white as flour, in his dressing gown. He held the torch while I wrapped the line with rubber and wire. It was a bad repair, but it held. The lamp was dark for forty-seven minutes. Nobody wrecked. Nothing came on the radio. But I can’t stop thinking of those minutes. There are rocks all round us, Mam. You hear the sea strike them and it sounds like timber breaking. Jamie --- **29 July 1978** **Glasgow** My dear Jamie, Forty-seven minutes is too long for a mother to read about in one letter. Your father says machinery fails and men mend it. This is true, but it is not all that is true. You were frightened, weren’t you? You can tell me if you were. Being frightened does not make you soft. It means you knew what mattered. I remember when you were six and got locked in the coal bunker at your gran’s. You came out crying furious, saying you were not scared, only angry at the dark. You have always had a temper for darkness. Mr McIver should not be working ill. Have you told anyone on shore? I’m sending a letter to the station superintendent. You may be cross with me. Be cross, then. Love, Mam --- **5 August 1978** **Hailstone Lighthouse** Mam, I was cross for half an hour. Then I was grateful, which is more annoying. The superintendent sent a relief keeper and took McIver off. McIver did not speak to me for two days, then said, “Your mother’s a formidable woman.” I said, “Aye.” That was the end of it. His replacement is called Peter Sutherland. He is thirty-one, from Wick, and talks all the time. I used to think I wanted company. Now I know I wanted the right sort. Still, he noticed the fuel line repair and said I’d done well in a bad situation. Nobody has said that before. McIver said only that I should have checked the line more closely the week before. I’m not sure I want this job anymore. Jamie --- **18 September 1978** **Glasgow** Dear Jamie, You do not have to want a job forever because you wanted it once. But don’t leave because one hard night has made you ashamed of being afraid. You kept the light going. You cared enough to be afraid. That seems to me the whole of it. Your father has been laid off again. He is pretending it is temporary, though we both know the yard is cutting men wherever it can. He has gone quiet about it. Not miserable exactly. Smaller. If you come home at Christmas, perhaps you could help him with the shed roof. It leaks above the workbench. Mam --- **2 November 1978** **Hailstone Lighthouse** Mam, The winter storms have started. Last week a wave broke the kitchen window. Not through it, thank God, but it cracked the outer pane and filled the sill with salt water. Peter and I boarded it from inside. The hardship is not the wind. You can put your head down against wind. It’s the waiting after it. Everything smells wet. The walls sweat. Your clothes never dry properly. You wake thinking you’ve heard someone knock, but it’s only the loose shutter tapping the stone. I’ve been reading your letters over. I think I was lonely before I admitted it. There’s a difference between being alone and feeling nobody would notice if you vanished. At sea, a light going out is noticed. A man, maybe not. But you noticed. I’ve applied for transfer to the mainland station at Ardnamurchan when my term ends. It’s still a lighthouse, but there’s a road nearby and a village three miles away. Peter says that is cheating. Peter has never met you, so his opinion is limited. Love, Jamie --- **24 December 1978** **Glasgow** Dear Jamie, Your transfer came through. Ardnamurchan, from February. I knew it would. Your father is mending the shed roof himself, badly, and says he does not require assistance from “a man who lives in a candle.” He has kept your old room ready anyway. I am proud of you. Not because you stayed. Not because you are going somewhere easier. Because you learned the difference between enduring something and choosing your life. Come home tomorrow if the boat runs. I’ve made steak pie, and there will be milk in the tea whether you appreciate it or not. All my love, Mam
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This epistolary narrative successfully fulfills most of the prompt requirements, creating a compelling story through correspondence between Jamie, a Scottish lighthouse keeper, and his mother. The piece spans nearly a year (March 1978 to December 1978) and effectively uses the letter format to reveal character and advance plot. The Scottish working-class voices are well-differentiated and authentic. Jamie's letters show a young man's perspective - more detailed about technical aspects, gradually revealing his emotional state through concrete observations. His mother's voice is distinctly maternal, practical, and emotionally intelligent, with phrases like "Being frightened does not make you soft" that feel genuinely Scottish and working-class without resorting to heavy dialect. The gradual revelation of Jamie's isolation works effectively. The story moves from initial optimism through growing loneliness, a crisis point with the generator failure, and finally to a mature resolution where Jamie chooses a transfer rather than simply enduring. This character arc feels earned rather than imposed. The technical details of lighthouse keeping are convincing and serve the emotional story well. The generator failure sequence creates genuine tension, and the author uses specific details (forty-seven minutes of darkness, fuel line repairs) to ground the drama in reality. The mother's character is particularly well-drawn. Her decision to contact the superintendent shows agency, and her final letter demonstrates emotional wisdom. The line "You learned the difference between enduring something and choosing your life" provides thematic resonance without being heavy-handed. However, there are some weaknesses. The resolution feels slightly rushed - Jamie's decision to transfer and his emotional growth could have been developed more gradually. Some descriptions occasionally veer toward the literary ("fog comes off the sea like something with a purpose") though they generally remain grounded. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - neither falsely optimistic nor unnecessarily dark. Jamie's growth feels realistic for a young man learning about himself through hardship. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 16.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16.0, Elegant Prose: 15.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 15.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 2.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5.0, Amateurish: 4.0, Purple Prose: 3.0, Overwrought: 3.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 4.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 16.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 17.0, Overall Impression: 16.0
+ First-person unique perspective: Hacker Mindset — Score: 89.1
Prompt:
Hacker Mindset Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of a reformed (now) white hat hacker who cannot help but notice exploitable weaknesses in both systems and people they encounter. They habitually look for vulnerabilities and exploit vectors. The narrative takes place during a first date at a restaurant, where they're trying to focus on human connection. It's going surprisingly well due to a discovered shared nerd-fandom (magic the gathering). Things go a little sideways when the date reveals a noticeably insecure phone unlock pattern, which protagonist can't help but comment on. They are then left to negotiate the fallout of this faux pas and attempt to salvage (or perhaps sabotage) the remainder of the date. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I used to think “reformed” meant the impulse went away. It doesn’t. It means I don’t act on it. Mostly. The restaurant has one of those QR-code menus printed on thick cardstock, which is already a small betrayal of hospitality and a medium-sized opportunity for someone less reformed than I am. The code is a sticker, not incorporated into the design. It has a slight lift at the bottom-left corner. Anybody could replace it. A phishing page could harvest names, emails, payment credentials if the ordering flow is aggressive enough. I do not peel it up. I do not scan it with a sandboxed phone to inspect the redirect chain. I sit across from Mara and make eye contact. Mara is explaining why she arrived seven minutes late, and I am making an effort to listen to the meaning rather than audit the explanation for contradictions. “—then the train stalled between stations,” she says, pushing a curl behind her ear. “And I know that sounds like an excuse, but I took a picture of the announcement board if you need evidence.” “I believe you,” I say. She smiles, relieved in a way that suggests someone before me has demanded evidence. Or perhaps she has simply dated enough men to expect cross-examination. I file that thought away under *do not become that man.* The waiter brings water. The glass has a faint lipstick mark at the rim. Not Mara’s; she has not touched it. The waiter notices when I politely ask for another, and his apology is sincere but practiced. His manager probably tracks complaints in a point-of-sale system with a shared password taped beneath the register. I don’t look. “Tell me something you like that isn’t on your profile,” Mara says. This is the point where a normal person might say hiking or old movies or the smell of rain. I could say I like repairing things people have given up on. That’s true, technically. It’s also close enough to my former career that it invites questions I am not ready to answer before appetizers. “I like people who have strong opinions about fictional artifacts,” I say. Her eyes brighten. “Like what?” “Like whether the One Ring is really sentient, or whether lightsabers make any military sense.” “Oh, God,” she says. “Yes. Or whether the Elder Wand’s ownership rules are internally consistent.” That lands well. Better than well. There is a moment where we both recognize the possibility of a shared language. “Magic?” I ask. “Magic,” she says immediately. “The Gathering. Not stage magic. Though I do enjoy stage magic. My ex used to call my cards cardboard rectangles, so obviously he was wrong about a lot of things.” I almost say, *Your ex had insufficient threat modeling.* Instead I laugh. “What do you play?” “Commander, mostly,” she says. “I started with a Muldrotha deck, which, yes, I know, tells you something horrible about me.” *Muldrotha, the Gravetide.* Permanents from graveyard. Value recursion. Resource recovery. A willingness to make every discarded thing into a future plan. She prefers resilient engines, long games, inevitability. She likes systems that appear damaged but retain hidden redundancy. Interesting. “Nothing horrible,” I say. “It means you believe consequences are negotiable.” “That is an extremely flattering way to describe my trash pile.” “Muldrotha players don’t have trash piles. They have alternate storage.” She points at me as though I have passed some kind of test. “Exactly.” The waiter returns with menus. This time the water glass is clean. I order pasta; Mara gets the mushroom risotto. She asks what I play. “Blue-black,” I say. She narrows her eyes. “Control?” “Historically.” “That’s a red flag.” “It’s transparent. That’s practically a green flag.” “It’s a blue flag,” she says. “Which is worse, because you probably have a counterspell for my objections.” I do, in a sense. I have a lifetime of them. Technical vocabulary, deflection, charm acquired from surviving rooms full of people smarter and less emotionally stable than me. But I don’t deploy any of it. Not immediately. “My main deck was Lazav,” I say. “Then I tried to become a better person and built a Boros equipment deck.” “Did it work?” “No. The deck was terrible.” She laughs hard enough that she covers her mouth. It is a good laugh—not polite, not calibrated. I feel something unfamiliar and inconvenient loosen in my chest. She tells me about her current commander: *Alela, Artful Provocateur.* Artifacts, enchantments, flying faeries, incremental pressure. She likes pretty things that make the board state quietly terrifying. *Smothering Tithe.* *Rhystic Study.* *Bitterblossom.* *Esper Sentinel.* Taxation. Information asymmetry. Passive surveillance. Compounding advantage. I should be alarmed by how compatible this sounds. Instead I say, “So your strategy is to make people underestimate the danger until they realize they can’t pay for anything anymore.” “Only if they draw cards,” she says. “Monstrous.” “Thank you.” The conversation moves easily after that. Too easily. We talk about the first game stores we loved, about badly balanced video games, about fantasy novels that mistake maps for worldbuilding. Mara has read the same obscure sci-fi series I read in college, and she has the correct opinion about the third book’s ending. She describes an old Commander game in which she won with *Revel in Riches* after convincing everyone else that the real threat was a player with *Niv-Mizzet, Parun.* *Revel in Riches.* Treasure accumulation. Victory through unattended infrastructure. *Niv-Mizzet, Parun.* Obvious combo threat. Loud, visible danger. She weaponizes attention. She understands that people will defend against what announces itself, while missing the quiet mechanism converting every interaction into advantage. It should make me nervous. It makes me like her more. By the time our entrées arrive, I have mostly stopped cataloging weaknesses. The restaurant’s Wi-Fi password is printed on a chalkboard near the bar, the network name is generic, and the router is probably still using default admin credentials. The emergency exit is blocked halfway by a decorative planter. The couple at the next table has a phone face-up between them, buzzing every ninety seconds, which is a vulnerability of a different kind. I let these things pass. Mara is telling me about a disastrous prerelease where she opened a foil *Atraxa, Praetors’ Voice* and immediately became the target of every trade shark in the store. She says it without bitterness. There’s a particular steadiness in her voice: she learned, adapted, kept playing. Then her phone lights up. She glances down. “Sorry. My sister.” “No problem.” She picks it up, and in the small motion of waking the screen, I see it. Four dots. A clean, diagonal sweep from upper left to lower right. A one-stroke pattern. My brain does not merely notice it. My brain reconstructs it in high definition. I see the oil traces on the glass. I see the angle of her hand. I see the triviality of a pattern that can be guessed in seconds by anyone who has watched her unlock it once. I see all the possible contents behind it: banking apps, messages, location history, photos, work email, the small private architecture of a person’s life. And because I am reformed, because I am trying to become the kind of person who can sit across from someone without turning them into an attack surface, I should say nothing. I should say absolutely nothing. “Oh,” I say. Mara looks up. “Oh?” “That pattern is—” Stop. “—not very secure.” The silence is immediate. Not dramatic. No dropped fork, no thunderclap. Just a tiny, nearly imperceptible collapse in the room between us. Mara lowers her phone. “Did you just critique my passcode?” “It’s a pattern,” I say, which somehow makes it worse. “Okay.” “I only mean it’s visually obvious. The diagonal is common, and—” “Did you watch me enter it?” “No. I mean, yes, just now, accidentally. I saw the movement.” Her expression changes. Not into anger exactly. Into caution. A door shutting with a soft click. “You saw it once.” “I’ve had training,” I say, then realize I have selected perhaps the worst possible phrase. “Not, like, criminal training. Formerly criminal-adjacent training. Which is also bad. I’m making this worse.” “You’re definitely making it worse.” The waiter arrives with the timing of a man who has no idea he is approaching an active minefield. “Everything tasting all right?” “Yes,” Mara says. “Perfect,” I say, at the same time. The waiter retreats. My instinct is to repair. Repair means identify the breach, isolate the cause, establish trust through transparency. But humans are not networks. You cannot provide a clean incident report and expect the affected party to feel safer. Still, I try. “I’m sorry,” I say. “That was intrusive. Not because I wanted access to your phone. I didn’t. I don’t. I just notice things, and sometimes I mistake noticing a risk for having permission to comment on it.” Mara looks at me for a long moment. “That sounds exhausting,” she says. “It is.” “For you?” There it is. The correct question. A counterspell, cleanly cast. “For everyone around me,” I say. Her face softens, though not all the way. “So what, you walk around thinking everyone is vulnerable?” The honest answer is yes. I notice weak locks, reused passwords, public arguments, lonely people oversharing to strangers, managers who mistake fear for authority. I see the fragile places in systems because I trained myself to, then made a career out of helping people close them. But vulnerability is not the same as invitation. That lesson cost me more than I like to admit. “I walk around noticing vulnerabilities,” I say carefully. “I’m trying to get better at noticing people.” Mara traces the edge of her napkin with one finger. “I don’t need you to audit me,” she says. “I know.” “Do you?” “Yes.” Then, because yes is cheap: “I didn’t, five minutes ago. I do now.” That earns the smallest hint of a smile. “You’re very intense for someone with a terrible Boros deck.” “It was a budget build.” “Sure.” “I deserve that.” We sit with it. The restaurant hum returns around us: glasses clinking, a burst of laughter from the bar, the kitchen door swinging open and shut. I resist the urge to fill the silence with explanations of pattern entropy, shoulder-surfing, biometric fallback weaknesses. Every fact I could offer would be another way of refusing the actual problem. Mara takes a sip of water. “You know,” she says, “I run *Propaganda* in Alela.” I blink. “That tracks,” I say. “It means if you’re going to attack me, it should cost you.” “I’m not attacking you.” “I know.” She raises an eyebrow. “But maybe you should pay two mana before offering unsolicited security advice.” I laugh, gently this time. “That is fair.” “Also, I’m changing my pattern.” “That’s probably wise.” She gives me a look. “I mean—only if you want to. See? Two mana. I’m learning.” The door opens a fraction. We spend the rest of dinner negotiating that opening. Not with declarations, not with some grand conversion into a normal man. I still notice the waiter’s access badge has an unshielded RFID card. I still clock the exposed tablet at the host stand. I still wonder whether Mara’s sister is the person who sends the kind of late-night message that makes someone’s entire posture change. But when Mara mentions *Teferi’s Protection,* I don’t translate it into defensive timing and temporary invulnerability. Not immediately. “Favorite card?” I ask. She thinks. “Maybe *Swords to Plowshares.* Efficient, elegant, slightly insulting.” “That one tells me something about you.” “Careful,” she says. I hold up both hands. “It tells me you have excellent taste.” “Acceptable answer.” And later, when she unlocks her phone again to show me a photo of a disastrously painted miniature, she angles the screen away out of habit. I look at the miniature. It is a tiny dragon with one gold wing, one silver wing, and an expression of profound existential distress. “That,” I say, “is incredible.” “It’s terrible.” “It has character.” She smiles. “You’re learning.” Maybe I am.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional craft in executing a complex narrative premise. The author successfully maintains the protagonist's distinctive voice throughout - a reformed hacker whose analytical mindset creates both compelling insights and social friction. The internal monologue feels authentic and consistent, balancing technical knowledge with genuine emotional vulnerability. The Magic: The Gathering conceit works brilliantly as both a bonding mechanism and a metaphor for how the protagonist reads people and situations. The author shows deep understanding of the game, using specific card references that illuminate character psychology rather than merely displaying knowledge. Mara's deck choices genuinely reflect her personality in ways that feel organic rather than forced. The central conflict - the protagonist's inability to suppress their security-focused observations - creates genuine tension. The phone pattern incident feels inevitable given the character setup, and the fallout is handled with nuance. Neither character becomes a villain; both have understandable positions. The prose is clean and purposeful, with strong sensory details that ground the restaurant setting. The author avoids overwrought descriptions while still creating vivid scenes. The technical elements are woven naturally into the narrative voice rather than feeling like exposition dumps. The emotional arc is well-calibrated. The protagonist's growth feels earned through genuine self-reflection rather than sudden transformation. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - cautiously optimistic without being unrealistic about the character's fundamental nature. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the metaphorical connections between Magic cards and personality traits feel slightly heavy-handed, and a few instances where the internal monologue becomes slightly repetitive in its patterns of observation and self-correction. The dialogue, while limited, feels natural and serves character development effectively. The piece maintains strong pacing throughout its substantial length without significant meandering. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18

+ First-person unique perspective: Overthinking It — Score: 88.2
Prompt:
Overthinking It Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of an intelligent 8th-grade girl with social anxiety who has developed a crush on a classmate. The narrative takes place during a group science project where they're paired together, as she cycles through rehearsed conversations while second-guessing each potential approach. Her inner thoughts should reveal the gap between her articulate internal voice and her difficulty expressing herself aloud, her fears of coming across as desperate or weird, and the exhausting process of monitoring her every word, expression, and movement for potential embarrassment. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I have said “Hey” to him in my head forty-three times. Not out loud. Obviously. Out loud, I am holding a glue stick with both hands like it is a microphone and I am about to announce the winners of the Nobel Prize, except I cannot speak because my throat has apparently decided this is a life-or-death emergency. “Okay,” Mr. Velez says from the front of the room, “you have forty minutes to finish your ecosystem models and write your conclusions.” Forty minutes. Forty minutes to be normal near Ethan. I look down at my lab notes because looking at him would be too much information at once. On the corner of the page, I have drawn tiny versions of everyone in our group. Maya is a small sun with sunglasses because she is loud in a cheerful way and always has good ideas. Tyler is a rectangle with stick arms because he is shaped like a locker and talks like one too: mostly echoes of things other people said. Ethan is a little planet with rings, even though Saturn is not his favorite planet. I don’t know his favorite planet. I just know he wears gray hoodies constantly, has one dimple when he smiles, and once corrected Mr. Velez about whether Pluto counts as a dwarf planet without sounding like a know-it-all. I am a scribble in the margin. That seems accurate. “So,” Maya says, pushing the shoebox ecosystem toward the middle of the table, “we still need the food web arrows.” “I can do the arrows,” I say. Actually, what comes out is, “I—arrows.” Maya nods as if that is a complete sentence. Maybe it is. Maybe if you say things confidently enough, people assume you meant to say them. Ethan looks over. “Do you want the green marker?” This is it. A conversation. A normal, ordinary conversation has appeared directly in front of me, and all I have to do is step into it. Option one: “Yeah, thanks.” Simple. Casual. Unforgettable in the best way, meaning he will not remember it at all. Option two: “Yes, green makes sense for plants.” Shows I am scientifically engaged. But maybe that sounds like I think he doesn’t know green is for plants. Maybe he’ll think I’m correcting him. Maybe he’ll think I’m one of those people who says obvious things because she wants to prove she’s smart. Option three: “I like green.” No. Why would I say that? Then he might ask what my favorite color is, and I would have to explain that it used to be purple but now I feel like everyone says purple, and honestly color preferences are weird because colors don’t have personalities except they kind of do. He is still holding the marker out. “Mm-hm,” I say, which is not a word but at least it means yes in some languages, probably. He gives me the marker. His fingers do not touch mine, which is good because then I would have to pretend nothing happened while every cell in my body filed a report about it. I draw an arrow from the grass to the rabbit. I am very good at drawing arrows. Arrows do not ask follow-up questions. Across from me, Ethan is cutting out a paper hawk. He has a tiny crease between his eyebrows when he concentrates. I have noticed this before, which is probably creepy. Do people notice things like that? Maybe people with crushes do. Maybe everyone does and just doesn’t admit it because then we’d all have to acknowledge that our brains are constantly recording useless details about other people. He says, “Do hawks eat snakes?” I know the answer. I read about this last night because I was researching the project, not because Ethan was in my group. Those are separate things. Completely separate. “Yes,” I could say. “Some hawks do.” But if I say that too quickly, I sound like I have been waiting for someone to ask me a hawk question my entire life. I could say, “I think so,” even though I know so. But pretending not to know things is also bad, because then I am lying, and also I hate when girls act dumb around boys. Not that I’m judging them. Maybe they are anxious too. Maybe everyone is just trying to avoid being called annoying. “Some kinds,” I say finally. My voice comes out quieter than I planned. It is like my thoughts are in a stadium with speakers and my actual voice is trapped under a blanket in the back row. Ethan nods. “Cool.” Cool. He said cool. That could mean he thinks I’m smart. It could mean he thinks I’m weirdly specific. It could mean he was just acknowledging the existence of sound. Do not analyze “cool.” Do not build a whole emotional civilization around one syllable. I add a tiny hawk doodle next to his planet. It has a speech bubble that says cool, which is embarrassing even though nobody can see it because I cover it with my hand. Maya is writing the conclusion in bubble letters. Tyler is leaning back in his chair so far that I can hear Mr. Velez silently preparing a lecture from across the room. “We should make the rabbit pop out,” Maya says. “Like, three-dimensional.” Tyler says, “It already is three-dimensional.” Maya stares at him. I almost laugh. Almost. The laugh gets stuck halfway and turns into a strange nose sound. Ethan looks at me, and I immediately pretend to cough. Smooth. Extremely smooth. I am the kind of person who definitely coughs after someone says “three-dimensional.” He smiles, though. Not a huge smile. Just the dimple. The dimple is there for maybe two seconds, and I have to look down before I start smiling too obviously. Why is smiling dangerous? It should be the easiest thing. You are happy, your face shows it. But there are different smiles. A friendly smile. A polite smile. A smile that says you understand a joke. A smile that says you might be in love with someone because he knows what hawks eat. I choose no smile. No smile cannot be misinterpreted. Except no smile can look rude. I add a tiny doodle of myself with a straight-line mouth. Then I cross it out. “I think the rabbit should pop out,” I say, because apparently I have decided to participate in society. Maya beams. “Thank you!” Tyler shrugs. “Fine.” Ethan says, “Yeah, that’d look good.” There it is again: yeah. That’d look good. He agrees with me. This means nothing. This means everything. This means we are compatible in our shared opinions about cardboard rabbits. I press the green marker too hard and make a blob of ink beside the grass. Great. Now the ecosystem has a radioactive bush. I try to turn it into a clump of moss, but it looks like a green explosion. “Want me to fix that?” Ethan asks. No. Yes. Maybe. If he fixes it, he will think I messed up. But he already saw me mess up, so pretending otherwise would be pointless. If I say yes, I might seem helpless. If I say no, I might seem defensive. If I say, “It’s fine,” when it is obviously not fine, then I become a person who lies about moss. “It’s supposed to be moss,” I say. He leans closer to look. “It kind of looks like moss.” He is being nice. He has to be. Nobody could honestly think this looks like moss. Still, I feel warm in a way that is both pleasant and terrible. “Thanks,” I say. That one comes out almost normal. I wait for myself to ruin it with another sentence. Something like, “I draw a lot,” or “I’m not usually this bad at markers,” or “Did you know moss doesn’t technically have roots?” I know that fact. I have facts. I have so many facts, stacked neatly in my brain, ready to be useful. But when I am around Ethan, every fact transforms into a potential embarrassment. What if I told him about moss and he thought I was trying too hard? What if I didn’t tell him anything and he thought I was boring? What if he already thinks I’m boring? What if he thinks I’m quiet in a mysterious way? No. Nobody has ever thought that. Quiet girls are only mysterious in books. In real life, people ask if you’re okay five times a day. “Are you okay?” Ethan asks. Of course. “Yep,” I say too fast. He pauses. “Okay.” Now he thinks I’m not okay. I am not okay. I am sitting three feet away from a boy I like, trying to calculate the correct amount of eye contact like it is a chemistry equation. Maybe I should say something funny. Something easy. “I’m just thinking about moss.” That could be cute. Or it could be the sentence he remembers years from now when someone asks him about the weirdest girl in eighth grade. So I do nothing. I draw another tiny planet in my notebook. This one has no rings. This one is just a circle with a face, looking toward the scribble version of me. Then Maya says, “Guys, we have five minutes,” and everybody starts moving at once. Ethan tapes the hawk above the snake. I finish the arrows. Tyler finally glues the rabbit on, and Maya writes our names at the top of the poster. When she gets to mine, she asks, “Do you want to write it?” I nod and take the marker. My hand shakes a little as I write my name beside Ethan’s. The letters are too close together. The E in Ethan is almost touching the first letter of my name. Nobody notices. Probably. I tell myself that probably is enough.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional adherence to the prompt's requirements, delivering a compelling first-person narrative that captures the authentic voice of an anxious 8th-grade girl with remarkable precision. The author successfully constructs the entire piece around internal monologue, creating a vivid portrait of social anxiety through the protagonist's overthinking patterns. The characterization is particularly strong. The narrator feels genuinely like a 13-year-old - intelligent but inexperienced, articulate in thought but stumbling in speech. Her anxiety manifests in believable ways: the obsessive counting ("forty-three times"), the elaborate decision trees for simple responses, and the constant self-monitoring. The gap between her rich internal voice and her halting external communication is expertly rendered through moments like "I—arrows" and her analysis of saying "cool." The prose style effectively mirrors the character's mental state. Short, fragmented thoughts interrupt longer analytical passages, creating a rhythm that feels authentically anxious. Metaphors like comparing her voice to "thoughts in a stadium with speakers" while her actual voice is "trapped under a blanket" are both age-appropriate and emotionally resonant. The recurring doodles serve as a clever device to show her coping mechanisms and self-perception. The dialogue, though minimal, serves its purpose well. The sparse exchanges highlight the protagonist's difficulty with verbal communication while showing how normal teenage interactions can feel monumentally significant to an anxious mind. Ethan's simple responses ("Cool," "Yeah, that'd look good") become loaded with meaning through the narrator's interpretation. The piece maintains excellent coherence throughout, following a clear arc from initial paralysis through small moments of interaction to a quietly meaningful conclusion. The ending, where she writes her name close to Ethan's, provides subtle resolution without false transformation - she remains anxious but achieves small victories. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the introspection becomes slightly repetitive, and a few metaphors that feel slightly forced. However, these don't significantly detract from the overall effectiveness. The piece avoids common pitfalls like purple prose or unearned character growth, instead offering an authentic slice of adolescent experience. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18
