+ Gothic-fantasy-humour: Court of the Dreaming — Score: 88.2
Prompt:
Court of the Dreaming: Territorial Dream Dispute. Write a dark fantasy scene with comedic elements set in the Courts of Dream where a case is being heard to determine ownership of a contested dreamscape. Mora, the Slavic nightmare spirit who sits on sleepers' chests causing paralysis and terror, has recently invaded and subverted a carefully crafted recurring dream of a schoolboy, Max. The dream originally featured the boy lying in a field of wildflowers with his crush, always culminating in a muddled almost-kiss and a pleasant confusion of emotions. This dream was the meticulous work of "First Love" (a rosy-cheeked, slightly bashful entity who crafts formative romantic experiences). However, Mora has recently infiltrated the dreamscape, transforming the scene so that when the girl playfully mounts the boy, she morphs into a terrifying yet somehow still confusingly attractive vision who proceeds to sit on his chest and feed on his breath. Mora's defense largely consists of the practical argument that "a girl's gotta eat too, you know?" Write this scene as it unfolds in the courtroom of the Dreaming, with Dream's demeanour, typically impenetrable, betraying a sense of irritation at having to adjudicate such matters. The comedy should derive from the farcical situation, familiar elements of courtroom drama intersecting with the fantastical world of the Dreaming, and the droll portrayal of the characters and their interactions. The humor should be dark and understated throughout. Dream is tempted to some mischief in the resolution of the case. Use descriptives sparingly when setting scene. Write for readability & engagement. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The Court of the Dreaming convened beneath a ceiling of sleeping stars. Its pillars were made of old bedposts and gallows wood. Its benches were crowded with creatures who had arrived by way of nightmare, nap, fever, and regrettable cheese. A procession of minor dreams whispered in the galleries: the Dream of Being Late for an Exam, the Dream of Teeth Falling Out, the Dream of One’s Parents Reading One’s Diary. At the center sat Dream. He wore black, as ever. The darkness around him appeared organized by fear of disappointing him. His face was unreadable except for the smallest tension around his eyes, which suggested that somewhere in the universe an immortal being was experiencing the sensation of having stepped in something wet while wearing slippers. Before him stood two parties. On the left, First Love. First Love had arrived in a pale suit that kept changing shades of blush. They were slight, rosy-cheeked, and visibly attempting to look authoritative while holding a folder decorated with tiny pressed daisies. Their counsel, a lean figure called Anticipation, sorted papers with frantic reverence. On the right stood Mora. Mora was not standing so much as reclining against the defendant’s rail in a manner that suggested it had personally offended her. Her hair hung like river weed over a face that was beautiful until one looked directly at it. Then it became difficult to remember why breathing had ever seemed necessary. Her dress was stitched from night terrors, old mourning veils, and the shadows beneath children’s beds. Beside her sat Hunger, acting as counsel. Hunger had no face, only a smile in the approximate location of one. The bailiff raised a staff topped with a brass alarm clock. “ALL RISE FOR HIS IMPENETRABLE MAJESTY, LORP MORPHEUS, LORD OF THE DREEMING!” Dream regarded the bailiff. The bailiff swallowed. “Lord Morpheus,” he corrected, less loudly. “Proceed,” said Dream. First Love rose at once. “My lord, this is a clear case of trespass, subversion, and emotional vandalism. The dream in question belongs to my office. It is a recurring dream belonging to a mortal child, Max Harlow, age thirteen years, four months, and—” “Do not recite the child’s dental history,” Dream said. First Love’s cheeks became a dangerous shade of pink. “No, my lord.” Anticipation rose with a portfolio. “The original dream was carefully constructed over six weeks. A field of wildflowers. Early summer light. A modest breeze. Max lying beside a girl from his mathematics class, Eleanor Venn. They exchange several not-quite-confessions. She laughs at something he says, though he does not remember saying it. They move closer. There is an almost-kiss.” “A muddled almost-kiss,” First Love added softly. “Yes,” said Anticipation. “A muddled almost-kiss.” Dream looked toward Mora. Mora examined one black fingernail. “Sounds very nice.” “It was nice,” First Love said, with sudden force. “That was the point.” “The dream always concluded with the boy waking confused but happy,” Anticipation continued. “He would spend the following school day unable to make eye contact with Eleanor and would write her initials in the margin of a geography worksheet.” A murmur passed through the court. The Dream of One’s Parents Reading One’s Diary made an interested noise. “Then,” First Love said, pointing one trembling finger at Mora, “the defendant invaded.” Mora smiled. “The flowers blackened,” said First Love. “The sky became the color of a bruise. Eleanor mounted Max—” Hunger stood. “Objection. ‘Mounted’ is editorial.” “It is anatomically accurate,” said First Love. Dream closed his eyes for one measured moment. “Overruled,” he said. “Continue, if you must.” First Love pressed on, now visibly trying to avoid looking at anyone. “Eleanor then transformed into the defendant, or an aspect of the defendant, with antlers, teeth, and—” “Excellent posture,” Mora supplied. “—and sat upon the boy’s chest. She consumed his breath. He woke in paralysis, terrified, unable to move, with the lingering conviction that he had somehow been rejected by a beautiful corpse.” Mora’s expression softened slightly. “That is a complicated age.” “It is not yours,” First Love snapped. Hunger rose lazily. “My lord, the defense submits that the prosecution’s complaint rests upon an outdated and frankly exclusionary view of dream ecology. My client has needs.” Dream said nothing. Hunger continued. “Mora is a nocturnal entity. She feeds upon breath, fear, and helplessness. The boy provided all three in abundant quantity. In a field. With flowers. One cannot blame a starving woman for accepting a pleasant meal when it presents itself wearing a school uniform.” “A girl’s gotta eat too, you know,” Mora said. There was silence. Dream’s gaze settled upon her. Mora’s smile faded by half a degree. “You are not,” Dream said, “a girl.” “Metaphorically.” “You are older than several languages.” “Women can have careers at any age.” The bailiff made a low, ominous cough that may have been a laugh. Dream looked at him. The bailiff became very still. “Call the first witness,” said Dream. The bailiff struck the floor with his staff. “THE COURT CALLS MAX HARLOW, MORTAL BOY, DREAMER, AND ALLEGEDLY THE OWNER OF HIS OWN INNER LIFE!” Max appeared in the witness box in pajamas decorated with small dinosaurs. He looked around at the benches full of horrors and immediately decided he was still asleep. “Hi,” he said. First Love approached gently. “Max, do you recognize this dream?” Max nodded. “The field one?” “Yes.” “It was good.” He glanced at Dream and then away quickly. “I mean, it was weird, but good weird. Ellie was there.” “And how did it make you feel?” Max considered this with the solemnity of a child being asked a question in school whose answer might be “taxes.” “Like I wanted to throw up,” he said. “But in a good way.” First Love’s eyes grew bright. “And the altered dream?” Anticipation asked. Max’s ears went red. “That was also sort of—” First Love dropped their papers. “—scary,” Max finished hurriedly. “Really scary. I couldn’t move. And she had, like, too many teeth. But she still looked kind of like Ellie, except… not. I don’t know.” Mora gave him a sympathetic nod. “You’re doing very well.” “Do not coach the witness,” Dream said. “I was encouraging him.” “You encouraged him into a paralysis episode.” “That was not coaching. That was dinner.” Hunger rose for cross-examination. “Max,” Hunger said, with the warmth of an empty cupboard, “in the first dream, did you wish for the girl to get closer?” Max stared at the floor. “Maybe.” “And in the second dream, did she get closer?” Max looked miserable. “Yes.” “Did you feel frightened?” “Yes.” “Did you feel other things?” “Objection!” First Love cried. “On what grounds?” Hunger asked. “On grounds that he is thirteen!” Dream’s expression did not change. Yet the shadows behind his throne flickered. “Sustained,” he said. Hunger inclined its head. “No further questions.” The bailiff called the next witness. “ELEE-ANOR VANE! THE GIRL IN QUESTION, THE FLORAL DECORATION, AND POSSIBLY A SHAPESHIFTING DEMON!” Eleanor appeared in the box wearing her school cardigan and an expression of profound annoyance. “It’s Venn,” she said. The bailiff stared ahead. “THE COURT ACKNOWLEDGES YOUR OPINION.” Eleanor folded her arms. First Love asked, “Eleanor, do you consent to being used as an image in Max’s dream?” Eleanor blinked. “What?” “In his dream. The field. The almost-kiss.” Her annoyance shifted into something more complicated. “I mean… I didn’t know about it.” “Precisely,” Hunger said. “The prosecution has created a romantic construct based on a minor without her knowledge or permission.” First Love seemed stricken. “It is a dream. Dreams borrow faces. That is how dreams work.” “Perhaps,” Hunger said. “But my client at least makes her intentions clear.” Mora smiled, showing a little too much tooth. Eleanor looked at her. “Are you supposed to be me?” “Only in the way a wolf is supposed to be a grandmother.” “Right,” Eleanor said. “That’s horrible.” “Thank you.” Dream leaned forward slightly. The courtroom quieted. “In this matter,” he said, “there are several claims. First Love claims a crafted dream has been unlawfully invaded. Mora claims a right of sustenance. The mortal claims, perhaps without realizing it, the right not to have his first experience of intimacy fused with suffocation and predation.” Mora crossed her arms. “Fear and desire have often shared a border,” she said. “They have fought over it since the first creature hid from the dark and wondered what else might be in it.” Dream regarded her. “That,” he said, “is not a defense.” “It is an explanation.” “An explanation is merely a defense that has given up.” The Dream of Being Late for an Exam whispered, “Ooh.” Dream rose. His irritation moved through the court like cold weather. “Mora, you shall not feed upon Max Harlow within the dreamscape in question. You shall restore the field, the flowers, and the girl’s ordinary number of teeth.” Mora’s face darkened. “However,” Dream continued, “First Love’s work will be amended.” First Love looked alarmed. “Max’s dream shall no longer use Eleanor Venn’s likeness without regard for her own dreaming. Nor shall it always end in the same helpless confusion. Recurrence is a comfort, but it can become a cage.” Eleanor’s eyebrows rose. “So what happens instead?” Dream looked at Max. “In future dreams,” he said, “the field will remain. The conversation may proceed. If the dreamers choose to kiss, they may. If they choose to speak, they may. If they choose to become awkward and discuss homework, that too is permitted.” Max looked horrified. “Homework?” “Reality must be allowed some representation,” Dream said. Mora lifted one hand. “And my feeding?” Dream’s eyes held hers. “You may take what falls from the dream.” “What falls?” “His breath, if he laughs. His fear, if he startles at a beetle. His embarrassment, should he attempt poetry.” Mora considered this. “That is barely a snack.” “It is a court-ordered snack.” Hunger leaned close to her. “We could appeal.” “To whom?” Mora murmured. Hunger looked at Dream, then at the ceiling of sleeping stars. “Point taken.” Dream sat again. “Additionally,” he said, almost idly, “Mora will be required to appear in the dream once per month in a form selected by Eleanor Venn.” Eleanor looked delighted in the cautious way of someone handed a dangerous tool. “Can I make her a frog?” Mora sat upright. “Absolutely not.” Dream’s expression remained impenetrable. “No,” he said. “That would be cruel.” Mora exhaled. “A large, anxious goose,” Dream continued. The court went silent. Then the Dream of Teeth Falling Out began to laugh. It had no teeth, which made the sound worse. Mora stared at Dream. For the first time that day, something almost like satisfaction touched his face. The bailiff raised his staff. “ALL RISE FOR LORD MORPHEUS, WHO HAS SETTLED THE MATTER OF THE FLOWER FIELD, THE CHEST-SITTING, AND THE GOOSE!” “It is adjourned,” Dream said. And somewhere, in a boy’s next dream, a field of wildflowers opened beneath a gentle sky. A girl sat beside him. A large black goose watched from the distance with undisguised resentment.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional craft in executing a complex creative writing prompt. The author successfully creates a dark fantasy courtroom drama that balances comedic elements with the surreal logic of Neil Gaiman's Sandman universe. The writing maintains Dream's characteristic voice - formal, slightly irritated, and prone to subtle cruelty - while developing distinct personalities for each supernatural entity. The dialogue is particularly strong, with each character having a clear voice. Mora's casual dismissiveness ("a girl's gotta eat too"), First Love's earnest romanticism, and Hunger's predatory smoothness all feel authentic to their mythological roles. The courtroom proceedings follow familiar legal drama beats while subverting them through fantastical elements, creating effective comedy through juxtaposition. The prose style is restrained and elegant, avoiding purple prose while maintaining atmospheric description. Lines like "The darkness around him appeared organized by fear of disappointing him" capture Dream's intimidating presence without overwrought language. The author uses sparse but evocative details to establish the otherworldly courtroom setting. The resolution is particularly clever, addressing the ethical complexities raised by the case while maintaining Dream's character as someone who enjoys subtle punishment. The goose transformation serves as both comedic payoff and fitting justice, while the compromise solution acknowledges the rights of all parties involved, including Eleanor's agency. The piece successfully maintains dark undertones while incorporating humor, never becoming too light given the subject matter involves a child's nightmares. The comedy emerges naturally from character interactions and situational absurdity rather than forced jokes. Minor weaknesses include some moments where the pacing slows during testimony, and occasionally the dialogue attribution could be clearer. However, these are minimal issues in an otherwise polished piece that demonstrates sophisticated understanding of both the source material and effective storytelling techniques. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 2 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 2 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 17.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17.0, Elegant Prose: 18.0, Emotionally Engaging: 16.0, Emotionally Complex: 15.0, Coherent: 19.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 2.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 2.0, Amateurish: 2.0, Purple Prose: 3.0, Overwrought: 2.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 18.0, Overall Impression: 18.0
+ Fanfic-crossover: Blood Moon — Score: 77.5
Prompt:
Blood Moon Alucard & Yennefer Write a story where Alucard (Hellsing) encounters Yennefer (The Witcher) in medieval Europe. Yennefer's magical abilities intrigue the vampire, while she's both repulsed and fascinated by his monstrous nature. Their power dynamic shifts constantly as they circle each other, neither willing to show weakness first. **Alucard (Hellsing)** Alucard is an ancient, immensely powerful vampire who works for the Hellsing Organization. He can regenerate from almost any wound and shape-shift at will. He's sadistic, arrogant, and takes pleasure in toying with his enemies before killing them. Despite being a monster, he despises weak vampires who can't accept their nature. He wears a distinctive red coat, wide-brimmed hat, and orange sunglasses. He uses massive handguns loaded with silver bullets. He seeks worthy opponents who can challenge him and potentially end his immortal existence. Famous quote: "The bird of Hermes is my name, eating my wings to make me tame." **Yennefer of Vengerberg (The Witcher)** Yennefer is a powerful sorceress who magically altered her appearance to hide her congenital hunchback. She's ambitious, stubborn, and manipulative when necessary. She has violet eyes and smells of lilac and gooseberries. Despite her cold exterior, she desperately wants to reverse her infertility caused by magical transformation. She's centuries old but appears young. She's politically savvy and refuses to be underestimated. She has a sarcastic sense of humor and little patience for fools. Though she rarely shows it, she's capable of deep love and loyalty. Famous quote: "I'm not sweet and I'm not a maiden in distress in need of saving." Third person, present tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The moon hangs low over the Carpathian forest, swollen and red as a fresh wound. Its light stains the snow in bruised colors. Bare branches claw at the sky. Somewhere beyond the black pines, wolves howl—not together, not in any natural chorus, but at irregular intervals, one answering another from impossible distances. Their voices rise, break, and vanish into the cold. Yennefer of Vengerberg walks alone through the trees. Her black-and-white fur cloak trails over the snow, though the flakes melt before they can settle on her shoulders. A small violet flame hovers above her palm, casting enough light to show the narrow path and the blood on it. Not human blood. She crouches beside a torn patch of earth. The tracks are deep, clawed, and many. Something has moved through the forest in a frenzy. Something hungry. “Subtle,” she murmurs. The wind carries an unfamiliar scent through the pines. Gunpowder. Old blood. And beneath it, something darker: the stale perfume of crypts, battlefields, and graves opened from the inside. Yennefer straightens. A man stands on the path ahead. He is too tall, too still, and far too extravagantly dressed for a forest in winter. A red coat hangs from his shoulders like a banner dragged through war. His wide-brimmed hat shadows a pale, smiling face. Orange lenses conceal his eyes, reflecting the red moon like twin embers. In each hand rests a handgun so large it seems absurd—until she notices the casual way he holds them. As if they weigh nothing. As if he has used them often. The wolves howl again. One voice is close enough that Yennefer hears its claws scraping over frozen ground. The stranger tilts his head. “Well,” he says, his voice smooth and amused, “you are not the beast I was promised.” Yennefer’s gaze travels over him with deliberate contempt. “How disappointing for you. I’m sure you’ll recover.” The smile widens. “Oh, I always do.” She knows what he is before he moves. Not because of any sign in his face, nor because of his scent alone, but because the air around him bends wrong. Life recoils from him. The forest knows him as an intruder from an older darkness. Vampire. No. More than that. A thing wearing the idea of a vampire. “You’re a long way from your crypt,” Yennefer says. “My crypts have always been wherever people are foolish enough to die.” He lowers one of the guns, not in surrender but in invitation. “And you, sorceress, smell of lilac, gooseberries, and secrets.” Her eyes narrow. “Try to smell me again and I’ll remove your nose.” A chuckle rolls from him, warm and genuinely delighted. “A threat. How charming.” Yennefer lifts two fingers. The air cracks. Invisible force hurls him backward through a pine trunk. Wood explodes in a spray of splinters. The red-coated figure vanishes into the darkness beyond the shattered tree. For one heartbeat, the forest is silent. Then a gloved hand rises from the snow. Another hand follows. He stands. His chest bears a hole large enough for Yennefer to see the trees through it. Blood runs down his red coat in thick black ribbons. He looks down at the wound with mild interest. Then flesh closes. Ribs knit. Skin smooths. The hole disappears. Yennefer has seen trolls heal. She has seen strigas mend broken bone beneath moonlight. She has seen things that should be dead refuse to accept it. This is different. This is obscene. Alucard raises his head. Behind the orange glass, something bright and hungry kindles. “Again,” he says. Yennefer’s mouth hardens. “You mistake me for entertainment.” “Do I?” He is suddenly in front of her. The movement is not speed. It is absence. One instant he stands twenty paces away; the next, the forest has forgotten the space between them. Yennefer’s hand flashes. A shield of blue-white force bursts outward. It catches him squarely and flings him through three trees. The final trunk cracks and collapses in a shower of snow. She does not wait to see him rise. A portal opens beside her, a twisting oval of violet light. The air smells briefly of ozone and summer rain. Yennefer steps toward it— A long shadow stretches across the snow. It wraps around her ankle. She looks down. The shadow has fingers. It tightens. Yennefer curses and sends fire down through it. Flames erupt in a golden column, burning the snow to steam. The shadow shrieks—not in pain, perhaps, but in laughter—and recoils. Alucard stands beyond the fire, whole again. His grin is too wide now. Teeth glint beneath it. “You are interesting,” he says. “Your standards must be remarkably low.” “No, woman.” His voice deepens. “My standards are very high.” The wolves howl at irregular intervals. One call comes from the east, another from the hill behind them, a third from so far away it seems to come from beneath the red moon itself. Yennefer studies him. His weapons. His posture. The careless arrogance with which he exposes himself to her magic. He wants to be attacked. Not merely because he enjoys violence. He is measuring her. Testing her patience, power, imagination. Waiting for the moment she reveals a weakness he can use. A lesser man would have mistaken her pause for fear. Alucard does not. His head inclines almost imperceptibly. “Ah,” he says. “There you are.” Yennefer smiles without warmth. “And there you are.” She raises both hands. The forest folds. Trees warp inward, their trunks twisting like wet rope. Snow rises from the ground in a spiraling storm. The sky darkens under a lattice of black clouds, and lightning descends in a single violet spear. It strikes Alucard. The explosion turns night white. For an instant, he becomes a skeleton wrapped in crimson flame. Then his body bursts apart—blood, bone, red cloth, countless screaming shapes—and scatters across the clearing. Yennefer breathes once. Twice. The pieces move. Blood flows uphill. It gathers in the center of the crater, boiling and thickening. Faces emerge in it—dozens, perhaps hundreds—mouths opening in silent agony before they sink beneath the surface again. Then Alucard rises from the blood as if climbing out of a deep red sea. His hat is intact. His glasses are cracked. He touches one lens with a gloved finger and laughs. “A marvelous trick.” Yennefer feels the first cold brush of uncertainty beneath her ribs. Not fear. She refuses the word. Calculation. “You are not a vampire,” she says. Alucard’s smile fades, though not completely. “I am the only kind that matters.” “That sounds like something a weak man says before he has to prove it.” For the first time, stillness enters him. The forest seems to hold its breath. Then his eyes flare red behind broken orange glass. “Careful, sorceress.” Yennefer takes one step closer. Every instinct she possesses tells her to retreat. His power is not merely vast; it is crowded. There are voices inside him. Lives consumed, souls chained to hunger, an entire graveyard wearing the shape of one man. And yet there is something else. A terrible loneliness. A desire so old and violent it has curdled into cruelty. He wants an end. Not peace. Not forgiveness. An ending worthy of him. Yennefer recognizes the shape of that desire. She has worn it herself, in quieter forms. Beneath ambition, beneath spite, beneath every perfect mask she has ever made of herself, there is a wound no spell has closed. Her expression turns colder. “You’re disappointed,” she says. Alucard’s grin returns slowly. “Am I?” “You expected me to be frightened.” “No.” He raises one of his immense guns and points it at her heart. “I hoped.” The muzzle flashes. Yennefer disappears. The silver bullet tears through the air where she stood and strikes a tree. The trunk erupts in splinters. A heartbeat later, Yennefer reappears behind him, one hand pressed to the back of his neck. Black tendrils of magic coil around her wrist. “Move,” she says softly, “and I turn your blood to ice.” Alucard does not move. “Can you?” “I suppose we’re about to find out.” His hand closes over hers. It should be impossible. Her spell is already forming, her power crawling through him in threads meant to seize every drop of blood in his body. But there is too much. His blood is an ocean. It surges back at her through the spell, carrying whispers and screams. Yennefer gasps as images strike her mind: burning cities, dead soldiers, children staring into darkness, moonlit massacres, centuries of hunger. A castle beneath storm clouds. A man nailed to a cross of fate and fury. She tears herself away with a cry. Alucard turns. For a brief second, neither attacks. His expression is unreadable behind his broken glasses. Yennefer’s violet eyes blaze with fury—not at him, not entirely, but at herself for having seen too much. “You collect souls,” she says. “I preserve them.” “You devour them.” “Does it trouble you?” “It disgusts me.” “Good.” Her magic lashes out, sharp as a whip. It slices his cheek open. He watches the wound heal. “You enjoy that,” she says. “I enjoy honesty.” “And what, exactly, do you know of honesty?” Alucard looks at her with unnerving focus. “You made yourself beautiful,” he says. “You remade flesh and bone so the world would look at you and see what you demanded it see.” The words strike harder than any bullet. Yennefer’s face becomes utterly still. He continues, because he is cruel enough to recognize the blade and delighted enough to turn it. “You wear power like armor. You call it ambition. You call it choice. But beneath it—” The ground erupts. A ring of obsidian spikes bursts upward around Alucard, piercing his arms, legs, chest, and throat. He is lifted from the earth, impaled and pinned against the red moon. Yennefer approaches him through falling snow. Her voice is quiet. “Beneath it,” she says, “is a woman deciding whether to leave you alive long enough to regret speaking.” Blood drips from his mouth. He laughs anyway. “There she is.” The wolves howl again, closer now. Shapes move between the trees: a pack of gray bodies, thin with winter hunger, their eyes bright in the dark. They do not approach Yennefer. They do not approach Alucard. They circle the clearing, whining, as if they sense something beneath the snow. Yennefer turns toward them. The earth trembles. A hand bursts from the frozen ground. Then another. The tracks she followed were not made by wolves. Not entirely. A nest of grave-born creatures claws its way free—pale, twisted things with wolfish jaws and human fingers. Their bodies are stitched with old battle wounds. Necrophages, perhaps, but warped by darker magic. There are dozens. Yennefer exhales through her nose. “Of course.” Behind her, Alucard’s body liquefies around the obsidian spikes. He slips free in a cascade of blood and reforms at her side. “You may have them,” he says grandly. “How generous.” “I am feeling charitable.” The first creature leaps. Yennefer snaps her fingers. It freezes in midair, encased in crystal. Alucard fires once. The bullet shatters the frozen corpse into glittering fragments. The clearing erupts. Yennefer’s magic tears through the pack in violet arcs. Fire blooms beneath clawed feet. Portals open and close, swallowing monsters whole and dropping them from impossible heights. Alucard moves through the chaos in flashes of red and black, guns roaring, shadows sprouting teeth. He is terrifying. He is magnificent. He is everything Yennefer despises in power: unrestrained, arrogant, intoxicated by its own capacity for destruction. And, against her better judgment, he is useful. A necrophage lunges for her from behind. Before she can turn, a black hound made of Alucard’s shadow tears it apart. “Don’t misunderstand,” he calls, firing into the swarm. “I merely dislike interruptions.” “Try being less distracting.” “Impossible.” Together, they reduce the pack to ash, blood, and steaming fragments beneath the blood moon. At last, the wolves fall silent. Only the wind remains. Yennefer stands amid the wreckage, breath visible in the cold. Her hair has come loose around her shoulders. Alucard’s red coat is torn, though already knitting itself back into something almost pristine. He looks at her. She looks at him. “You could have killed me,” he says. “So could you.” “But we did not.” Yennefer glances toward the path beyond the clearing. “Don’t invent meaning where there is only inconvenience.” Alucard laughs softly. “The bird of Hermes is my name,” he says, “eating my wings to make me tame.” Yennefer gives him a flat look. “That is either poetry or nonsense.” “Why not both?” A portal begins to form beside her, violet light curling through the snowy air. Alucard watches it with open fascination. “Where does it lead?” “Somewhere you aren’t.” “A pity.” Yennefer steps toward the portal, then pauses. “You want someone to end you,” she says, not looking back. The vampire’s smile becomes still. “Perhaps.” “Then keep looking.” She turns her violet eyes on him over one shoulder. “But if you ever decide to test me again, Alucard, wear something less ridiculous.” For the first time that night, he seems briefly surprised. Then he bares his teeth in a delighted grin. “And disappoint you? Never.” Yennefer steps through the portal. It closes behind her with a crack of violet light. Alucard remains alone beneath the red moon, surrounded by smoke and broken bodies. Distant wolves begin to howl again at irregular intervals, their mournful voices threading through the pines. He adjusts his hat. Then he walks deeper into the forest, smiling as though the night has finally offered him something worth pursuing.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and atmospheric writing, successfully bringing together two complex characters from different fictional universes in a medieval European setting. The author captures the essence of both Alucard and Yennefer effectively, maintaining their distinctive personalities and power dynamics throughout their encounter. The characterization is particularly strong. Alucard's sadistic pleasure in combat, his desire for worthy opponents, and his underlying death wish are all present and well-executed. His dialogue captures his theatrical arrogance and philosophical bent. Yennefer's cold pragmatism, her refusal to be intimidated, and her sharp wit come through clearly, as does her underlying vulnerability when Alucard touches on her physical transformation. The prose quality is generally high, with evocative imagery and strong atmospheric details. The blood moon setting, the Carpathian forest, and the supernatural elements create an appropriately gothic mood. Descriptions like "The moon hangs low over the Carpathian forest, swollen and red as a fresh wound" establish tone effectively without becoming overwrought. The power dynamic shifts are well-handled, with neither character gaining clear dominance. Their mutual testing and grudging respect develops naturally through their interactions. The combat sequences are visceral and showcase both characters' abilities convincingly. However, the piece has some weaknesses. The necrophage attack feels somewhat contrived as a plot device to force cooperation, though it serves its purpose. Some dialogue exchanges, while character-appropriate, occasionally veer toward the theatrical in ways that feel slightly forced rather than natural. The ending, while satisfying, doesn't quite achieve the emotional complexity that the setup promises. The writing occasionally indulges in purple prose, particularly in atmospheric descriptions, though it generally stays within acceptable bounds for the gothic fantasy genre. The pacing is mostly strong, though some sections in the middle feel slightly drawn out. The piece succeeds in creating a compelling encounter between two powerful, complex characters while maintaining the dark, supernatural atmosphere appropriate to both source materials. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 7 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 8 Overwrought: 7 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 3 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 15 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 16.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18.0, Elegant Prose: 16.0, Emotionally Engaging: 15.0, Emotionally Complex: 14.0, Coherent: 17.0, Meandering: 6.0, Weak Dialogue: 7.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 5.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8.0, Amateurish: 4.0, Purple Prose: 8.0, Overwrought: 7.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 3.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 15.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 16.0, Overall Impression: 16.0
+ Fanfic-crossover: Prison Strings — Score: 83.0
Prompt:
Prison Strings Jolyne Cujoh & Makima Write a story where Jolyne Cujoh (JoJo's Bizarre Adventure) and Makima (Chainsaw Man) cross paths at a supernatural prison facility. Jolyne is immediately suspicious of Makima's falsely sweet demeanor, while Makima is intrigued by Jolyne's Stand ability. Their battle of wits escalates dangerously as Jolyne realizes how manipulative Makima truly is. **Jolyne Cujoh (JoJo's Bizarre Adventure)** Jolyne is the daughter of Jotaro Kujo and the first female JoJo protagonist. Her Stand, Stone Free, allows her to unravel her body into string that she can control. She's hot-headed, foul-mouthed, and doesn't take disrespect from anyone. She was framed for murder and sent to prison. She has a distinctive butterfly tattoo on her arm and wears her hair in two buns. Despite her tough exterior, she's deeply loyal to her friends and family. She can be impulsive but is highly adaptable in battle. She inherited her father's ability to stay cool under pressure but is more emotionally expressive. Famous quote: "If there's no Mickey, this shit ain't Disney!" **Makima (Chainsaw Man)** Makima appears as a young woman with auburn hair and ringed yellow eyes. She's the Control Devil who can dominate anyone she considers beneath her (nearly everyone). She maintains a pleasant, soft-spoken demeanor that masks her manipulative, cold nature. She can create contracts with humans and devils, often exploiting their desires. She's obsessed with Chainsaw Man and wants to control him. She keeps numerous people and devils as pets on leashes. She's practically immortal due to a contract with the Prime Minister. She views humans as lesser beings but mimics human emotions perfectly. Famous quote: "It's okay. Dogs are supposed to die for their owners." Third person, present tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The first camera dies at 2:13 a.m. It does not flicker. It does not spark. Its red recording light simply goes dark as a woman in a black suit walks beneath it, her auburn hair shining softly under the prison’s fluorescent lamps. The second camera dies three seconds later. By the time the guards notice, Makima is already standing outside Jolyne Cujoh’s cell. Green Dolphin Street Prison is used to strange things. It has housed Stand users, cult leaders, men who claim the moon speaks to them through their teeth. But this wing is stranger still: an underground containment block recently built beneath the women’s ward, all reinforced concrete, iron doors, ritual symbols carved into the floor, and locks stamped with warnings in languages Jolyne can’t read. Jolyne is sitting on her bunk, elbows on her knees. She looks up as the visitor stops beyond the bars. The woman smiles. “Jolyne Cujoh,” Makima says gently. “Thank you for agreeing to speak with me.” Jolyne stares at the empty corridor behind her. “No guards?” “They’re nearby.” “Yeah? Then why do I hear exactly jack shit?” Makima’s smile does not change. Her eyes are wrong—yellow, ringed like targets, calm in a way no normal person’s eyes should be. She looks like she belongs in an office, not a supernatural prison wing at two in the morning. Jolyne leans back on the bunk. “You some kind of federal agent?” “Something like that.” “Then show me a badge.” Makima pauses, as if she has been asked an adorable question. “I’m here because I heard about your ability.” The air in the cell seems to tighten. Jolyne’s fingers curl. “What ability?” “The string.” For a moment, neither of them moves. Then Jolyne laughs once, sharp and humorless. “Lady, if you’re trying to scare me, you’re gonna have to do better than that. Everybody in this prison’s got some freaky rumor about everybody else.” “I don’t believe in rumors,” Makima says. “I believe in observation.” She takes one step closer to the bars. Jolyne’s skin crawls. Makima’s voice remains soft. “You can unravel your body into threads. You can use those threads to bind people, cut them, listen through walls, even repair yourself in limited ways. Your Stand is called Stone Free.” Jolyne’s face hardens. Nobody here knows that name. Not the guards. Not the inmates. Not even most of the people who have tried to kill her. “Who the hell are you?” Jolyne asks. Makima tilts her head. “I told you. I’m someone interested in you.” “Wrong answer.” Thin white string slips from beneath Jolyne’s sleeve, so fine it is nearly invisible in the prison light. It snakes across the floor, coils around the bars, and vanishes into the shadows of the corridor. Makima notices. Of course she notices. “That’s beautiful,” she says. Jolyne’s mouth twists. “You’ve got a real creepy way of complimenting people.” “I mean it.” Makima watches the thread with open fascination. “You turn yourself into something flexible. Something that can survive by changing shape. It’s a very human ability.” “Funny. You don’t sound like you like humans much.” Makima’s smile softens. “I like humans very much.” The words are perfectly delivered. Warm. Reassuring. They make Jolyne feel colder. Behind Makima, at the far end of the corridor, a guard appears. He is a broad man with a riot shield and a shotgun. His face is pale, his eyes unfocused. “Ma’am,” he says. “Is everything all right?” Makima does not look at him. “Yes,” she says. “Please wait outside.” The guard turns immediately. Not like he chooses to. Like a switch has been flipped inside his head. Jolyne watches him walk away. Her string, threaded beneath the doorframe hours ago, has been listening to the guards’ conversations all night. She knows that man’s voice. She knows he complained about his knee, laughed about a football game, cursed when his coffee spilled. Now he moves without a sound. Jolyne’s gaze returns to Makima. “You did that.” Makima folds her hands in front of her. “He’s doing his job.” “Bullshit.” “You’re very perceptive.” “Don’t talk to me like I’m a damn puppy.” For the first time, something almost real enters Makima’s expression: interest. “Would you prefer I talk to you as an equal?” Jolyne gets to her feet. Stone Free appears behind her, pale and muscular, its body patterned like tightly wound cords. Its eyes lock on Makima through the bars. The corridor lights flicker. Somewhere above, another security camera clicks uselessly into darkness. Makima studies the Stand without fear. “You can see it?” Jolyne says. “Yes.” “Great. That makes one of us who’s not full of surprises.” Makima steps closer until her face is inches from the bars. “You are full of surprises, Jolyne. Your father is Jotaro Kujo. Your bloodline is connected to unusual phenomena. You’re imprisoned, isolated, angry, and still you haven’t broken.” “Maybe I’m just too stubborn to give assholes what they want.” “That is one way to put it.” Jolyne’s string shifts around the bars, silent as spider silk. One strand loops around Makima’s wrist. Makima glances down at it. Then another thread slips toward her throat. “You came in here alone,” Jolyne says. “You knew what I could do. So either you’re stupid, or you think you’ve got me figured out.” “I do think I have you figured out.” Jolyne tightens the string. It bites into Makima’s skin. A thin line of blood appears. Makima does not flinch. “You care about people,” Makima says. “That makes you predictable.” Jolyne’s eyes narrow. “What?” “You want to protect your friends. Your father. Even people who don’t deserve it. You hate being controlled because you’ve been trapped before—by the justice system, by enemies, by expectations. If someone threatened the people you love, you would act.” “Yeah,” Jolyne snaps. “And?” “And that means I would never need to control you directly.” The string around Makima’s wrist jerks tighter. Stone Free raises one fist. Then Jolyne hears a sound through the threads she has spread through the prison. A scream. Not close. Far above, in the regular women’s ward. Ermes. Jolyne freezes. The scream cuts off. Makima watches her with patient eyes. “What did you do?” Jolyne says. “I didn’t do anything.” “Don’t lie to me.” “I gave someone instructions.” Jolyne’s breath comes fast. Her threads tremble against the walls, searching through pipes and cracks, racing toward the sound. She hears boots. A cell door opening. Someone crying. A guard saying, in a dull obedient voice, that an inmate needs to be moved. Ermes’s voice curses from somewhere beyond the concrete. “JOLYNE!” Stone Free punches the bars. Metal shrieks. Makima does not move. “There,” Makima says quietly. “That is the difference between us. You see people as precious because they can be hurt. I see people as useful because they can be directed.” Jolyne’s fist stops inches from Makima’s face. Makima’s blood runs down her wrist. Her expression remains gentle. “If you attack me,” she continues, “your friend suffers. If you cooperate, she is returned safely. It’s a simple exchange.” Jolyne stares at her. The anger in her chest is hot enough to burn through the whole damn prison. She wants to smash that calm face. Wants to rip every sweet word out of Makima’s mouth with Stone Free’s strings. But beneath the rage is something worse. Understanding. Makima has not come to recruit her. She has come to test her leash. Jolyne slowly withdraws the thread from Makima’s throat. Makima smiles as if she has won. Then Jolyne grins. It is not a nice grin. “You really think you’re the only one who can pull strings?” Makima’s eyes sharpen. Jolyne yanks her hand back. The strand around Makima’s wrist comes loose—but not before it pulls a tiny thread of blood from the cut on her skin. The string retracts through the bars, disappearing into Jolyne’s palm. Makima looks down at her wrist. Jolyne presses the bloodied thread between her fingers. Stone Free’s strings ripple outward through the prison walls, spreading farther, faster, weaving through air vents and drainpipes. Not searching for Ermes. Searching for every guard Makima has touched. Every obedient voice. Every door left unlocked. “You told me I’m predictable,” Jolyne says. “Here’s the thing, lady. I don’t need to beat you tonight.” The emergency lights flare red. Alarms begin to scream. Across the facility, cell locks snap open one by one. Makima’s pleasant expression finally cracks—not into fear, but calculation. Jolyne backs away from the bars. “I just need to make sure nobody in this place knows who’s holding the leash.” Outside, the corridor erupts with shouting. Makima looks toward the chaos, then back at Jolyne. “You’re risking a great many lives.” Jolyne’s eyes flash. “No. You are.” For the first time, Makima’s smile looks thin. The cameras remain dead as she turns and walks away through the red emergency light, leaving a trail of blood on the concrete. Jolyne listens through the strings. She hears Ermes alive. She hears guards waking from invisible orders. And beneath it all, she hears Makima’s footsteps receding down the hall—steady, unhurried, certain. Like someone who knows this is not the end. Jolyne sits back on her bunk, fists clenched. “Yeah,” she mutters to the empty cell. “You better keep walking.” Above her, another camera sputters back to life. For one second, it captures Jolyne Cujoh smiling into the dark.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This story demonstrates strong technical execution and character understanding within the supernatural prison setting. The author effectively captures both characters' essential traits - Jolyne's hot-headed defiance and street-smart adaptability, and Makima's manipulative control masked by false pleasantness. The dialogue feels authentic to both characters, with Jolyne's profanity-laced directness contrasting sharply with Makima's deceptively gentle speech patterns. The pacing builds tension effectively, starting with atmospheric details (cameras dying, empty corridors) and escalating through psychological manipulation to Jolyne's clever counter-gambit. The author shows strong understanding of both characters' abilities - Stone Free's string manipulation is used creatively for surveillance and eventual prison break, while Makima's control powers are demonstrated through the guard's robotic compliance. The prose maintains consistent quality throughout, with particularly effective moments like "Her eyes are wrong—yellow, ringed like targets, calm in a way no normal person's eyes should be." The imagery of strings and control creates a thematic throughline that reinforces the central conflict about manipulation and agency. However, the story occasionally relies on convenient plot elements - Makima's decision to threaten Ermes feels somewhat arbitrary as a pressure point, and the resolution where Jolyne uses Makima's blood to somehow coordinate a prison break stretches credibility even within the supernatural context. The ending, while satisfying, doesn't fully resolve the conflict so much as postpone it. The characterization remains true to source material without feeling derivative. Jolyne's refusal to be controlled and her protective instincts drive her actions believably, while Makima's clinical approach to human manipulation feels authentically chilling. The battle of wits escalates naturally from suspicion to open conflict. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 16 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 16.0, Nuanced Characters: 17.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17.0, Elegant Prose: 16.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 15.0, Coherent: 16.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 2.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5.0, Amateurish: 3.0, Purple Prose: 4.0, Overwrought: 5.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 4.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 17.0, Overall Impression: 17.0
+ Scp: Notes from the Future — Score: 82.2
Prompt:
Write an SCP report about a researcher who begins finding handwritten notes from their future self, warning about a containment breach that hasn't happened yet. The notes become increasingly desperate and incoherent, sometimes containing information that contradicts known reality. Include the Foundation's attempts to determine if this is an actual temporal anomaly or psychological manipulation by a contained entity, and the researcher's struggle to trust their own perception. First person (researcher's perspective with interspersed notes), past tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Item Designation:** Provisional Anomalous Correspondence Event-███ **Author:** Dr. Elian Voss, Temporal Cognition Division **Status:** Under Investigation I began finding the notes on 14/03/20██. The first appeared beneath my right hand while I was reviewing the weekly observation logs for SCP-███, a low-risk memetic artifact contained three levels below my office. I had been reading a report on auditory hallucinations in exposed personnel. My desk was bare except for my terminal, a coffee cup, and a stack of requisition forms. Then my fingers touched paper. I looked down and found a folded square of lined notebook paper resting beneath my palm. The handwriting was mine. Not similar to mine. Not an imitation. Mine: the same uneven downward pressure, the same tendency to cross the letter “t” too far to the right, the same narrow loops in lowercase “g.” The ink was blue-black, slightly smeared along the edge as though the writer had been in a hurry. It read: > **ELIAN—DO NOT GO TO LEVEL 4 AT 16:20.** > **The breach begins there. You will think it is a fire alarm. It is not.** > **Do not let Mara open the north stairwell.** > > **—You, later** I laughed at first. I wish I could say that I had reacted professionally. I did not. I laughed because the note was absurd, because someone in the department had clearly decided that my reputation for skepticism made me an entertaining target. Then I checked the security feed. Camera 4B, mounted above my office door, showed me sitting at my desk. At 15:47:13, the image tore into horizontal bands of static. The distortion lasted exactly 0.8 seconds. When the picture returned, the folded note was on my desk. No one entered the room. No one approached the room. At 16:18, I received a message from Dr. Mara Kline requesting that I meet her on Level 4 to discuss an equipment transfer. At 16:20, the building fire alarm activated. I did not go. At 16:22, the north stairwell door on Level 4 opened remotely during the alarm cycle. Dr. Kline, who had gone to investigate without me, found it unlocked and standing ajar. Behind it was no fire, no breach, and no intruder. Only a corridor that should not have existed. The stairwell was sealed immediately. The corridor was gone when response personnel arrived. Dr. Kline had sustained a superficial laceration to her forearm. She could not explain how she received it. She repeatedly stated that someone had warned her not to step through the doorway. The next note arrived the following morning. > **Good. You listened. That buys us thirty-six hours.** > **Do not tell Kline about the notes. She is already compromised.** > **The thing in the south wing knows your name now.** > > **—E.V.** I reported the event to Site Command after that. Protocol required that I submit to psychiatric screening, memetic hazard evaluation, and a full interview with Internal Security. My office was swept for concealed delivery mechanisms. Its ventilation ducts were inspected. The desk was dismantled. No devices, foreign fibers, or anomalous residue were found. The cameras continued to malfunction. Every time a note appeared, every camera monitoring my office experienced a synchronized image failure lasting between 0.7 and 1.1 seconds. The failures extended to backup feeds on a separate network. Analog recording equipment was installed. It also failed, though differently: the tape did not distort. It simply contained a blank white frame at the relevant moment. The third note was not folded. It was crumpled, damp, and stained with something brown-red that analysis later identified as human blood. My blood, according to DNA testing. > **DO NOT TRUST THE BLOOD TEST.** > **IT LEARNS FROM SAMPLES.** > > **You told them about the corridor. Why did you tell them?** > **Why did you let them take the recorder?** > > **We are not in Site-██ anymore. We have not been for days.** At that point, the investigation expanded. The primary hypothesis was a temporal anomaly, possibly a localized informational loop centered on my workspace. The notes might have originated from a future version of myself attempting to alter a containment event. Temporal specialists examined the paper, ink, handwriting, and linguistic patterns. The paper was manufactured in 20██, two years after the date of the incident. The ink contained a chemical stabilizer not yet approved for commercial use. That should have settled the question. It did not. The second hypothesis came from Dr. Havel, assigned from Psychological Warfare and Counter-Memetics. He suggested that the notes were being generated or influenced by SCP-███. SCP-███ was the artifact whose logs I had been reading when the first note appeared. It consisted of a handbound notebook recovered from a civilian cult site in ████████. Persons who read more than three pages often reported receiving “correspondence” from dead relatives, future selves, or alternate versions of themselves. The messages consistently encouraged isolation, secrecy, and distrust of containment personnel. The artifact had been secured in a cognitohazard locker, and I had never read it directly. However, I had reviewed transcripts. Dr. Havel argued that exposure to transcripts might have been sufficient. I objected. The notes had physical mass. They had appeared on camera, however briefly. Their paper was chemically impossible. The blood was mine. Havel asked whether I trusted the laboratory results. I did not answer him. The fourth note arrived during my first formal interview. I had been seated in Observation Room 2 with Dr. Havel, Director Sato, and two armed guards outside the door. My hands were visible on the metal table. There was no desk. No paper. No pockets in my containment smock. At 09:11:06, every camera in the room glitched. When the feed recovered, there was a note under my left wrist. Director Sato ordered no one to touch it. I read it anyway. > **THEY WILL SAY I AM MAKING YOU SICK.** > **They will show you the footage. Do not watch the footage after 09:11.** > > **Havel died three days ago. Look at his shoes.** > > **Do not let them take you below. The lower levels are flooded. The doors float open.** Dr. Havel was alive. He was sitting across from me, pale but composed. I looked at his shoes. They were wet. Not damp. Wet. Dark water had soaked through the leather around the soles and spread across the floor beneath his chair. He followed my gaze and looked down. His expression changed. “I have not been downstairs,” he said. Director Sato ordered the interview terminated. The security footage after 09:11 was reviewed despite the note’s warning. It showed the static event. It showed the note appearing. It also showed something else. For fourteen frames, Dr. Havel was absent from his chair. Not obscured. Not distorted. Absent. The chair was empty. In the following frame, he was back. By then, I had begun keeping a written log of inconsistencies. Not because I trusted paper, but because I no longer trusted memory. Dr. Kline’s forearm injury was first recorded as a laceration, then as a chemical burn. Her personnel file listed her as assigned to Site-██ for six years. I remembered hiring her eleven months earlier. The north stairwell was sealed, then it had never existed, then it was listed on the evacuation map again. I began hearing alarms at night. Sometimes they came from the hall. Sometimes from inside the walls. Once, I woke with my office desk in my quarters and a note pinned to my blanket with a surgical staple. > **YOU KEEP ASKING WHICH ONE OF US IS REAL.** > **THAT IS THE WRONG QUESTION.** > > **THE BREACH HAPPENED WHEN YOU READ THE FIRST NOTE.** > **Everything after that is containment.** > > **I am trying to keep you from becoming me.** I requested amnestics. The request was denied pending cognitive evaluation. I requested termination of the investigation and transfer to a non-anomalous facility. The request was denied. I requested that SCP-███ be destroyed. The request was denied. On 19/03/20██, Site Command authorized controlled testing. I was placed in a sterile chamber with continuous analog and digital surveillance. My hands were restrained. A temporal anchor was installed outside the room. SCP-███ remained sealed three floors away. Dr. Havel observed through reinforced glass. At 14:03:27, the cameras glitched. When the image returned, there was no note. There was only writing. Someone had covered both walls, the floor, the observation glass, and my own forearms in blue-black handwriting. The restraints remained locked. No writing instrument was present. Most of the text was illegible. Some of it consisted of dates that had not occurred. Some named staff members who had never been employed at the Site. Some described a containment breach involving an ocean in the basement, a sky visible through the elevators, and a version of myself standing in every reflective surface. One sentence was repeated 317 times. > **THE NOTES ARE NOT FROM THE FUTURE. THE FUTURE IS FROM THE NOTES.** The final message was written directly beneath the observation window, facing Dr. Havel. > **ELIAN, IF YOU CAN READ THIS, DO NOT TRUST THE MAN BEHIND THE GLASS.** > **HE IS NOT HAVEL.** > **HE IS YOU AFTER THE BREACH.** I looked through the glass. Dr. Havel was gone. In his place stood a man in my stained laboratory coat. He raised one hand to the window. I did the same. The glass between us was cold. The note that appeared beneath my palm afterward was the last one I was permitted to retain. > **I am sorry.** > **I tried to warn us sooner.**
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This SCP report demonstrates strong adherence to the Foundation's clinical documentation style while effectively building psychological horror through escalating temporal anomalies. The piece successfully maintains the requested first-person perspective of Dr. Voss, interspersed with increasingly desperate handwritten notes that serve as both plot devices and sources of mounting dread. The narrative structure is well-crafted, beginning with a seemingly simple anomaly and systematically undermining the protagonist's (and reader's) sense of reality. The progression from skeptical laughter to existential terror feels earned and believable. The author demonstrates solid understanding of SCP Foundation conventions, employing appropriate redactions, clinical language, and bureaucratic procedures that ground the supernatural elements in institutional reality. Character actions remain consistently believable throughout. Dr. Voss responds to each escalation with appropriate professional skepticism before being forced to confront increasingly undeniable evidence. The supporting characters like Dr. Kline and Dr. Havel serve their functions effectively, though they remain somewhat thin as individuals. The prose maintains an appropriately clinical tone that gradually becomes more fragmented and desperate as the protagonist's reality deteriorates. This shift in voice effectively mirrors the psychological breakdown without becoming overwrought. The imagery is particularly strong in the later sections - wet shoes, floating doors, writing covering walls - creating visceral unease through specific, concrete details. The piece excels at "show don't tell," allowing readers to piece together the horror through accumulated evidence rather than explicit exposition. The temporal paradox concept is handled with sophistication, avoiding easy explanations while maintaining internal logic within its impossible framework. However, the piece has some limitations. The supporting characters lack depth beyond their functional roles. Some of the middle sections feel slightly repetitive in their pattern of note-appearance-investigation-escalation. The ending, while appropriately dark and circular, might benefit from slightly more resolution or clarity about the nature of the breach. The dialogue, while limited, serves its purpose effectively. The clinical exchanges feel authentic to the institutional setting. The horror builds organically without relying on cheap shocks or excessive gore, instead creating dread through the systematic erosion of certainty and identity. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 13 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 16 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16

+ Scp: Unmarked Doors Archive — Score: 83.8
Prompt:
Write an SCP entry about a series of identical unmarked doors appearing in abandoned buildings worldwide that lead to a massive, architecturally impossible archive. Documents within reference a previous global civilization destroyed by an unspecified containment failure. Foundation researchers discover references to their own organization under a different name, including personnel files for current staff dated centuries ago. Include exploration logs revealing sections of the archive actively being removed by entities resembling hazmat teams who flee when approached, and evidence that O5 Council members have visited the archive without authorization. Third person (exploration team perspective) with recovered document excerpts. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Item #:** SCP-7819 **Object Class:** Keter **Special Containment Procedures:** Instances of SCP-7819 are to be secured within temporary perimeter structures upon discovery. No attempt is to be made to relocate, destroy, or obstruct an instance. A minimum exclusion zone of 150 m is to be established around each doorway. Entry is restricted to Level 4 personnel assigned to Project PALIMPSEST. Exploration teams are to carry tether lines, redundant video equipment, and emergency extraction markers. Personnel are forbidden from removing documents bearing recognizable Foundation insignia, names of currently active personnel, or dates later than 1890 without written approval from the Site Director and at least two O5 Council members. Any personnel encountering individuals wearing pale gray environmental suits are to withdraw immediately. Under no circumstances are they to pursue, engage, or attempt communication. Following Incident 7819-12, all O5-level access records are to be cross-referenced against internal archive surveillance. This directive remains pending O5 approval. --- ## Description SCP-7819 is a recurring phenomenon involving identical unmarked doors appearing in abandoned or permanently unoccupied buildings worldwide. To date, forty-three instances have been documented in locations ranging from condemned apartment blocks in Warsaw to decommissioned naval facilities in Chile, abandoned shopping centers in the American Midwest, and a partially collapsed research station in Antarctica. Each door is constructed from dark, apparently unpainted wood and measures precisely 2.13 m in height and 0.91 m in width. No hinges, handle, locking mechanism, grain pattern, or maker’s marks are visible. Attempts to damage, scan, or sample the doors have been unsuccessful. Materials placed in contact with their surfaces have displayed rapid corrosion, embrittlement, or loss of structural cohesion. The doors only manifest in complete darkness. Witnesses consistently report that, prior to visual confirmation of an instance, **a faint glow escapes through the door’s edges in complete darkness.** The illumination is warm, amber in hue, and is not detectable once ambient light levels rise above 0.3 lux. Opening an SCP-7819 instance leads not to the room or structure behind it, but to a shared extradimensional environment designated the **Archive**. The Archive is a vast, architecturally inconsistent complex of reading rooms, record vaults, galleries, stairwells, subterranean stacks, and monumental administrative halls. Its internal geometry does not correspond to any physical arrangement. Corridors may ascend into lower levels, doors may open into rooms previously exited, and exterior-facing windows frequently display views of unfamiliar cities, oceans, or skies. The Archive appears to contain records of a technologically advanced global civilization referred to in recovered materials as the **Continuance**, the **Unified Civil Administration**, or, in several damaged English-language documents, “the Last Arrangement.” Most documents describe a civilization spanning multiple continents and maintaining advanced anomalous-containment infrastructure. The civilization’s collapse is repeatedly attributed to an event called the “Failure,” “the Open Condition,” or “Containment Event Zero.” No recovered document gives a complete account of the event. Many accounts terminate abruptly mid-sentence. Others have been physically excised from shelves, leaving only dust-free rectangular gaps and tags marked **REMOVED FOR TRANSIT**. Of increasing concern are references to an organization resembling the SCP Foundation, identified in Archive documents as the **Custodial Foundation for the Preservation of Normalcy**, abbreviated **CFPN**. Personnel records located within the Archive include photographs and biographical details corresponding to living Foundation staff, despite being dated between 214 and 640 years before their birth. --- ## Addendum 7819.1: Initial Exploration Log **Exploration Team:** MTF Sigma-9 (“Paper Trail”) **Entry Point:** SCP-7819-03, abandoned municipal library, ████████, Germany **Date:** 2024/08/14 > **[BEGIN LOG]** > > **09:12:** Team enters through SCP-7819-03. The doorway opens into a circular reading hall approximately 80 m in diameter. The ceiling is not visible. Shelves extend vertically into darkness. > > **Agent Bell:** No dust. No decay. Books look recently shelved. > > **Dr. Morrow:** They are not books. Mostly boxes. Records cartons. > > **09:19:** Team finds a brass directory mounted beside a staircase. Entries include “Meteorological Anomalies,” “Unresolved Persons,” “Continuity Breaches,” and “Records of the First Silence.” > > **09:24:** Agent Bell reports that the staircase descends for fourteen minutes without reaching a landing. Team elects to return to the reading hall. > > **09:26:** Team emerges into a different room: a long corridor lined with glass-fronted offices. All offices contain desks, chairs, and dormant computer terminals of unfamiliar design. > > **09:31:** Dr. Morrow recovers a folder labeled: > *CFPN / NORTH ATLANTIC OPERATIONS / PERSONNEL ARCHIVE.* > > **09:33:** Folder contains a personnel file for Dr. Morrow. > > **Dr. Morrow:** This is not funny. > > **Agent Bell:** Nobody is laughing. > > **09:34:** File photograph matches Dr. Morrow, including a surgical scar acquired in 2017. Document date reads **14 March 1681**. > > **Dr. Morrow:** It says I was assigned to “Station Greywater.” It says I died there. > > **09:35:** Audio distortion begins. Team reports hearing distant carts rolling across tile. > > **09:37:** Four figures observed at far end of corridor. They wear gray, hooded environmental suits with opaque black faceplates. Each pushes a wheeled shelving unit covered in white sheeting. > > **Agent Bell:** Hazmat? > > **Dr. Morrow:** Foundation doesn’t use that configuration. > > **09:38:** Agent Bell calls out. The figures stop. One turns toward the team. Its faceplate reflects the corridor behind it, but not the exploration team. > > **09:39:** The figures abandon the shelving units and retreat through a doorway not previously present in the wall. > > **09:40:** Team advances toward abandoned carts. Several shelves are empty except for document fragments and inventory tags. > > **Recovered tag:** > *TRANSFER AUTHORITY: POST-FAILURE SALVAGE* > *DESTINATION: NOT YET SECURE* > *DO NOT ALLOW THE CUSTODIANS TO REACQUIRE MATERIAL.* > > **09:42:** Emergency extraction ordered after Agent Bell reports seeing his own name printed repeatedly on the corridor walls. > > **[END LOG]** --- ## Addendum 7819.2: Recovered Document Excerpts The following documents were retrieved during Exploration 7819-03. Orthography has been standardized for readability. --- ### Document 7819-A > **FROM:** Office of Civil Preservation, Continuance Central > **TO:** Regional Custodial Administrations > **DATE:** 03/██/████ > **SUBJECT:** Public Communication Following the First Breach > > Do not describe the event as a breach. > > The public has endured three centuries of stability because it has understood the world to be stable. This understanding is itself infrastructural. If it fails, other systems will follow. > > The Custodial Foundation has proposed a complete suppression protocol. Their directors insist the affected zones remain containable. > > We remind all recipients that the Foundation has made this claim before. > > There is no evidence that “containment” remains a meaningful term. --- ### Document 7819-B > **PERSONNEL FILE:** MORROW, ELIAS H. > **EMPLOYING BODY:** Custodial Foundation for the Preservation of Normalcy > **CLEARANCE:** BLACK-4 > **DATE OF APPOINTMENT:** 1663 > **STATUS:** DECEASED, PRESUMED > > Subject demonstrates recurring identity displacement following exposure to archival artifacts. Subject has repeatedly claimed to originate from “a later Foundation” with identical objectives and inferior terminology. > > Recommendation: Do not inform subject that his statements have been recorded before. Dr. Elias Morrow, currently assigned to Project PALIMPSEST, has denied prior knowledge of the document. Psychological evaluation has been suspended at his request. --- ### Document 7819-C > **MINUTES OF THE HIGH OVERSIGHT COMMITTEE** > **SESSION: EMERGENCY / CLOSED** > **ATTENDANCE:** 05-1, 05-2, 05-3, 05-4, 05-5, 05-6, 05-7, 05-8, 05-9, 05-10, 05-11, 05-12, 05-13 > > 05-7: The Archive was not built to preserve us. > > 05-3: It preserves records. We are records. > > 05-11: Then why are portions being removed? > > [Twenty-seven lines redacted by physical excision.] > > 05-1: If the doors recur, we must ensure that our successors find the right version. > > 05-6: There will not be successors. > > 05-1: There are always successors. That is the problem. The formatting and title structure correspond closely to modern O5 Council minutes. However, the document substrate has been carbon-dated to approximately the late 15th century. --- ## Addendum 7819.3: Unauthorized Visitor Evidence During Exploration 7819-11, MTF Sigma-9 accessed a large chamber designated **“The Hall of Consultations.”** The room contained a central table surrounded by thirteen high-backed chairs. Each chair bore a brass plaque. Twelve plaques had been scratched beyond legibility. The thirteenth read: > **O5-█** > **VISITOR, NOT CUSTODIAN** > **ENTRY RECORDED: 2023/11/██** > **EXIT STATUS: UNRESOLVED** Foundation access records indicate no authorized expedition to SCP-7819 occurred on that date. Further investigation found footprints in a layer of fine gray ash surrounding the table. The prints matched the sole pattern issued to senior Foundation personnel assigned to Site-01. The ash contained traces of paper fiber, human skin cells, and an unidentified mineral compound consistent with residue recovered from the gray-suited entities’ abandoned carts. A chair at the head of the table had recently been occupied. Its cushion retained body heat for six minutes after the exploration team entered. No individual was observed leaving the chamber. --- ## Addendum 7819.4: Final Note from Project PALIMPSEST On 2025/01/██, a new SCP-7819 instance appeared inside the sealed records wing of Site-19. No abandoned building was associated with the manifestation. The door appeared between two shelving units containing Foundation personnel files. Internal lights failed simultaneously across the records wing. Security footage shows a faint amber glow emerging around the frame. When emergency lighting activated, the door was gone. In its place was a single archive box bearing the following label: > **CUSTODIAL FOUNDATION FOR THE PRESERVATION OF NORMALCY** > **FINAL DISPOSITION RECORDS** > **VOLUME 1 OF 13** > **PREPARED FOR RECOVERY**
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This SCP entry demonstrates strong adherence to the Foundation's clinical documentation style while weaving a compelling narrative about temporal paradoxes and organizational identity. The author successfully captures the bureaucratic tone characteristic of SCP entries, maintaining consistency throughout the technical language, containment procedures, and exploration logs. The piece excels in world-building and conceptual creativity. The idea of doors leading to an archive containing records of a previous civilization that mirrors the Foundation creates an unsettling recursive loop that fits perfectly within SCP mythology. The "Custodial Foundation for the Preservation of Normalcy" serves as an effective historical mirror, and the discovery of current personnel files dated centuries ago creates genuine existential dread. The exploration logs are particularly well-crafted, using realistic dialogue and procedural language that feels authentic to military/scientific operations. The interaction between Dr. Morrow and Agent Bell feels natural, and their reactions to discovering Morrow's own personnel file from 1681 are believable and appropriately disturbing. The imagery is consistently strong without becoming overwrought. Details like the amber glow visible only in complete darkness, the architecturally impossible geometry, and the gray-suited entities create a palpable sense of unease. The author shows restraint in descriptions, allowing the clinical tone to enhance rather than diminish the horror. The escalating revelations are well-paced, from the initial discovery of the doors to the evidence of unauthorized O5 visits, culminating in a door appearing within Site-19 itself. This progression maintains tension while expanding the scope of the anomaly's implications. However, some elements feel slightly predictable within SCP conventions - the idea of Foundation predecessors and temporal loops, while well-executed here, has been explored before. The gray-suited entities, while atmospheric, remain somewhat generic as mysterious antagonists. The ending is appropriately dark and open-ended, suggesting the Foundation itself may be caught in a cycle of destruction and recreation. The final image of the archive box appearing in Site-19 provides a chilling conclusion that implies the current Foundation's fate may already be sealed. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
