+ Iteration 3 — Avg Score: 82.2
+ Classic sci-fi: The Azra Gambit — Score: 70.9
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi (Author style: Asimov) The Azra Gambit Colonial mars is being mined by corporations who take leases on indentured labourers. The thing they are mining is Azra, a recently discovered exotic metal which accelerates radioactive decay to such a rate that it is greatly sought after for interstellar drives and weapons alike. This has created both a gold rush and an arms race as various interests vie for control and endeavour to unlock Azra's secrets. The story follows Arthur Neegan, a first generation settler and mining engineer. Upon discovering that his unassuming plot sits atop an immense Azra vein, he is subjected to a flurry of interest and scrutiny. Write the next chapter in this story, in which an armed retinue descends on Arthur's home and politely but forcefully invites him to a meeting with some unknown party off-world. The insignia look like that of the Antares diplomatic corp -- diplomatic in name only. Arthur finds himself in the centre of a political tug of war. The chapter involves a meeting with this unknown party, who makes Arthur an offer. The scene should be primarily dialogue, interspersed with vivid description & scene setting. It should sow hints of the larger intrigue, stakes & dangers. Include Asimov's trademark big-and-small-picture world building and retrofuturistic classic scifi vibe. The chapter begins with Arthur aboard the transfer vessel, wondering just what he's gotten involved in. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
I can’t write in Asimov’s exact style, but I can write a classic mid-century, idea-driven science-fiction chapter with crisp dialogue, planetary politics, and a retrofuturist atmosphere. --- The transfer vessel’s air filtration system hums with a persistent mechanical drone. Arthur Neegan had listened to it for forty-three minutes by the chronometer set into the bulkhead opposite him. It was not a particularly remarkable hum. It had no rhythm, no modulation, no suggestion of malice. Yet he had come to hate it with an intensity disproportionate to its nature. That was because it was the only thing in the cabin that admitted it was alive. The two men seated across from him had not moved since the ship had left the landing pad outside his homestead. They wore slate-gray field uniforms without rank flashes. Their sidearms were holstered in plain view, which Arthur understood was a courtesy. Men who wished to conceal a threat did not wear weapons openly. On the breast of each uniform was a small silver device: a star with six pointed rays, enclosed in a circle. Antares Diplomatic Corps. Diplomatic in the way a sealed pressure door was diplomatic. It did not argue; it merely separated those on one side from those on the other. Arthur folded his hands in his lap. “Are we in orbit?” he asked. The man nearest the hatch did not look at him. “We are in transit.” “That is not an answer.” “It is the answer authorized for you at present.” Arthur looked toward the porthole. It was shuttered. “I own a mining claim,” he said. “I have an unpaid lease balance, three drilling rigs, twelve indentured men assigned to my books, and a water recycler that is overdue for service. I do not have time for authorized answers.” The second man, the younger one, glanced at him. His expression contained something that might have been pity. “You have time now, Mr. Neegan.” Arthur felt the craft turn. Not a violent movement, but a subtle alteration in the artificial gravity. Somewhere beyond the walls, machinery altered its complaint by half a tone. He said, “Am I under arrest?” “No.” “Have I been charged with anything?” “No.” “Then why was my front door cut open?” The older man answered. “Your door was not cut open. Its locking mechanism was bypassed.” “It exploded inward.” “A technical distinction.” Arthur smiled thinly. “You people do excellent technical work.” The younger man said, “You will have an opportunity to express concerns to the representative.” “Whose representative?” Neither replied. Arthur leaned back. The seat was molded gray plastisteel, too clean for a colonial transfer craft and too expensive for a corporate shuttle. That, more than the men’s weapons or their insignia, had convinced him that he was in deep trouble. A farm owner could be bullied by a corporation. A mine engineer could be bought by a consortium. A debtor could be collected by the Labor Registry. But no ordinary concern sent an Antares vessel for a man with fifty hectares of dry Martian soil and a dead wife’s portrait still hanging in his kitchen. The Azra vein had changed matters. Three weeks ago, his deep survey probe had registered an anomaly beneath the basalt shelf. At first he had assumed a fault in the instrument. Azra had been found in scattered seams across the Tharsis Basin, never in quantity, always mixed with useless regolith and dangerous isotopes. The surveys had promised traces. Arthur had discovered a continent of it. Or so the calculations said. And calculations, Arthur had learned in thirty-seven years on Mars, were only facts that had not yet acquired enemies. The hatch opened without warning. “Mr. Neegan,” said the older guard. “We have arrived.” They escorted him down a narrow corridor lined with soft blue illumination. The vessel was larger than he had supposed. Through an open service hatch he glimpsed a bank of humming cylinders, each stamped with the red-and-gold insignia of the Antares League: a stylized sun crossed by a spear. The League had called itself a union of sovereign systems. Mars called it an alliance. Earth called it an inconvenience. The men and women living in the frontier worlds called it many other things, usually after checking the room for listening devices. At the end of the corridor stood a circular door. It irised open. The room beyond was almost absurdly luxurious. There was real wood on the walls, dark and glossy. Arthur had seen real wood only twice in his life: once in a museum in Lowell City, and once in a corporate director’s office where it had been displayed behind glass like a relic of an extinct civilization. A window, unshuttered, looked onto Mars. The planet filled half the sky. Red deserts curled beneath white cloud streaks. The black line of space made a hard arc above the horizon. Far below, the mining lights of the Tharsis settlements glimmered in clusters, tiny and regular as circuitry. A woman stood before the window. She was perhaps fifty, though the expensive smoothness of her face made that uncertain. Her hair was silver, cut short in the severe fashion of the Antares administrative class. She wore no uniform, only a dark blue suit with a narrow silver line at the collar. “Arthur Neegan,” she said. “Apparently.” She turned, and smiled. “You are not what I expected.” “You have been looking at the wrong reports.” “That is possible. Reports are usually written by men hoping to be promoted.” “And you are?” “Director Selene Varr. Special Envoy of the Antares Diplomatic Corps.” Arthur looked at the star insignia on her collar. It was the same as the guards’, but made of platinum. “Diplomatic,” he said. “Officially.” “And unofficially?” “Efficient.” She gestured to a chair. “Please sit.” Arthur remained standing. “I would rather know why I was removed from my property by armed men.” Varr’s expression did not alter. “You were not removed, Mr. Neegan. You were extracted from a developing security incident.” “My property is the security incident.” “Your property is a geological fact. The incident is the number of people who now know about it.” Arthur considered that. “Who knows?” “Three Martian lease corporations. Two Earthside industrial groups. The Colonial Directorate. The Labor Registry, of course.” She paused. “And at least one intelligence service not formally acknowledged by its government.” “You left out Antares.” “No,” Varr said. “I included us under intelligence service.” The honesty was so direct that Arthur sat down. Varr took the opposite chair. Between them stood a low table bearing two glasses and a bottle of amber liquid. Arthur did not touch either. “What is Azra?” he asked. “An element.” “That is what the university papers say.” “And what do you think it is?” “I think it is something that makes matter impatient.” Varr’s eyes sharpened. Arthur continued. “Put it near a radioactive isotope and the half-life collapses. Fuel becomes power. Waste becomes heat. A warhead becomes something a great deal worse than a warhead.” “Good,” she said. “You understand the problem.” “I understand why corporations want it.” “Corporations want profit. Governments want leverage. Armies want certainty.” Varr leaned forward. “Azra offers all three, but not in the quantities previously discovered.” “And mine does?” “Your vein, if the survey data are genuine, contains more Azra than all known deposits combined.” Arthur said, “My survey data were private.” “Nothing is private once it becomes important.” Outside the window, Mars turned silently below them. Arthur thought of his homestead: the low pressure dome, the dusty solar panels, the narrow greenhouse where he had grown tomatoes that tasted faintly of metal. He thought of the indentured workers who had looked at him with fear as the Antares men arrived. He had told them it was nothing. He had lied. “What do you want?” he asked. Varr poured herself a glass of the amber liquid. “We want you to refuse the first offer you receive.” Arthur stared at her. “That is your proposal?” “It is the beginning of it.” “Why?” “Because the first offer will be from Helix Mining Consolidated. They will offer you a generous purchase price, debt cancellation, and residency privileges in one of the inner domes.” “They already sent a representative.” “Then you know their style.” “He had a gold pen.” “Helix is very proud of its pen.” Arthur almost smiled. “And what happens when I refuse?” “Then they will ask the Colonial Directorate to review the legal status of your claim. If that fails, they will discover irregularities in your labor contracts. If that fails, there may be an accident at one of your shafts.” Arthur’s face hardened. “My men are not bargaining pieces.” “No,” said Varr quietly. “They are hostages. Everyone in this matter is a hostage to someone.” The hum of the filtration system seemed suddenly louder. Arthur said, “What does Antares offer?” Varr placed her glass down. “Recognition of your claim under Antares protection. Technical assistance. Security personnel. A share in a new Azra Development Authority, of which you would be the founding civilian director.” “And Antares gets control of the vein.” “Antares gets access.” “Which is another word.” “Yes,” she said. “But a more negotiable one.” Arthur rose and walked to the window. Mars lay below him, ancient and mute. Humanity had crossed the void to reach it, carried its flags and laws and debts, and had somehow managed to make even this vast dead world feel crowded. “Why me?” he asked. “Because you found it.” “People have been killed for less.” “People will be killed for this,” Varr said. He turned. Her composure had gone. Only for an instant, but enough. Behind the polished envoy, Arthur saw exhaustion. Fear, perhaps. Varr said, “Mr. Neegan, there are laboratories on Earth that have begun testing Azra-reactor configurations. There are military stations around Ganymede that have requested samples under emergency authorization. There are men in the Colonial Directorate who believe Mars should declare independence before Earth can seize the field.” “And Antares?” “Antares believes that if any one power controls Azra, the rest of humanity will spend the next century learning obedience.” Arthur looked again at the red planet. “So you want me to save humanity.” “I want you,” Varr said, “to own your own discovery long enough to choose who ruins it.” For the first time since the armed men had entered his home, Arthur Neegan felt truly afraid. Not of the guards. Not of Antares. But of the possibility that the woman was telling the truth.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical competence and successfully captures many elements of classic science fiction in the Asimov tradition. The author establishes a compelling premise around the discovery of Azra, an exotic metal that accelerates radioactive decay, and places protagonist Arthur Neegan in a believable political predicament. The worldbuilding is effective, presenting a Mars that feels lived-in and authentic. Details like the water recycler needing service, the indentured workers, and the corporate lease structures create a convincing colonial setting. The political intrigue involving multiple factions (Helix Mining, Colonial Directorate, Antares League) feels appropriately complex without becoming confusing. The dialogue carries the story well, with distinct voices for Arthur and Director Varr. Arthur's practical, no-nonsense responses ("You people do excellent technical work") contrast effectively with Varr's diplomatic precision. The conversation reveals information naturally while maintaining tension. However, the piece has notable weaknesses. The pacing feels somewhat mechanical, particularly in the opening section aboard the transport vessel. The guards' responses are so uniformly evasive they border on caricature. While this may be intentional, it creates a stilted quality that undermines immersion. The characterization, while competent, lacks depth. Arthur is presented as a straightforward everyman caught in circumstances beyond his control, but we learn little about his inner life beyond basic competence and wariness. Varr is more interesting but remains largely a vehicle for exposition. The prose is generally clean but occasionally falls into tell-don't-show patterns, particularly when explaining the political situation. Phrases like "Arthur felt truly afraid" are direct statements rather than demonstrated emotions. The ending, while not inappropriately positive, feels somewhat abrupt. Arthur's realization about the truth creates dramatic tension but doesn't fully resolve the immediate narrative arc of the chapter. The piece succeeds in creating the requested retrofuturistic atmosphere and includes appropriate big-picture worldbuilding elements. The central concept of Azra as a game-changing resource that could destabilize interplanetary politics is compelling and well-executed. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 16 Believable Character Actions: 15 Nuanced Characters: 12 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 16 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 14 Elegant Prose: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 13 Emotionally Complex: 11 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 7 Tell-Don't-Show: 8 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 9 Amateurish: 7 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 3 Unearned Transformations: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Sentences Flow Naturally: 15 Overall Reader Engagement: 14 Overall Impression: 14

+ Contemporary romance: Darkroom Revelations — Score: 86.3
Prompt:
Darkroom Revelations Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of Dani, a 25-year-old assertive art student known for her bold style and take-charge attitude in relationships. For the past semester, she's been quietly fascinated by Elliot (he/him), a 19-year-old boy in her experimental photography class. Dani has been intrigued by his unconventional masculinity: killer thrift-shop-nu-goth style, soft demeanour and quiet intellect. When they're paired for a final project requiring late nights in the darkroom, Elliot tentatively shares that he's been questioning his gender identity and expression. Set during one of these intimate darkroom sessions where the red light creates a confessional atmosphere. Explore Dani's complex attraction—her usual assertiveness complicated by her desire to protect Elliot's vulnerability, her excitement at witnessing his self-discovery, and her confusion about what this means for her own identity as someone typically attracted to more defined gender presentations. The narrative should capture the tender awkwardness of their conversations, Dani's internal struggle between taking charge and wanting to give Elliot agency, and the unexpected chemistry that develops as both find themselves in uncharted territory. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The darkroom always smelled like wet pennies and old rain. Developer chemicals, damp paper, the faint plastic heat of the enlargers—none of it was pleasant, exactly, but after three years in the art building I’d started to associate it with possibility. Images came out of darkness here. Mistakes did too. At eleven forty-seven on a Thursday night, Elliot and I were the only people still working. The safelight above the sink turned everything red: his hands, the sleeves of his black mesh shirt, the silver chain looped through his belt. He had dressed like a vampire who worked in a library—oversized charcoal cardigan, lace-up boots, chipped black nail polish, a little moth pin on his collar. I had noticed the moth pin in September. I had noticed most things about Elliot since September. He was nineteen, which I tried not to think about in a way that made me feel ancient and irresponsible. He was only six years younger than me, but at twenty-five I had become accustomed to knowing what I wanted: the project, the grade, the person, the next move. I liked people who made that easy. People who walked into a room already fully outlined. Elliot was all soft edges. He was leaning over the trays, watching an image develop beneath the liquid. The photo was one he’d taken of a cracked mirror behind the old theater building. In it, his own face appeared in fragments: one eye, the curve of his mouth, the dark sweep of his hair. “It’s good,” I said. He startled slightly. “You think?” “I don’t say things I don’t mean.” “I know.” He smiled, small and nervous. “That’s kind of terrifying.” “People tell me that a lot.” “I can see why.” There was no bite in it. His eyes flicked up toward mine, then away again. I folded my arms, trying not to grin. “Is that a compliment?” “Maybe.” “Coward.” He laughed under his breath. It was a beautiful sound, annoyingly. Quiet, like he didn’t quite trust himself to take up the space. Our final project was supposed to be a collaborative series about identity and constructed selfhood. Most groups had gone obvious: portraits in costume, family archives, elaborate self-portraits with mirrors. Elliot and I had been circling something less direct. Shadows. Fragmentation. Fabric and skin and objects people carried without thinking. We had agreed to photograph each other’s hands. Then Elliot had suggested mirrors. Then, last week, he’d brought in a roll of film full of images of himself wearing clothes that seemed to belong to different versions of a person: a suit jacket over a lace blouse, combat boots with a long black skirt, his grandmother’s pearl earrings alongside a chain wallet. Nothing looked like a costume. That was what unsettled me. He looked more himself in every frame. I watched the image in the tray sharpen. “Can I ask you something?” he said. “Sure.” He didn’t answer immediately. His fingers hovered over the tongs. “You don’t have to make it a big thing.” “Okay.” “That sounded like you’re definitely going to make it a big thing.” “I’m trying to be reassuring.” “You’re bad at sounding casual.” “That is not true.” He looked at me. “Fine,” I said. “I’m terrible at sounding casual.” The image in the developer darkened. Elliot moved it to the stop bath with careful hands. “I’ve been thinking,” he said. “About gender. Mine, I guess.” I stayed still. Not dramatically still. I didn’t want to turn it into a Moment with capital letters. The red light hummed. Water trickled somewhere in the back of the room. Outside, rain tapped lightly against the high basement windows. Elliot swallowed. “I don’t know if I’m—” He stopped. “I don’t know. Not a guy, exactly. Or maybe I am a guy, but not in the way I thought I was supposed to be. That sounds stupid.” “It doesn’t.” “It does when I say it out loud.” “It doesn’t,” I repeated. He glanced at me, searching my face with such open uncertainty that something in my chest tightened. I was used to being the person who pushed. The one who said, Come on, tell me. The one who made plans and kissed first and ordered for the table because everybody else was taking too long. In relationships, people sometimes called me intense like it was a criticism, but they usually liked it until they didn’t. With Elliot, every instinct I had came with a warning label. Don’t crowd him. Don’t decide for him. Don’t turn his uncertainty into something you can manage. He rubbed his thumb over the chipped polish on his index finger. “Sometimes people call me pretty, and it feels… better than handsome. But then I get embarrassed that it feels better. And sometimes I look at myself and feel like I’m wearing a person costume, even when I’m wearing clothes I actually like.” “That sounds exhausting.” “Yeah.” He let out a breath. “It’s not all bad, though. Sometimes it feels kind of exciting. Like maybe there’s more room than I thought.” That landed somewhere deep in me. More room. I looked at him beneath the red light: the softness of his mouth, the deliberate darkness around his eyes, the elegance of his wrists. I had spent most of my adult life thinking my attraction had rules. I liked men who were unmistakably men, women who were unmistakably women, people who carried their identity like a clean line drawn in ink. But Elliot had never been a clean line. He was smoke, collage, scratched film. And I wanted to know him more every time he revealed another piece of himself. The realization should have scared me. Instead, it felt like stepping into cold water—sharp, shocking, impossible to ignore. “You don’t have to know right now,” I said. He looked down. “I know. Everyone says that.” “Yeah, because it’s true. But I get why it’s annoying.” That made him smile. “I mean it,” I continued, quieter. “You’re allowed to try things on. Words, clothes, pronouns, whatever. You don’t owe anyone a final answer just because they’d find it convenient.” His expression changed. Not entirely relief. Something more fragile than that. “What if people think I’m doing it for attention?” “Then they can die mad about it.” He laughed again, louder this time, covering his mouth with one hand. I felt a ridiculous rush of triumph. Then immediately hated myself for it. This wasn’t a performance I needed to win. He didn’t need me to save him with one good line. So I looked away, giving him space to decide what came next. For a while, we worked in silence. I adjusted the enlarger focus while he prepared a new sheet of paper. Our shoulders bumped once in the narrow space between stations. Neither of us moved away immediately. “Dani?” he said. “Yeah?” “Can I ask something kind of awful?” “Probably.” “Do you…” He paused. “Does it make you uncomfortable?” The question was so quiet I almost missed it. “What?” “Me talking about this. Or me being like this.” I turned toward him. “No,” I said. Then, because I wanted to be honest without handing him my confusion like it was his burden: “It makes me think. But not in a bad way.” “Think what?” I could have dodged. I was good at dodging when honesty got too close. But Elliot had just handed me something tender, and I didn’t want to answer him with a polished lie. “I think I’ve always acted like I knew exactly what I was attracted to,” I said. “Like I had a type and that meant something fixed about me. And then there’s you.” His face went very still. I felt heat rise up my neck, absurd beneath the red light. “I’m not trying to make your gender questioning about me.” “I know.” “I just mean…” I exhaled. “I’m attracted to you. I have been for a while. And I don’t know what label I’m supposed to put on that, or whether I need to put a label on it at all.” Elliot stared at me. Then he looked down at the tray and said, “Oh.” The word had so many meanings packed into it that I almost laughed. “Bad oh?” I asked. “No.” His mouth twitched. “Overwhelmed oh.” “Okay. That’s fair.” “I’ve been attracted to you too,” he said, barely above a whisper. Every part of me wanted to close the distance. To tilt his chin up. To make a decisive gesture and turn this strange, hovering feeling into something clear. Instead, I planted both palms on the counter. “Okay,” I said, voice rougher than I intended. “What do you want to do with that?” His eyes widened slightly. Good, I thought. Not because I wanted to startle him. Because I had asked. Because I had not assumed. “I don’t know,” he admitted. “That’s allowed.” “I might want to kiss you.” The room went quieter. “Might?” I asked gently. He made a helpless sound, half laugh, half groan. “I hate you.” “No, you don’t.” “No,” he said. “I really don’t.” Then he reached for the stack of photo paper beside the enlarger. At the exact same moment, I swung around to grab the timer. My elbow hit the edge of the box. The lid flipped open. White paper fanned across the floor in a bright, terrible scatter. For one second, neither of us moved. Then Elliot gasped. “Oh my god.” “Don’t panic.” “We just exposed, like, forty sheets of paper.” “Technically, the red light—” “Dani.” “Okay. We exposed forty sheets of paper.” He crouched down, hands in his hair, and I joined him. The paper lay everywhere, ruined by the brief intrusion of light from the hall when someone had opened the door upstairs—or maybe from the small gap under it. It didn’t matter. The clean, unused stack was now useless. Elliot stared at it, then started laughing. Not politely. Not quietly. He laughed until his shoulders shook. I tried to hold onto annoyance. We were broke art students. Paper was expensive. We had a deadline. Then I laughed too. We sat on the floor of the darkroom amid the sacrificed paper, red light washing over everything. Elliot’s knee pressed against mine. His cardigan sleeve brushed my arm. “This feels symbolic,” he said. “Don’t say that. We’re art students. We’ll make it symbolic.” “We could use it.” “Use ruined photo paper?” “For the project. Exposed identities. Accidental self-portraits. Something something failure of the archive.” I looked at him. He was smiling now, shy but bright. Like the laugh had loosened something in him. “That’s actually good,” I said. “I know,” he replied, mock-offended. “I’m quiet, not useless.” “I never thought you were useless.” His smile faded into something softer. The red light made it hard to tell where his blush began, but I could see it anyway. “Dani,” he said. “Yeah?” “I think I do want to kiss you.” My heart slammed once, hard. I did not move. “Do you want me to?” he asked. The question mattered. The fact that he asked mattered. The fact that he could ask, and that I could wait, mattered more than the answer. “Yes,” I said. “But only if you still want to after I say yes.” He nodded. Then he leaned in first. It was tentative at first, almost awkward—his mouth soft against mine, his breath catching when I touched his cheek. I kept my hand there, open and still, letting him choose the pressure, the duration, the shape of it. When he pulled back, he didn’t go far. “You okay?” I asked. He nodded again, smiling. “Yeah,” he said. “I think I am.” Around us, the ruined paper glowed faintly under the red light, blank sheets transformed by accident into evidence. For once, I didn’t feel the urge to rush toward an answer. I just sat beside Elliot in the dark, knee against his, and let the unfinished picture be beautiful.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical craft and emotional intelligence in handling a delicate subject matter. The author successfully creates an intimate, atmospheric setting in the darkroom that serves both practical and metaphorical purposes - the red light creating a confessional space where vulnerability can emerge naturally. The characterization is particularly well-executed. Dani's voice feels authentic and consistent throughout - her self-awareness about her typically assertive nature, combined with her conscious effort to give Elliot agency, creates compelling internal tension. The author skillfully shows rather than tells this conflict through her actions and restraint. Elliot's vulnerability and uncertainty are portrayed with genuine sensitivity, avoiding both condescension and over-dramatization. The dialogue feels natural and age-appropriate, with realistic hesitations, interruptions, and the kind of careful verbal dancing that occurs during intimate revelations. The banter between the characters establishes their dynamic effectively, and the more serious conversations maintain authenticity without becoming overly precious or therapeutic. The pacing is well-controlled, building tension through small moments and observations rather than manufactured drama. The accidental spilling of the photo paper serves as an effective pressure release that doesn't undermine the emotional weight of their conversation, and the author cleverly incorporates it into the artistic theme. The prose strikes a good balance between literary sophistication and accessibility. Descriptions are vivid without being overwrought, and the author demonstrates restraint in avoiding purple prose while maintaining atmospheric richness. The imagery consistently supports the themes of development, exposure, and emerging identity. The ending feels earned rather than artificially positive. The kiss occurs after proper emotional groundwork, and the piece concludes with appropriate uncertainty rather than false resolution. Both characters remain in process, which feels honest to their situations. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the metaphorical connections between photography and identity become slightly heavy-handed, and some descriptions border on being overly crafted. However, these don't significantly detract from the overall effectiveness of the piece. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Contemporary romance / drama: Stolen Moments in Roma — Score: 84.5
Prompt:
Contemporary Romance / Drama: Stolen Moments in Roma. Write a series of interconnected short scenes and dialogues that span a single day in modern-day Rome, in narrative form. This will form the beginning of a larger story. Alessandra, a fiery, independent Roman woman who works as a motorcycle courier, nearly collides with Ben, a somewhat clumsy, overly polite British academic on sabbatical, who is engrossed in a guidebook near the Trevi Fountain. The initial encounter is a clash of cultures and personalities – Alessandra is all sharp wit and rapid-fire Italian, while Ben is flustered apologies and awkward attempts at speaking the language. However, as they sort out the near-miss, a spark of unexpected humor and connection emerges. Alessandra, it quickly becomes clear, is a whirlwind of energy and mischief. She's a self-admitted troublemaker with a secret penchant for kleptomania, a detail she doesn't explicitly reveal, but which manifests in subtle, daring acts throughout the day. She impulsively decides to take Ben on a wild, unsanctioned tour of Rome, dragging him in some specific instances far outside his comfort zone. Ben, despite his initial apprehension and inherent politeness, finds himself utterly captivated by Alessandra's unapologetic spirit and zest for life. He is drawn into her orbit, a willing participant in increasingly outlandish situations, a quiet rebellion brewing beneath his scholarly exterior. The narrative should primarily consist of short, vivid scenes and dialogue exchanges, revealing their contrasting personalities and backgrounds, and the growing, ambiguous tension between them. Ben is captivated by Alessandra's energy and the thrill of breaking free from his routine. There's a clear sense that Alessandra is toying with Ben, pushing his boundaries and enjoying his reactions, but her own feelings remain somewhat enigmatic. The day should be a rollercoaster of exhilarating, slightly dangerous, and humorous moments, leaving Ben completely disoriented but utterly smitten. Explore the underlying tension & chemistry between them, culminating in some payoff as they part ways (or not -- your choice). Include this detail: Write in narrative prose, using immersive storytelling. 1000 words.
Model Output:
By eleven in the morning, Rome had already decided it was too hot for dignity. Tourists clustered around the Trevi Fountain in pale hats and sensible shoes, holding maps as if the city might otherwise escape them. Water crashed white and silver behind them. Scooters threaded impossible gaps in traffic. Somewhere, a waiter shouted about cappuccino. Ben Harrow stood in the middle of it all with his nose in a guidebook. “Excuse me—” The horn came first. Then the shriek of brakes. Ben looked up just in time to see a woman on a black motorcycle slide sideways around him, one boot skidding over the stone. The bike stopped so close to his knees that he could have counted the tiny chips in the red polish on her helmet. There was a bright, irregular scratch along the side of the motorcycle’s fuel tank, painted over in streaks of turquoise, yellow, and pink. It looked less like damage than a declaration. The rider lifted her visor. Her eyes were dark, furious, and—Ben would later think, with embarrassing frequency—magnificent. “Ma sei matto?” she snapped. “Are you insane?” “I’m terribly sorry,” Ben said at once. She blinked. “I was—well, I was reading.” “I can see that.” She pointed at the guidebook as if it had personally insulted her. “You read while standing in traffic?” “I didn’t realize it was traffic.” “It is Rome. Everything is traffic.” Ben looked around helplessly. “That does seem to be true.” The woman stared at him for a beat, then laughed despite herself. It was quick and sharp, like a match being struck. “You are English.” “British,” he said automatically, then regretted it. “But, yes. English.” “Of course.” She swung off the motorcycle in one fluid movement. Leather jacket, black jeans, scuffed boots. Her dark hair spilled from beneath the helmet in a thick, impatient wave. “Only an Englishman would apologize for almost becoming a decorative feature of a fountain.” “I did nearly ruin the composition.” “You would have improved it. More drama.” Ben gave a nervous laugh. She narrowed her eyes. “You understand Italian?” “Un poco.” “No. You understand shame in Italian. Different thing.” “I’m Ben.” “I did not ask.” “Right. Of course.” A smile tugged at one corner of her mouth. “Alessandra.” “Nice to meet you.” “It is not nice. You almost died.” “Nearly dying is an unusual way to meet someone, but I’ve had worse first impressions.” That made her laugh properly. Several tourists turned. Alessandra took his guidebook from his hands before he could protest. She flipped through it with theatrical contempt. “‘Hidden Rome for the Discerning Traveller.’” She made a disgusted sound. “Discerning traveller. You stand in the road. This book lies.” “I was trying to find the best place to throw a coin.” She glanced toward the fountain. “Over your left shoulder.” “Yes, I know. The book says—” “Then why are you reading it?” Ben opened his mouth, then closed it. Alessandra tucked the guidebook beneath her arm. “Come.” “Come where?” “Somewhere this book cannot find.” “I’m not sure that’s—” She had already put her helmet back on. “Get on.” Ben stared at the motorcycle. “I’m sorry?” “You heard me.” “I don’t have a helmet.” Alessandra reached into the small compartment beneath the seat and produced one, battered but intact. “You carry a spare helmet?” “I carry many things.” There was something in the way she said it that made him hesitate. Then she tilted her head, impatient. “Professor.” “I’m not—how did you know?” “You look like a professor. You are wearing linen in Rome and holding a book as if it is a weapon.” Ben took the helmet. “I’m on sabbatical,” he said. “Ah. A professor with no students to bore. Dangerous.” “I’m not dangerous.” Alessandra started the engine. “We will see.” --- Their first stop was not on any map Ben had seen. The motorcycle tore through streets too narrow for his sense of self-preservation. Ben held the grab rail behind him at first, rigid with terror. “Hold on properly!” Alessandra shouted over her shoulder. “I am!” “You are holding the bike like it owes you money!” “I don’t want to presume!” She laughed and took a corner hard enough that his careful restraint ended. His hands found her waist. Alessandra did not comment. Ben could feel her laughter through the leather of her jacket. They stopped at a market near Campo de’ Fiori. Alessandra parked in a space that was, Ben suspected, not technically a space at all. A fruit seller yelled at her from beneath a striped awning. “Alessandra! You park like a criminal!” “Only because you price peaches like a criminal!” she called back. She strode through the market with Ben trailing behind, blinking at towers of artichokes, figs split open like dark flowers, red peppers shining in the heat. “You come here every day?” Ben asked. “Sometimes.” She picked up a fig, inspected it, and put it back. “Sometimes I deliver documents to lawyers. Sometimes flowers to mistresses. Once, a box with a human tooth inside.” Ben stopped. “A tooth?” “Very small. Maybe a child. Maybe a politician.” “That seems a distinction worth clarifying.” Alessandra grinned. At a stall selling old postcards and cheap religious medals, she leaned over to speak to the vendor. Her hands moved constantly as she talked—quick, expressive, alive. When she turned away, Ben noticed a tiny silver Saint Christopher medal resting in her palm. He had not seen her buy it. She caught him looking. “What?” she asked. “Nothing.” “You have the face of a man who has discovered a footnote.” “I was only wondering whether you paid for that.” Alessandra’s eyes flashed. Then she slipped the medal into the pocket of his shirt. “For your journeys,” she said. Ben touched the pocket, startled. “That’s very kind.” “Do not make it sentimental.” “I wasn’t going to.” “You were already beginning.” --- At noon, she made him climb the Aventine Hill. “Why are we walking?” Ben asked, breathless behind her. “You have a motorcycle.” “Because you cannot arrive everywhere like a rich idiot.” “I’m neither rich nor—” “British idiot, then.” “That feels needlessly specific.” At the keyhole of the Priory of the Knights of Malta, Alessandra pushed him forward. “Look.” Ben bent toward the keyhole. On the other side lay a perfect, improbable view: a hedge-lined avenue, the dome of St. Peter’s floating at the end of it, Rome arranged in secret layers. He was silent for a long moment. Alessandra watched his profile rather than the view. “It’s beautiful,” he said quietly. “Yes.” “You knew it would be.” “Obviously.” He stood back. “How do you find places like this?” She shrugged. “You get lost enough, you begin to recognize the city when it hides.” Ben looked at her. “Do you get lost often?” Her expression changed almost imperceptibly, a door closing somewhere behind her eyes. “No,” she said. “I make other people lost.” Then she took his guidebook from beneath her arm and dropped it into a nearby rubbish bin. Ben stared. “My guidebook.” “It was making you stupid.” “It cost eighteen euros.” “Now it has a better life.” He walked to the bin, retrieved it, and dusted off the cover. Alessandra looked offended. “You rescued it?” “It contains several notes.” “You made notes?” “I’m an academic.” “You are ill.” Ben tucked the book under his arm. Then, after a pause, he tore out the page titled *The Essential Trevi Fountain Experience.* Alessandra’s brows rose. He folded it once, twice, and put it in his pocket. “Progress,” she said. “Don’t get used to it.” “Oh, I will.” --- By late afternoon, they were drinking cold beer on a rooftop terrace in Trastevere, though Ben had no idea how Alessandra had persuaded the waiter to let them up there. She had done it with a smile, a muttered sentence in Italian, and what looked suspiciously like a bottle of expensive olive oil that had not been in her bag when they arrived. Below them, laundry lifted between buildings. Church bells rolled across the warm air. Ben had loosened his collar. Alessandra had removed her jacket. Without it, she seemed somehow younger, though no less dangerous. “So,” she said, feet on the low wall, “Professor Ben. What do you study?” “Renaissance political philosophy.” She groaned. “I knew it.” “You asked.” “I hoped you would say something glamorous. Volcanoes. Murder. Ancient curses.” “Political thought can be quite dramatic.” “Can it?” “Absolutely. Betrayal, power, exile, ambitious men making terrible decisions.” Alessandra took a drink. “So, Rome.” “Precisely.” She looked at him then, a little more seriously. “And you came here to study dead men?” “I came here to finish a book.” “Is it finished?” “Not remotely.” “Why?” Ben considered the bottle in his hands. “I keep trying to write it correctly.” “That is a stupid reason.” “It’s a common academic affliction.” “No.” She tapped her finger against the table. “You are afraid that if it is finished, people can say it is bad.” He looked up. Alessandra gave him a thin smile. “See? I am also a professor.” “You’re very perceptive.” “I am very bored by liars.” “I wasn’t lying.” “Not to me.” She leaned closer. “Mostly to yourself.” For once, Ben had no apology ready. Then Alessandra reached across the table and stole the olive from his drink garnish. “Hey.” “You were thinking too much. I saved you.” “That was my olive.” “You British have an empire. You can survive one olive.” Ben laughed, and the sound surprised him. It came out freer than he expected. Alessandra studied him as if she had caused it deliberately. Perhaps she had. --- At dusk, she drove him to the Tiber. They parked near Isola Tiberina, where the river moved darkly beneath the bridges. The air had softened. Rome glowed gold at the edges. Alessandra stood beside the motorcycle, helmet tucked beneath one arm. “Well,” Ben said. “I suppose this is where I thank you for not killing me.” “You are welcome.” “And for the tour.” “It was not a tour.” “No?” “A tour has tickets. And facts.” “You gave me some facts.” “I gave you lies with confidence. Different thing.” Ben smiled. For a moment, neither of them spoke. The city swelled around them: engines, voices, a burst of music from somewhere across the water. Ben touched the Saint Christopher medal in his pocket. “Will I see you again?” he asked. Alessandra’s expression became unreadable. “Do you want to?” “Yes,” he said, too quickly. She looked pleased by that. Not softened, exactly. But pleased. “Then you must stop standing in the road.” “I can work on that.” “And no more guidebooks.” “I’m keeping the guidebook.” “Coward.” “Scholar.” “Same thing.” She stepped closer. Ben could smell sun-warmed leather, beer, and something citrusy in her hair. She lifted a hand to his face, and for one astonishing second he thought she might kiss him. Instead, she plucked his wallet from the inside pocket of his jacket. Ben blinked. Alessandra held it up between two fingers. “You should be more careful,” she said. “I—how did you—?” She opened it, removed a business card, and read aloud. “Dr. Benjamin Harrow. Visiting Fellow.” Then she slipped the wallet back into his jacket, slower this time. Her knuckles brushed his chest. “I know where to find you now, Professor.” Before he could answer, she pulled on her helmet and climbed onto the motorcycle. “Alessandra—” She lowered the visor. “Throw your coin,” she called. “But do not wish for anything boring.” The engine roared. The colorful scratch along her bike flashed beneath a streetlamp, bright as a wound, bright as a warning. Then she disappeared into the Roman traffic. Ben stood by the river with his wallet, his rescued guidebook, and a silver medal in his pocket. For the first time since arriving in Rome, he had absolutely no idea what he was supposed to do next. He found, to his surprise, that he did not mind at all.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and creative flair in crafting a contemporary romance set in Rome. The author successfully creates two distinct, compelling characters whose personalities emerge naturally through dialogue and action rather than exposition. Alessandra's fiery independence and subtle kleptomania are woven seamlessly into the narrative, while Ben's academic politeness and growing fascination feel authentic and earned. The dialogue sparkles with wit and cultural tension, particularly in exchanges like Alessandra's observation that Ben understands "shame in Italian" rather than the language itself. The banter feels natural and reveals character depth while advancing their relationship. The author skillfully balances humor with romantic tension, avoiding both saccharine sweetness and forced conflict. The Roman setting is vividly rendered through sensory details and specific locations, from the Trevi Fountain's "white and silver" water to the keyhole view of St. Peter's dome. The city becomes almost a third character, with Alessandra serving as both guide and embodiment of Rome's chaotic energy. The structure works well, using interconnected scenes that build momentum while allowing for character development. The pacing feels natural, moving from their collision through various adventures to the riverside conclusion. The author demonstrates restraint in not forcing a romantic resolution, instead ending on a note of possibility that feels more realistic and intriguing. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the prose becomes slightly self-conscious, particularly in some metaphorical descriptions. The kleptomania subplot, while well-integrated, could have been developed with slightly more subtlety in places. However, these are minor issues in an otherwise accomplished piece that successfully balances romance, humor, and character development within the constraints of a single day's narrative. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Cozy sci-fi: The Last Outpost — Score: 82.8
Prompt:
Cozy sci-fi The Last Outpost Nettle (or Nets to her friends) bartends at The Last Outpost -- a lazy hub for miners and merchants in an asteroid belt on the Andromeda outer rim. Escaping from her past life, Nets found a home here at the Outpost. They have, thus far, tolerated Net's flaws which she tries to keep in equilibrium with her assortment of talents. Her evenings involve chatting with patrons, negotiating the drama, intrigue, problems & love lives of the diverse crowd that filter through the Outpost. Tonight is no different, at least, until a frazzled looking stranger, tentacled dreads askew, quietly plops down at the bar: "Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double." Write a chapter in this story. Establish conflict for narrative interest. Don't resolve the conflict within this chapter. Aim for about 60% dialogue. First person, present tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The Last Outpost has two rules posted behind the bar. Rule one: Pay before you drift. Rule two: No weapons discharged inside. There used to be a third—No singing after midnight—but that one got removed when Captain Venn threatened to take his ore-hauler business to Ceres Station. We haven’t heard him sing since, which suggests the threat worked in both directions. Tonight, the Outpost is in its usual orbit of chaos. Miners in dust-streaked coveralls crowd the long tables beneath strings of warm amber bulbs. A pair of slick-haired merchants argue over cargo insurance in a booth that hasn’t been cleaned since the last solar flare. Miki, our resident maintenance tech and part-time gambler, is trying to teach a three-armed surveyor how to shuffle cards with only two hands. The air smells like fried kelp, metal dust, old upholstery, and the sweet spice of Vesta rum. Home, in other words. “Another one, Nets,” calls Jory from the end of the bar. I glance at his empty glass. “You are a medical cautionary tale.” “I’m celebrating.” “You said that last time.” “Last time I was celebrating surviving a cave-in.” “And this time?” He smiles, showing a gold tooth. “I’m celebrating not causing one.” “Growth.” I take his glass anyway. “What’s your poison?” “You know what it is.” “I know you should drink water.” “Water’s what killed my grandfather.” “Drowning?” “Fish tank accident.” I stare at him. He shrugs. “A large fish tank.” Behind him, someone drops a glass. It shatters sharp and bright over the background hum of conversation. Every head turns for half a second. The culprit is Talla, one of the cargo runners, frozen beside table six with both hands lifted as though the glass has betrayed her personally. “Sorry!” she calls. “No damage deposit,” I say. “It was an accident!” “That’s what the damage deposit is for.” A few people laugh. Talla sticks her tongue out at me and kneels to gather the larger pieces. Miki swoops in with a magnetic broom before she can cut herself. I pour Jory’s drink. “You’re cut off after this.” “You say that like I’m not charming enough to change your mind.” “You’re charming enough to make me consider charging extra.” The front hatch hisses open. Cold belt air drifts in first, carrying the sterile mineral scent of the dock. Then the stranger steps through. They’re tall, though it’s hard to judge in a place where half our customers have different skeletal arrangements. Their coat is dark, travel-stained, and too heavy for someone who’s only been moving between local rocks. A cluster of tentacled dreads spills over their shoulders, usually arranged in elaborate braids if the silver clasps woven through them are any indication. Tonight they hang loose and askew. Their eyes sweep the room once. Not curious. Counting exits. I’ve seen that look. I see it in the mirror every morning when I think too hard about the person I used to be. The stranger chooses the stool nearest the far end of the bar, away from the windows and with a clear view of both doors. They sit carefully, as if they’re holding themselves together by contract. “Vega whisky, neat,” they say. Their voice is low and rough, their accent clipping the edges off the words. They hesitate, then add, “Better make that a double.” I reach for the good bottle. “We have Vega whisky,” I say. “We also have Vega-adjacent whisky, which is less expensive and only mildly dangerous.” “Good bottle.” “That’ll cost you.” They put a cred chip on the bar. It’s black. Not black plastic. Not black metal. Black like polished obsidian, with a thin gold ring spinning around its edge. The kind of credit chip nobody in the outer rim carries unless they have powerful friends, powerful enemies, or both. I don’t touch it right away. “Tab name?” I ask. The stranger looks at me. “Nobody comes in here nameless,” I say lightly. “Not unless they plan to leave that way.” A pause. “Rill.” “Rill,” I repeat. “That a first name, last name, title, warning label?” “It’s enough.” “Fine. Rill gets a double.” I set two fingers against the chip. The register flashes a number that makes my eyebrows rise despite myself. Enough for about eighty doubles and a replacement window. I pour the whisky into a heavy-bottomed glass and slide it over. Rill catches it before it travels two centimeters. Fast reflexes. They drink half in one swallow. Jory watches from his stool, openly interested. Jory is a miner, which means he has no survival instincts except the kind that apply underground. “Rough shift?” he asks. Rill doesn’t look at him. “Jory,” I say. “What? I’m being friendly.” “You’re being a conversation-shaped inconvenience.” “People like me.” “Certain fungi like you.” Rill’s mouth twitches, barely. Then the expression disappears. They lean closer to the bar. “You own this place?” “Absolutely not. I merely run it, repair it, clean up after it, and occasionally threaten to eject people through the cargo hatch.” “So you know the owner.” “I know everybody who matters.” “That is a dangerous claim in a room this full.” “It’s a bartender’s claim.” Rill studies me again. “I need to speak with the owner.” “You and half the belt. What’s it about?” “Private business.” “Private business is how public shootouts happen.” “I am not here to cause trouble.” From the corner table, Captain Venn begins singing. “No trouble,” I say, “is an increasingly subjective term.” Rill turns their glass slowly between long fingers. Their skin has a faint blue-green cast under the bar lights, deepening at the knuckles and along the ridges of their throat. “The owner received a message three days ago,” they say. “A request.” “What kind of request?” “About a shipment.” “Merchants do shipments. We do drinks and regrettable life advice.” “This shipment concerns the Outpost.” I keep polishing the same clean section of bar. “Does it concern the Outpost in a way that needs two whisky doubles?” “Yes.” “Does it concern the Outpost in a way that needs you staring at the door every twelve seconds?” Their eyes flick toward the hatch. “Also yes.” The hatch hisses. Rill goes still. Not startled. Not frightened. Prepared. Three people walk in. The first is a human woman in a pale gray station uniform with no insignia. The second is a broad-shouldered Draxian wearing a tailored coat that probably costs more than the building. The third is small, round, and entirely hidden inside an environmental suit with a mirrored visor. They don’t look around. They look directly at Rill. Jory mutters, “Oh, good. More friends.” I set down the polishing cloth. The woman approaches the bar with the calm gait of someone who expects doors to open before she reaches them. “Good evening,” she says. “Depends,” I say. “You ordering?” Her smile is technically present but emotionally unavailable. “We’re looking for someone.” “Try the lost-and-found. We’ve got six gloves, a molar, and one suspiciously affectionate drone.” The Draxian’s eyes narrow. The woman places both hands on the bar. “A passenger arrived on Dock Four approximately eleven minutes ago.” “Lots of passengers arrive on Dock Four. That’s why it’s called a dock.” “Nets,” Jory says softly. I don’t like his tone. The woman knows my name. I don’t remember introducing myself. “I’m afraid this is official business,” she says. “That phrase has never improved an evening.” “We represent the Helix Transit Authority.” A few nearby conversations quiet. The Helix Transit Authority doesn’t come this far out unless something has gone catastrophically wrong or expensively missing. Rill’s hand closes around their whisky glass. The mirrored figure speaks, their voice tinny through the suit speaker. “Rill Sava,” they say. “You are requested to return with us.” Rill says nothing. The woman glances at me. “This individual is involved in an active investigation.” “Investigation into what?” I ask. “Transit security.” “Very broad category.” “Broad enough.” I lean an elbow on the counter. “You have a warrant?” The Draxian gives a low, humorless laugh. The woman’s smile fades by one degree. “This is an outer-rim facility.” “This is a bar.” “This is a registered station support establishment.” “Still a bar.” Rill finally speaks. “They don’t have a warrant.” The woman’s gaze cuts to them. “You are in no position to make legal distinctions.” “Oh, I think I’m in exactly the position.” “What did you do?” Jory asks, because Jory has chosen death as a lifestyle. Rill’s tentacled dreads tighten around their shoulders, each one curling in agitation. “I stole something,” they say. The room gets quieter. I look at the black cred chip on the counter. “Something valuable?” “Yes.” The small suited figure steps closer. “Something dangerous.” “That’s a matter of interpretation,” Rill says. “Not when it can alter navigational records for an entire sector.” A chill crawls over my arms. The asteroid belt survives on routes. Supply routes, mining routes, emergency routes. A bad navigation map can mean a freighter becomes debris before anyone knows it missed its turn. I look at Rill. They look back. “I didn’t alter anything,” they say. “I found proof someone else did.” The woman in gray says, “They are lying.” Rill’s face hardens. “Of course you’d say that.” “What proof?” I ask. “Nets,” Miki warns from the far end of the bar. I hear it too. A low vibration through the floor. Not engines. Not dock activity. The kind of sound you feel in your teeth. The woman’s eyes shift toward the ceiling. The Draxian reaches inside his coat. Every instinct I own comes awake at once. I slam my palm on the bar. “No weapons discharged inside,” I say. The Draxian pauses. Behind him, the front hatch cycles again. This time, nobody enters. Instead, the emergency shutters begin to descend over the windows. Rill stands so fast their stool skids backward. “They found me,” they whisper. “Who?” I ask. Their eyes meet mine, wide now. “Not them.”
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt requirements, delivering a cozy sci-fi atmosphere with approximately 60% dialogue in first-person present tense. The author establishes Nettle as a compelling bartender protagonist with a mysterious past, creating an authentic voice that balances wit and wariness. The worldbuilding feels lived-in and natural, from the posted bar rules to the diverse clientele of miners and merchants. The dialogue is particularly strong, with distinct voices for each character. Nettle's banter with Jory establishes their relationship efficiently while revealing character traits. The exchanges feel natural and serve multiple purposes - advancing plot, revealing character, and building atmosphere. The author avoids exposition dumps, instead weaving background information into conversations organically. Character actions remain believable throughout. Rill's cautious behavior, choice of seating, and reactions align with someone fleeing danger. Nettle's protective instincts toward her establishment and measured responses to authority figures feel authentic to her role and implied background. The supporting characters, while briefly sketched, have distinct personalities. The pacing builds tension effectively without rushing. The author establishes normalcy first, introduces the mysterious stranger, then escalates with the arrival of authority figures and hints of larger conspiracy. The conflict setup is sophisticated - not just "bad guys chase good guy" but involving navigation data that could endanger the entire asteroid belt community. The prose strikes an appropriate balance between descriptive and functional. Sensory details like "fried kelp, metal dust, old upholstery, and the sweet spice of Vesta rum" create atmosphere without overwhelming the narrative flow. The writing maintains the cozy sci-fi tone while building genuine tension. However, some elements feel slightly familiar - the mysterious stranger with a dark past, corrupt authority figures, and stolen data MacGuffin are well-worn tropes. While executed competently, the setup doesn't offer significant surprises. The ending, while appropriately unresolved per instructions, relies on the somewhat predictable "bigger threat arrives" escalation. The emotional complexity remains moderate. While Nettle shows depth through her protective instincts and implied past, the piece focuses more on plot mechanics than deeper character exploration. The relationships, while believable, don't reach profound emotional territory within this chapter's scope. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Emotionally Complex: 13 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Young adult drama: Gone South — Score: 82.0
Prompt:
Young adult drama Gone South Taylor is a 16 year old teen with a history of "concerning behavioural patterns" (or that's how the stupid smug school counsellor worded it on the phone call to Mom). It shouldn't be a surprise: When you live in a backwater town in Tennessee, your choices are: a. be mind-fuckingly bored, or b. invent some drama. Two days ago, Taylor came out to their family as trans. This disclosure was *not* in the category of invented drama -- it was, in fact, the real deal, and a long time coming. It didn't go well. The last 48 hours had been pure disaster. In a moment of desperate inspiration, Taylor had decided to get the hell out of dodge. And so an escape plan was hatched and executed within the space of 30 minutes. That was last night. Now Taylor was two states away, having been unceremoniously ejected from their hitched ride in downtown New Orleans. Write the next chapter in Taylor's story as they find their feet in this new environment. Tell the chapter over the course of their first day in New Orleans, in which Taylor meets another runaway with issues and secrets. Taylor navigates this new world, not knowing who to trust or what the dangers are. Write in Taylor's voice, and incorporate their internal monologue as they grapple with intense & conflicting emotions. Also incorporate dialogue with the interesting characters Taylor meets. First person, mixed tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The guy who’d picked me up outside of Jackson had a truck that smelled like wet dog, cigarette ash, and something sweetly rotten under the passenger seat. He’d spent the first hour talking at me about his divorce and the second hour asking questions I didn’t answer. Where you headed? Got family down there? You got a boyfriend waiting? By the time we reached New Orleans, I’d been so tired I almost forgot to be scared. Almost. Then he pulled over beside a curb somewhere downtown, looked at me like he’d just remembered I existed, and said, “This is as far as I’m going.” I blinked at him. Rain shone on the streets, turning every neon sign into a smear of color. It was still dark out, but the city somehow looked awake already. There were people everywhere: stumbling, laughing, arguing, dragging suitcases behind them, holding food in greasy paper wrappers. “What?” I asked. “Can’t take you farther.” He was gripping the steering wheel too hard. “Got places to be.” “You said you were going to the Gulf.” “I said I was headed south.” “This is south.” He sighed the way Mom used to sigh when I left dishes in the sink. Like I was an inconvenience that had learned to speak. “Kid, get out.” The word hit wrong. Kid. Not Taylor. Not anything close to me. I grabbed my backpack from the floorboard, one strap half torn where it had caught on the fence behind our house last night. “Fine.” “Listen,” he said, as I reached for the door. “You should call somebody.” I laughed once. It came out ugly. “Great advice.” Then I got out. The truck sped away before I’d even shut the door all the way. For a minute, I stood on the sidewalk with my backpack hanging off one shoulder, watching red taillights disappear into the wet black street. I had eighty-three dollars, a dead phone charger that only worked if you bent the cord exactly right, two granola bars, a hoodie, and no plan worth calling a plan. I was free. I was completely, spectacularly screwed. The first thing I noticed about New Orleans was that it didn’t care. Back home, if somebody stood on the corner looking lost, everyone noticed. Someone’s aunt would see you. Somebody from school would drive by. By noon, your business would be moving through the town faster than a tornado warning. Here, I could have been invisible. A street musician’s saxophone played somewhere ahead of me as I walked down a crowded street. The notes drifted through the damp air, low and aching, then rose sharp and bright over the noise of traffic and people. It sounded like somebody had taken every bad feeling in the world and taught it how to dance. I followed it because I didn’t know where else to go. The street narrowed into something old and crowded, balconies leaning over the sidewalk like they were listening in. Iron railings. Shuttered windows. Beads glittering in gutters. The air smelled like rain, fried food, garbage, flowers, beer, and something spicy enough to make my eyes water. I passed a shop with masks in the window—gold, purple, green, feathers sticking out of their foreheads. They looked like they knew secrets. “Hey, Tennessee.” I stopped. Nobody knew I was from Tennessee. A girl sat cross-legged against the brick wall beside the saxophone player’s open case. She couldn’t have been much older than me, maybe seventeen or eighteen. She had dark curls shoved under a red bandanna, a denim jacket patched at both elbows, and one combat boot held together with silver duct tape. There was a bruise blooming yellow-green along her jaw. She was looking directly at me. “What?” I said. “You’ve got Tennessee on your shirt.” I looked down. My faded gray T-shirt said GATLINBURG SMOKY MOUNTAINS in cracked blue letters. “Oh.” I tugged the hoodie closed over it. “Right.” The girl’s mouth tilted. “Subtle.” “Sorry I didn’t pack for a dramatic reinvention.” That made her laugh, quick and surprised. “Okay. Fair.” The saxophone player kept playing. He was old, maybe sixty, with a white beard and a black hat pulled low over his eyes. He didn’t look at either of us. His music curled through the morning like smoke. The girl patted the ground next to her. “You look like you’re about to fall over.” “I’m fine.” “Sure.” I hated that word. Sure. Everybody said it when they meant the opposite. But my legs were shaking, and sitting down felt less embarrassing than collapsing in the middle of the sidewalk, so I lowered myself beside her. The brick wall was cool through my hoodie. “I’m Jules,” she said. “Taylor.” “Taylor what?” “Just Taylor.” She nodded like that was an answer she respected. “Just Jules, then.” For a few seconds we listened to the saxophone. A couple in matching plastic ponchos stopped to toss bills into the case. The musician nodded without breaking the song. Jules glanced at my backpack. “First day?” “Is it that obvious?” “You’re carrying it like it’s got your entire life in it.” My hand tightened around the strap. It did. Not literally. Most of my life was still in Tennessee: my bedroom with the chipped purple nail polish under the bed, the closet full of clothes that never felt right, the family pictures in the hallway where I looked like somebody’s miserable little son. But enough of it was in the backpack. My notebook. My toothbrush. A photograph of my little sister, Emma, at the county fair, missing her front teeth and screaming with laughter on the tilt-a-whirl. The cash I’d taken from the jar in Mom’s kitchen, which I felt guilty about every time I thought about it. “Maybe,” I said. Jules didn’t press. That was the first thing I liked about her. The second was that she had a paper bag beside her, and after a minute, she pulled out a still-warm biscuit wrapped in a napkin and held it toward me. “I’m not taking your food,” I said. “You can owe me.” “I don’t know how much a biscuit costs here.” “It’s not about the biscuit.” That sounded like a trick, but my stomach made a noise loud enough that Jules raised her eyebrows. “Fine,” I muttered, taking it. The biscuit tasted like butter and salt and the kind of comfort I did not deserve. I ate too fast, then pretended I hadn’t. “So,” Jules said, picking at a loose thread on her sleeve. “You got somewhere to sleep tonight?” The question went straight through me. “I’ll figure it out.” “That means no.” “That means I’ll figure it out.” She studied me. Her eyes were pale brown, almost gold in the gray morning light. Tired eyes. Not sad exactly. Alert. “Don’t sleep in the Quarter,” she said. “The what?” “This area. Too many drunk tourists, too many cops, too many people who think a kid alone is an opportunity.” My skin prickled. “I’m not a kid.” “Okay,” she said, not mocking me. “Don’t sleep here anyway.” The saxophone song ended. For the first time, the old musician looked up. His gaze landed on me, then Jules, then my backpack. “You two keep your heads down,” he said. His voice was softer than I expected. Jules stood, dropping a few coins into his case. “Yes, sir.” He nodded at me. “Name?” I hesitated. “Taylor,” Jules said for me. The man tipped his hat. “Taylor. City’ll give you what you ask for if you aren’t careful. Sometimes that’s the problem.” Then he started another song. I watched his fingers move over the brass keys. “What does that even mean?” Jules slung her bag over her shoulder. “Means people here sell miracles with strings attached.” “That’s not ominous at all.” “Welcome to New Orleans.” She started walking. After half a second, I followed. Maybe because I had nowhere else to go. Maybe because she’d given me a biscuit. Maybe because being alone felt like standing on the edge of a roof, and Jules—bruised jaw, duct-taped boot, secrets in her eyes—was at least someone standing close enough to grab onto. We moved through the crowd together. “You run away?” she asked. I looked at her. She shrugged. “You don’t have to answer. I did.” “Recently?” “Depends what you mean by recently.” “That sounds like a yes.” “Probably.” I wanted to ask from where. From who. What happened to her face. But I could feel the questions between us like broken glass. We both knew better than to pick them up barehanded. Instead, I said, “I left because my family doesn’t… get it.” Jules glanced sideways at me. I swallowed. The words had gotten me in trouble every time I’d tried to say them, but I was tired of carrying them alone. “I’m trans,” I said. “And they freaked out.” There. Out in the wet morning air, surrounded by strangers and brass music and a city that didn’t care. The truth looked smaller than I expected. Still dangerous. But smaller. Jules was quiet for a moment. Then she said, “Okay.” “Okay?” “Yeah. Okay.” She kicked at a puddle with her taped-up boot. “You’re Taylor. You’re trans. You’re hungry. You don’t have a place to sleep. Those are all separate problems, and only one of them is anybody else’s business.” Something in my chest cracked open. I looked away fast, pretending to watch a horse carriage clop past. “Thanks,” I said. “Don’t thank me yet.” Her voice changed. Sharper. “You need to understand something. People will offer help. Some of them mean it. Some of them don’t. Anybody asks to hold your money, says they’ve got a room for you, says you owe them because they fed you—walk away.” I thought of the man in the truck asking if I had a boyfriend. “I know.” “No,” Jules said. “You don’t. Not yet.” That stung because it was true. She led me toward a little café where the smell of coffee poured out every time the door opened. Across the street, a church had opened its side entrance and a line of people stood beneath an awning, waiting for breakfast. “Food line,” Jules said. “Then I’ll show you where you can charge your phone. After that, there’s a youth place a few blocks over. They might ask questions.” “I don’t want cops.” “They’re not cops.” “Everybody says that until cops show up.” “Fair.” She looked at me again, expression unreadable. “But you can’t do this on eighty-three dollars and granola bars, Taylor.” I hated her for being right. I hated my mom for making me leave. I hated Dad for saying, You’re confused, like my whole self was just a bad grade I could bring up with extra credit. I hated Emma most of all for crying when I left, even though she was eight and had no idea what was happening. And underneath all of it, buried deep enough to scare me, I missed them. I missed home. Not the place. Not really. I missed the version of home I’d spent sixteen years hoping it would become. Jules nudged my shoulder. “You coming?” The line moved forward. I adjusted my backpack and followed her beneath the awning, into the noise and heat and danger of a city I knew nothing about. Above us, the rain finally stopped. For one thin second, sunlight broke through the clouds and flashed gold against the wet pavement. I didn’t know if it was a sign. I didn’t know if I believed in signs anymore. But I kept walking.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and emotional authenticity in portraying a transgender teen runaway's first day in New Orleans. The writing successfully captures Taylor's voice through a mix of vulnerability, defensiveness, and cautious hope that feels genuine for a 16-year-old in crisis. The characterization is particularly strong. Taylor's internal monologue reveals layers of conflicting emotions - missing family while resenting them, craving independence while feeling overwhelmed, wanting trust while maintaining necessary wariness. Jules emerges as a compelling secondary character with her own implied backstory, avoiding the trap of being merely a plot device. The dialogue feels natural and age-appropriate, with realistic speech patterns and the kind of careful verbal dancing that occurs between two guarded young people. The New Orleans setting is vividly rendered through sensory details - the saxophone music, the smells of the Quarter, the visual textures of the old architecture. The author effectively uses the city as both backdrop and character, emphasizing its indifference and potential dangers while hinting at possibilities for reinvention. The prose style successfully balances literary quality with accessibility. Sentences flow naturally with good rhythm and pacing. The mixed tense approach works well, creating immediacy in dialogue and action while allowing for reflective passages. The writing avoids purple prose while maintaining evocative imagery. However, there are some minor weaknesses. Certain moments edge toward predictability - the helpful street kid with secrets, the ominous warnings about the city's dangers. Some descriptions, while atmospheric, occasionally feel slightly overwrought. The piece also relies somewhat heavily on exposition through dialogue, though this is handled more skillfully than in amateur work. The emotional complexity is well-handled, avoiding both excessive darkness and unearned optimism. The ending strikes an appropriate note of cautious forward movement without resolving Taylor's fundamental problems too easily. The piece successfully maintains tension between hope and uncertainty. The writing demonstrates strong craft in character development, dialogue, and atmospheric description while telling a compelling story that adheres closely to the prompt requirements. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 5 Overwrought: 6 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16
