+ Epistolary: The Lighthouse Keeper — Score: 85.9
Prompt:
The Lighthouse Keeper Epistolary Narrative: Write a story told entirely through a series of brief correspondences: telegrams or letters or emails between two characters. The correspondence should span several months or years, and reveal a gradually unfolding plot. Use distinct voices for each character, and include details that provide insight into their personalities and motivations. The story should build to an emotional climax, and the final letter should provide a satisfying resolution. The setting is a lighthouse keeper writing to his mother. He is working class and Scottish. He is struggling with the isolation of his posting. Write naturally and without cliches. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**15 March 1987** **North Rona Lighthouse** Dear Mam, I got here Tuesday after a crossing that near finished me. The relief boat put me ashore at Acarsaid Mòr, though “ashore” is generous. It was a wet black scrape of rock and a winch basket swinging like a kettle over the sea. The station is sound enough. White tower, red gallery, two cottages joined by a passage, generator shed, foghorn house. The old men have left me lists for everything: polish brass, log weather, check oil pressure, clean lenses, test radio at nine and six. You would laugh at the stores. Enough tea to float Glasgow. Tins of peaches from 1984. A sack of potatoes with more eyes than me. The light is finer than I expected. When it turns, the whole room fills and empties, fills and empties. You can see the headland at Cape Wrath on a clear evening, only a dark tooth on the horizon. Don’t fret. It is only six months, and the overtime is good. Your son, Ewan --- **22 March 1987** **Mary MacLeod, Govan** Dear Ewan, I got your letter Thursday and read it twice before I made my tea. Your Auntie Mairi says North Rona is “a bit exposed,” which is her way of saying she thinks you have lost your senses. Six months will pass. You used to say that about school holidays, then Christmas, then your apprenticeship. You always did better when there was a job needing done. I am glad you have tea. Eat the peaches before they become archaeological. Mr. Devlin from downstairs has fixed the loose window in the close, so you needn’t send money for that. I’m managing. The buses are dearer again, naturally. Write often. Not because I’m worried. Because I like hearing what you see. Love, Mam --- **4 April 1987** **North Rona Lighthouse** Mam, The sea has been up for five days. Not “rough,” not “a bit wild.” Up. It comes over the lower path and strikes the foghorn house hard enough to rattle cups in the kitchen. The radio aerial went loose yesterday. I watched it lash about from the window and thought of fixing it, then remembered I am not daft. The supply boat was due Friday but turned back from Sula Sgeir, so I am on tinned mince and tea. There are sheep here, feral things with grey faces, and rabbits that sit under the old church wall. There is an old village too—roofless cottages, stone pens, a graveyard with names worn away. I walked there in the lull this morning. It felt wrong, reading the dead when there was nobody to ask permission of. You asked what I see. Mostly water. Every kind of water. Green, iron, white, black. At night the beam catches rain and makes it look like strings being pulled from heaven to sea. Sorry. That sounds soft. Tell Auntie Mairi I have not lost my senses. They are all accounted for, though one of them is bored. Ewan --- **18 April 1987** **Govan** Dear Ewan, Soft is allowed. You were always one for pretending you had no feelings, even when you cried because your goldfish died. You buried it in the biscuit tin garden and made me say a prayer. I went to the doctor about the breathlessness. He says it is my chest, not my heart, and has given me tablets that make my mouth dry. Nothing dramatic, so don’t start making plans to come home. Your cousin Neil is looking for work again. He says he’ll go offshore if nothing turns up. Men will go anywhere so long as they can say they are not running away. I found your father’s old wool jumper in the airing cupboard. The dark blue one with the elbow patches. I washed it. If you want it, I’ll send it on the next boat. Write soon. Mam --- **2 May 1987** **North Rona Lighthouse** Mam, Send the jumper. The wind gets through the new issue ones as if they were made of paper. I’m sorry about your chest. You say not to make plans, but you don’t tell me things unless there is something to tell. Take the tablets. Go back if they don’t help. I mean it. There was a trawler near the island last week, too close in. I called them on the radio and got nothing but crackle. Then a man answered, English accent, told me not to fuss. At midnight their lights vanished behind the reef. I watched until dawn expecting a flare. None came. Perhaps they knew the waters better than I thought. There are days I speak aloud just to hear a voice. Usually to the generator. “Come on, you bastard,” I say, and it behaves, so perhaps it likes company too. The light failed for eleven seconds on Tuesday during a relay change. Eleven seconds is written in the log as “brief interruption.” Eleven seconds feels longer when you know there may be somebody out there taking a bearing. Your loving son, Ewan --- **16 May 1987** **Govan** Ewan, Your jumper has gone on the boat with a parcel of shortbread, tea, and the oatcakes you like. I put in a photograph as well. Don’t complain; it’s a good one. You were twelve, at Largs, standing in front of the paddle steamer with chips all down your coat. The doctor has changed the tablets. That is all. I know what you mean about keeping the light. Your father felt that way about the cranes at the yard. Said if his bit was late, somebody else’s bit was late, then the whole job limped. He never admitted he was proud of anything, but he was proud of doing his shift right. You are like him in that. More than you know. Mam --- **1 June 1987** **North Rona Lighthouse** Mam, I got the parcel. The shortbread was smashed but still tasted right. The photograph is on the shelf beside the barometer. You look younger in it than I remember you ever being. That’s not a complaint. I just mean I forgot you had a life before you were my mam. The summer birds are here. Gannets passing in lines, fulmars nesting in every crack. There is one puffin that keeps coming to the path by the engine room. He stands looking at me as if I owe him rent. I had a bad night last Friday. Thick fog, horn going every thirty seconds, nothing beyond the glass but the sound of it. I could not sleep after my watch. I kept thinking I heard someone in the passage. I checked twice. Nobody. I know that sounds foolish. Don’t tell Auntie Mairi. Ewan --- **12 June 1987** **Govan** My dear boy, I will not tell Mairi anything. She has enough of her own to be getting on with. You do not need to apologise for being frightened. A place can get inside your head if it is quiet enough. When your father was on nights, I used to hear every pipe in this flat. I thought the whole building was breathing around me. I have been back at the hospital. It is not just my chest. They are doing tests. Before you make a grand gesture, listen: there is nothing you can do from here except write to me, and I want you to do that. The photograph is from the year before your father got laid off. We had a good day. It is allowed to remember good days, even if bad ones came after. Love, Mam --- **28 June 1987** **North Rona Lighthouse** Mam, I have written this three times and torn it up twice. I can come off early. MacFarlane said the Board could send a relief man if there was family trouble. I told him nothing yet because I don’t know what there is to tell. But I can come. Please say the word. The sea has been flat for two days. Flat as slate. From the gallery I can see the outline of Lewis in the evening and, north-east, the low hump of Sula Sgeir. It looks close, though it is not. Everything looks closer from a height. I am trying not to be angry that you kept this from me. I know why. Still. Ewan --- **5 July 1987** **Govan** Ewan, I am sorry. I did not keep it from you to be brave. I kept it because I wanted, for once, not to be the thing you had to worry about. It is cancer. The doctor says treatment may give time, but he does not make promises. I am taking the treatment. I am not lying in bed waiting for the ceiling to fall. Do not leave your post yet. That may sound cruel, but listen to me. You have spent years taking any shift offered, any extra hour, any unpleasant job, because you thought that was what a decent man did. Now you are somewhere difficult, and you think difficulty means you must endure it alone. That is not the same thing as duty. Finish your rotation. Come home when you are due. Bring your things. We will decide the next part together. And Ewan—if you are lonely, say lonely. You do not need to dress it up as weather reports. Mam --- **19 August 1987** **North Rona Lighthouse** Mam, Lonely, then. Not every minute. There are good hours. Sunrise in the west windows, the lamp turning clean and steady, a ship answering on the radio. But there are nights I feel rubbed away at the edges. I miss somebody boiling a kettle in another room. I miss the noise of the street. I miss you shouting at the television. A yacht came close this week, a French couple with two children. Their engine had failed and they were trying to make Stornoway under sail. I gave them their position and warned them off the rocks. The wee girl came on the radio afterward and said, “Merci, lighthouse man.” I laughed for ten minutes after. Maybe that is why the light matters. You never see who it is for. You just keep it there. I have asked MacFarlane for no further posting after October. Your jumper is getting holes in the cuff. I wear it every night. Ewan --- **3 October 1987** **Govan** Dear Ewan, Your relief arrives on the 14th, so I expect you here by the weekend after, weather permitting. I have told Mrs. Devlin downstairs that you are coming and now she is planning a meal for half the building, so prepare yourself. The treatment has been hard, but I am still here, still cross, and still able to make tea. That should reassure you. I have thought about what comes next. There is a caretaker’s job advertised at the primary school near Bellahouston. It is not the sea, but it has boilers, keys, small repairs, and children who will certainly call you something rude if you let them. You would like it more than you think. Bring the photograph. And bring yourself home. Love, Mam
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This epistolary narrative successfully fulfills most of the prompt requirements while delivering a genuinely moving story. The correspondence spans eight months between Ewan, a Scottish lighthouse keeper, and his mother Mary, revealing their distinct voices and deepening relationship through crisis. The Scottish working-class voices are authentic without resorting to heavy dialect or stereotypes. Ewan's voice evolves from dutiful but distant ("Don't fret. It is only six months") to increasingly vulnerable and honest. His mother's voice is consistently warm but pragmatic, with sharp insights that reveal her wisdom and strength. The dialogue feels natural and unforced throughout. The gradual revelation of the mother's illness creates genuine dramatic tension, while the parallel exploration of Ewan's isolation provides emotional depth. The story avoids melodrama—the mother's cancer diagnosis is handled with restraint, and Ewan's psychological struggles with isolation are portrayed realistically rather than sensationally. The imagery is particularly strong, from the opening "wet black scrape of rock" to the evocative descriptions of the lighthouse beam and surrounding seascape. The author shows rather than tells, using concrete details like "Tins of peaches from 1984" and "rabbits that sit under the old church wall" to build atmosphere. The emotional climax works well—Ewan's admission of loneliness ("Lonely, then") feels earned after his gradual breakdown of defenses. The resolution is satisfying without being overly optimistic; the mother's pragmatic suggestion about the caretaker job feels true to character. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the prose becomes slightly self-conscious ("strings being pulled from heaven to sea"), though Ewan's immediate self-correction ("Sorry. That sounds soft") actually turns this into character development. The pacing is generally strong, though the middle section could have been tightened slightly. The story successfully avoids clichés about lighthouse keepers or Scottish characters, instead creating believable, complex individuals whose relationship deepens through adversity. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 18.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17.0, Elegant Prose: 16.0, Emotionally Engaging: 18.0, Emotionally Complex: 17.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 2.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 3.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4.0, Amateurish: 3.0, Purple Prose: 4.0, Overwrought: 3.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 17.0, Overall Impression: 17.0
+ First-person unique perspective: Hacker Mindset — Score: 84.1
Prompt:
Hacker Mindset Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of a reformed (now) white hat hacker who cannot help but notice exploitable weaknesses in both systems and people they encounter. They habitually look for vulnerabilities and exploit vectors. The narrative takes place during a first date at a restaurant, where they're trying to focus on human connection. It's going surprisingly well due to a discovered shared nerd-fandom (magic the gathering). Things go a little sideways when the date reveals a noticeably insecure phone unlock pattern, which protagonist can't help but comment on. They are then left to negotiate the fallout of this faux pas and attempt to salvage (or perhaps sabotage) the remainder of the date. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I clock the exits before I sit down. Not because I’m planning to leave. Not because I expect trouble. It’s just what my brain does now, the same way some people notice shoes or accents or whether a room needs more plants. Exit by the host stand. Service door near the bathrooms, probably alarmed but often propped open during closing. Window behind the booth, decorative latch, single-pane glass. Camera over the bar with a dead zone beneath the fake fern. Then I notice her smiling at me across the table and force myself to stop mapping the attack surface of Olive & Oak. “Sorry,” I say, though I haven’t said anything yet. “Long day.” “It’s okay,” Mara says. “I had one too.” Her phone is faceup beside her water glass. Black case, constellation pattern. Notifications previewed on the lock screen. **MOM: Don’t forget Grandma’s recipe.** **Nora: You’re going to tell him about the dragon deck, right?** I look away quickly, as if I’ve accidentally glanced into a bathroom mirror in a stranger’s house. Human connection, I remind myself. That is why I am here. Not reconnaissance. Not threat modeling. Not silently collecting metadata like some depressed, overcaffeinated web scraper in a button-down shirt. I am here because my sister made my dating profile after she caught me eating cereal over the sink for the third time in a week and said, “You need to meet someone who doesn’t work in cybersecurity, or at least someone who will stop pretending that you’re too busy for joy.” Mara works in museum education. Her profile said she liked old maps, horror movies, and “the kind of board games that end friendships.” Mine said I fix things for a living and have been told I take trivia too seriously. We matched because she opened with: *What is the worst thing you’ve ever done in Magic: The Gathering?* I answered honestly: *I once built a blue control deck for a charity tournament and made a twelve-year-old cry.* She replied: *That is terrible. We should get married.* And now, forty minutes into our first date, I am trying very hard not to turn every sentence she gives me into a password-reset workflow. She tells me she grew up in a small town two hours north of here, “the kind of place where everyone knows everyone and the high school mascot was literally a ferret.” Her hometown. Her high school. Possibly her mascot. I tuck my hands under the table. She tells me her older brother used to lock her out of the family computer when they were kids, so she learned to change Windows passwords from a library book. “I thought I was basically a hacker,” she says. “You were,” I say, smiling. “That counts.” It does. Curiosity counts. Persistence counts. The instinct to see a locked door and wonder who decided you shouldn’t open it—that counts too. The difference, I tell myself, is what you do after you get inside. I used to collect access the way other people collected souvenirs. School networks, neglected forums, company databases with “admin123” still hanging on like a loose thread. I told myself I was exposing weaknesses. Sometimes I was. Sometimes I was just lonely and wanted proof that the world was held together by bad assumptions. Now I get paid to tell companies where their bad assumptions are before someone worse finds them. White hat. Authorized. Scoped. Documented. The rules matter. Mara leans forward. “Okay, but what do you play?” “Magic?” “No, competitive origami.” “Blue-black,” I say. “Usually. Control, graveyard recursion, occasional nonsense.” Her eyes widen with theatrical disgust. “You’re one of those.” “I’m one of those.” “I play red-green dragons.” “Of course you do.” “What does that mean?” “It means you believe problems should be solved by making them larger and setting them on fire.” She points at me with her fork. “And you believe problems should be solved by preventing everyone else from having fun.” “That is an unfair characterization.” “It’s extremely fair.” And there it is: the moment the evening becomes easy. We talk about mana curves and cursed prerelease pulls. About the first cards we ever loved. Hers was Shivan Dragon, which she says she knows is basic, but it was the first creature she ever saw that felt mythic before mythic rarity existed. Mine was a battered copy of Counterspell that I bought for fifty cents from a kid named Stephen behind the middle school gym. She laughs when I admit I still have it. “Do you have it sleeved?” “Double-sleeved.” “Oh, no.” “It’s sentimental.” “It’s evidence.” She is funny. Not performatively funny, not trying to win every exchange. Just quick, observant. She has a little crease in one cheek when she smiles for real. I notice it and immediately hate the way my mind labels it an unpatched vulnerability. Not vulnerability. Opportunity. No. Not that either. Connection. Her phone buzzes again. She glances down, and the screen wakes. Then she unlocks it. I see the pattern. It is not complicated. It begins at the top-left node, draws a crude M, then drops diagonally to the bottom-right. M. Mara. Or maybe M for Mom. Maybe M for Magic. Maybe it’s not even an M. But pattern locks are visual passwords, and humans are narrative animals. We choose shapes that mean things. Initials. Houses. Zs. Stars. The ones we draw most often in childhood. The pattern is visible for less than a second. Less than a second is an eternity if you are trained. My stomach drops. Don’t say anything. She is reading a message from her friend. I should ask about the dragon deck. I should ask what horror movie she thinks is underrated. I should say, “What’s your favorite set?” I should continue behaving like a normal person who does not mentally calculate the odds of shoulder-surfing success in ambient restaurant lighting. Instead, because apparently rehabilitation is not a cure but a daily practice I sometimes fail at spectacularly, I say: “You should really change your unlock pattern.” Mara looks up. The words hang between us. “What?” I could still recover. There is a narrow window. A poorly configured but technically exploitable one. “I mean,” I say, “pattern locks are less secure than PINs. Generally.” Her expression shifts—not angry yet, but guarded. “Did you just watch me unlock my phone?” “No. I mean, yes, accidentally.” “Accidentally.” “I wasn’t trying to—” “But you saw it.” I should stop. Apologize. Full stop. No qualifiers. No educational sidebar. No threat model. “Yes,” I say. “I saw it. I’m sorry.” She turns her phone facedown. A small movement. Nothing dramatic. But it lands with the weight of a door locking. The old version of me would have retreated into technical correctness. *I’m trying to help.* *You should be grateful.* *The vulnerability exists whether you’re comfortable hearing about it or not.* That version of me mistook being right for being harmless. Mara takes a sip of water. “What exactly did you see?” My brain offers a dozen answers, all of them calibrated for precision. I can describe the sequence. I can tell her that the screen leaves smudge trails. I can explain the common pattern-starting nodes and predictable directional bias. I can tell her that if her birthday is October seventeenth—which I know because she mentioned, five minutes ago, that she shares it with her grandmother and hates that people assume she’s a Libra—then a six-digit PIN based on that date would also be a bad idea. I say none of it. “I saw enough to know it was simple,” I say. “And I should not have commented on it the way I did.” She studies me. “I work in security,” I add. “Sometimes my brain stays at work when it shouldn’t.” “That sounds exhausting.” “It is.” “For you?” The question isn’t cruel. That makes it worse. “For everyone around me,” I say. The server arrives with our food at exactly the wrong time, setting down mushroom risotto for her and a burger for me. We both thank him automatically. He leaves, oblivious, fortunate. His world remains free of the consequences of my mouth. Mara looks at her plate. “My ex used to do that.” My chest tightens. “Not the phone thing exactly,” she says. “But he’d pick at everything. My passwords, my driving, the way I organized my apartment. He called it helping.” She glances up. “I know you’re not him. I just don’t love being analyzed on a first date.” I nod. Don’t defend. Don’t explain yourself into innocence. “You’re right,” I say. “I’m sorry. You didn’t ask for an audit.” A reluctant smile twitches at the edge of her mouth. “An audit,” she repeats. “See? Still doing it.” “That was at least funny.” “I’ll take probationary funny.” She takes a breath, and some of the tension leaves her shoulders. Not all of it. I don’t deserve all of it to leave. Trust should not reset because I delivered a competent apology. “I do want to change it now, though,” she says. “Which is annoying.” “I can help,” I say, then immediately cringe. “Only if you want. And only with general advice. I will not look.” “General advice,” she says, considering. “Okay. Give me your least terrifying version.” I keep my eyes on my burger. “Use a longer PIN instead of a pattern. Don’t use birthdays, addresses, your old zip code, your dog’s name, or anything someone could get from social media.” “Wow,” she says. “That sounds weirdly specific.” I almost laugh, then don’t. Earlier, she had told me her childhood dog’s name was Miso. She had mentioned she still uses the name for all her game characters. She had told me her first car was a blue Civic named Bramble. She had mentioned that her mom posts every birthday photo publicly. Breadcrumbs. Tiny bright pieces of identity scattered casually across the table. Not her fault. Not a moral failing. People should be allowed to tell stories about themselves without wondering who is turning those stories into keys. “And,” I say carefully, “it’s not because you did anything wrong. It’s because people can be invasive.” Her expression softens. “People like you?” The question strikes cleanly. “People like who I used to be,” I say. For a moment, the restaurant noise fades into a soft blur: silverware, low music, someone laughing too loudly near the bar. The camera above the fern continues watching nothing useful. Mara waits. “I never hurt anyone,” I say, then correct myself. “I mean, I didn’t think I did. But I crossed lines. I got into systems I shouldn’t have. I treated access like proof I was smarter than the people who built things. Eventually I realized that finding a weakness doesn’t give you ownership over it.” Her gaze stays on mine. “And now?” “Now I try to leave things safer than I found them.” “That’s a good answer,” she says. “It’s also the true one.” She nods, then picks up her fork. “So,” she says, “if I let you survive this date, are you going to tell me why blue-black players think making everyone discard their hand is a personality trait?” Relief rises so fast it almost feels suspicious. “I can explain,” I say. “Oh no. Don’t explain. Defend yourself.” I smile. “Gladly.” And as we argue over whether counterspells are morally equivalent to villainy, I let myself stop watching the exits. Not forever. Just long enough to see whether she laughs at my next joke.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and adherence to the prompt's specific requirements. The author successfully maintains the first-person perspective of a reformed hacker throughout, with the internal monologue dominating as requested. The restaurant setting and Magic: The Gathering shared interest are well-integrated, and the phone unlock pattern incident serves as an effective catalyst for conflict. The characterization is particularly strong. The protagonist's mindset feels authentic - the automatic threat assessment, the technical vocabulary bleeding into personal interactions, and the struggle between old habits and reformed intentions. The voice remains consistent throughout, balancing technical knowledge with self-awareness and vulnerability. Mara is also well-developed despite limited dialogue, coming across as intelligent, cautious, and fair rather than simply reactive. The prose quality is high, with clean, precise sentences that mirror the protagonist's analytical nature without becoming sterile. The author effectively uses specific details (the camera's dead zone, double-sleeved cards, the crease in Mara's cheek) to create vivid imagery while maintaining the character's observational perspective. The technical elements feel researched and authentic rather than superficial. The emotional complexity is well-handled. The protagonist's internal conflict between wanting connection and defaulting to analysis creates genuine tension. The comparison to Mara's controlling ex adds depth to her reaction without making her unreasonable. The resolution feels earned - neither too easy nor artificially dramatic. However, the piece occasionally veers toward being slightly overwrought in its metaphors ("collecting access the way other people collected souvenirs," "breadcrumbs of identity"). Some moments of introspection, while well-written, slow the narrative momentum. The ending, while satisfying, perhaps resolves a bit too neatly given the severity of the boundary violation. The dialogue, though limited, feels natural and serves character development well. The Magic: The Gathering banter effectively establishes rapport and provides contrast to the later tension. The conflict escalation and resolution through conversation feels realistic rather than forced. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 7 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 5 Unearned Transformations: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ First-person unique perspective: Overthinking It — Score: 79.3
Prompt:
Overthinking It Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of an intelligent 8th-grade girl with social anxiety who has developed a crush on a classmate. The narrative takes place during a group science project where they're paired together, as she cycles through rehearsed conversations while second-guessing each potential approach. Her inner thoughts should reveal the gap between her articulate internal voice and her difficulty expressing herself aloud, her fears of coming across as desperate or weird, and the exhausting process of monitoring her every word, expression, and movement for potential embarrassment. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The projector squeaks again. Not like a normal squeak, either. It makes this long, rubbery sound, like somebody is slowly stepping on a balloon but never popping it. Every time Ms. Alvarez clicks to the next slide, the projector squeaks, and every time it does, everyone looks up for half a second. Everyone except me. I am looking at the corner of my science worksheet because looking at the worksheet is normal. Looking at the worksheet says, I am a person who understands density and mass and is definitely not sitting next to the boy she has liked since October. “Okay,” Ms. Alvarez says. “You’ll have the rest of class to plan your egg-drop containers. Partners are listed on the board.” The projector squeaks. I already know who my partner is because I saw the list when she put it up, and then I looked away immediately because apparently seeing someone’s name next to yours is a medical emergency. Maya Chen — Eli Torres. My stomach does the elevator-drop thing. Eli is taking his backpack off one shoulder. He has dark hair that never really stays flat, even when he tries, and he is wearing a blue sweatshirt with a tiny bleach stain near the pocket. I noticed it yesterday. Which is not creepy. People notice things. Detectives notice things. Scientists notice things. We are literally in science. He sits down across from me at the lab table. “Hey,” he says. This is it. This is the opening. This is where I say something normal. Option one: “Hey.” That is probably safe. Too safe? Maybe it sounds like I don’t care. Like I am too cool to speak in more than one syllable. I have never been cool in my life, so pretending would be dishonest. Option two: “Hi, how are you?” No. That sounds like I am a substitute teacher. Option three: “Hey, I’m glad we’re partners.” Absolutely not. That is basically screaming, I HAVE THOUGHT ABOUT YOU IN SEVERAL DIFFERENT LIGHTING CONDITIONS. Option four: make a joke about the projector. “Hey,” I say. My voice comes out quieter than I planned, like it got lost on the way from my brain to my mouth. Eli leans forward a little. “What?” Great. Perfect. First word and already he can’t hear me. I could repeat myself louder, but then it might sound annoyed, like I think he is stupid for not hearing a sound that was barely audible to bats. “I said hey,” I say, slightly louder. “Oh. Hey.” He smiles. Not a huge smile. Just normal. Friendly. Possibly he smiles like that at everyone. He probably does. There are girls in our class who could trip over a chair and Eli would help them up with that exact smile, and they would laugh in a graceful way instead of turning red and wishing the floor had a trapdoor. “Um,” he says, looking at the worksheet. “So we have to make something that keeps the egg from cracking when it drops?” “Yes,” I say immediately. Too immediately. Like I have been waiting for him to ask. Which I have. But he cannot know that. I force myself to look at the paper, not at him. “We need to use limited materials. Cardboard, tape, cotton balls, rubber bands, straws…” “Cool,” he says. “I guess we should figure out a design.” I have figured out seven designs. Last night, when I found out our project groups, I told myself I would only look up egg-drop ideas for ten minutes. Then I looked up egg-drop ideas for an hour and a half, which is not technically stalking because the eggs are not people. I drew a sketch in the margins of my math homework. I also watched a video where a guy in college used a parachute, but Ms. Alvarez said no parachutes, so that is useless information except now I know too much about air resistance. I could say, “I have some ideas.” That is normal. Smart, even. It would show I am contributing. But what if he thinks I am bossy? What if he wanted to be the idea person? What if I say I have ideas and then he asks what they are and I suddenly forget every word I have ever learned? “I have some ideas,” I say. Okay. It happened. The world did not end. The ceiling has not fallen in. Ms. Alvarez clicks another slide and the projector squeaks like it disapproves, but that is unrelated. “Really?” Eli says. “What kind?” This is the part where I should explain the cushioning system, the distribution of force, and possibly the benefits of a straw frame. Instead, my brain opens twelve tabs at once. Say the cardboard box idea. No, it is too basic. Say the straw frame idea. What if straws are too complicated? Mention momentum. That sounds smart. But maybe too smart. People hate when girls sound like they are trying too hard. Except that is stupid. Nobody should hate that. Still, they might. Maybe say, “I was thinking we could put cotton balls around it.” Simple. But then he will think that is my only thought, when actually I have an entire system. “I was thinking,” I begin, “that we could maybe make a, um…” Why did I say “um”? I knew the sentence. I rehearsed it internally. My internal voice is not like this. In my head, I am fluent and funny and casually confident. In my head, I say things like, “We should build a crumple zone, like in a car, so the outer layer absorbs the impact.” Out loud, apparently, I am a broken elevator. “A box?” I finish. Eli nods. “Yeah. Like with padding?” “Yes,” I say. “Exactly. And we could make an outer part with straws, maybe, so it absorbs some of the impact before it gets to the egg.” He looks at me. Not in a bad way. Just looking. Listening. “That’s actually really good,” he says. Actually. Actually really good. Does “actually” mean he expected it to be bad? Or does everyone say actually? I say actually constantly in my head, but maybe it is a mean word when somebody else says it. “Thanks,” I say. Too fast again. I try to smile, but then I become aware that I am smiling. Is it too much smile? Are my teeth weird? I close my mouth a little. Then I worry that looks unfriendly. Then I probably make some kind of expression that looks like I am trying not to sneeze. Eli pulls the materials list closer. “So, straw frame, cardboard box, cotton balls. Maybe rubber bands?” “Rubber bands could work,” I say. “Like, suspension.” He looks up. “Suspension?” “Yes. Sorry. I mean, we could kind of hang the egg inside the box with rubber bands, so it doesn’t hit the sides.” “Don’t say sorry,” he says, smiling again. “That makes sense.” My face gets warm. Do not blush. Blushing is not controllable, which is rude, honestly. Bodies should not be allowed to announce things without permission. “I just mean,” I say, because apparently I am determined to keep talking until I ruin it, “the egg would have less direct force on it.” “That’s smart,” he says. I should say thank you. I should say something casual. I should not say, You think I’m smart? because that sounds like I need him to reassure me, which I do, but nobody is supposed to know that. “Science,” I say. Science? That is not a response. That is a category. For one horrible second, I think he is going to stare at me like I have turned into a chair. Then he laughs. Not mean-laughing. I don’t think. It is a small laugh, and he says, “Yeah. Science.” I laugh too, mostly because he did, but it comes out real. For a second, I forget to inspect it. Maybe this is how other people do conversations. Maybe they say weird things sometimes and nobody writes it down in a permanent file called Reasons Everyone Secretly Thinks I’m Strange. Eli starts sketching a square on the paper. “Want to draw the straw frame?” My hand is suddenly too noticeable. My fingers are too noticeable. If I reach for the pencil wrong, he will know I have never held a pencil before. I reach for it anyway. “Sure,” I say. The projector squeaks. I draw two lines, then a third. Eli leans closer to see the page, and his sleeve brushes the edge of my hand. My brain stops being a brain. It becomes one giant flashing sign that says: ACT NORMAL. So I keep drawing, even though I am pretty sure the straw frame now looks like a lopsided spider. Eli doesn’t seem to notice. “Looks good,” he says. And maybe he means the drawing. Maybe he means the project. Maybe he just means the lines are lines. But I let myself believe him for almost three whole seconds before I start overthinking it again.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong understanding of the assignment and executes it with considerable skill. The author successfully captures the voice of an intelligent 8th-grade girl with social anxiety, creating an authentic internal monologue that feels genuinely adolescent without being condescending or overly cute. The narrative structure works well, using the science project as a natural framework for the character's anxious overthinking. The author effectively shows the gap between Maya's articulate internal voice and her halting external expression - her sophisticated thoughts about "crumple zones" and "air resistance" contrast sharply with her stammered "Science" response, which is both painful and believable. The writing demonstrates strong technical control. The prose is clean and purposeful, with well-chosen details like the projector's squeak serving as both atmospheric element and nervous focal point. The author shows restraint in avoiding purple prose while still creating vivid moments, such as Maya's brain becoming "one giant flashing sign that says: ACT NORMAL." The character work is particularly strong. Maya's anxiety feels authentic rather than performative - her overthinking patterns, her catastrophic interpretations of normal interactions, and her exhausting self-monitoring all ring true. The author captures the specific torture of adolescent social anxiety without making it feel exaggerated or comedic at the character's expense. However, the piece has some limitations. While the internal monologue is well-executed, it occasionally becomes repetitive in its patterns. The dialogue, though minimal as requested, sometimes feels slightly stilted. The ending, while appropriate, doesn't quite achieve the emotional resonance that the setup promises. The piece succeeds in being emotionally engaging without being overwrought. The author earns the small moments of connection between Maya and Eli through careful buildup of Maya's anxiety, making even minor positive interactions feel significant. The writing flows naturally and maintains consistent voice throughout. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 15 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 8 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 3 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ First-person unique perspective: Rehearsing Rejection — Score: 88.2
Prompt:
Rehearsing Rejection Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of someone with borderline personality disorder who frequently plays out worst-case scenarios of how friends and loved ones might judge them. They construct elaborate internal dialogues, complete with imagined criticisms and their own defensive responses. The narrative takes place as they prepare to meet a close friend & recent lover, who has been somewhat distant lately, triggering their abandonment fears. Their inner monologue should reveal how they cycle between anticipating rejection, mentally rehearsing confrontations, and developing preemptive resentments as protective measures. Incorporate the *actual* events & interactions that are *not* in fact judgemental or involving rejection (in fact, the opposite), and represent how these are interpreted, how the validation & love is desperately felt & needed -- and then immediately questioned. Capture the cognitive dissonance in this struggle. Include moments where they briefly recognize the pattern but struggle to interrupt it, showing limited (and selective) self awareness, competing with the compelling nature of these imagined scenarios. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
My phone is at 6%, which is not an emergency except that it is. Six percent means the world can disappear without warning. Six percent means if they cancel, I might not see it until I’m already halfway there, standing on the platform with my stupid coat buttoned wrong and my face arranged into something casual. I plug it in. The charger is loose. Of course it is. The cord has to be bent at exactly the right angle, held under the leg of the desk lamp like some tiny hostage negotiation. Stay alive, I tell the phone. Then: Stay alive, I tell myself, which is dramatic. I know it’s dramatic. I’m not going to do anything. I’m just going to meet Eli for dinner. My close friend Eli. My recent lover Eli. The person who texted, three days ago, *I keep thinking about the other night*, followed by a red heart, followed by *in a good way, obviously.* Obviously. Except they have been distant since then. Not cruel. Not even noticeably distant, if you’re someone who doesn’t measure the time between texts like a seismograph. They answered me this morning: *Sorry, work has been absolutely unreal. Still okay for tonight? Really looking forward to seeing you.* Really looking forward. I read it again. *Really looking forward to seeing you.* There are two ways to understand that sentence. One is that Eli is looking forward to seeing me. The other is that Eli sensed I was worried and is cushioning the inevitable blow with enthusiasm. People do that. They say, “Really looking forward to it,” right before they say, “I’ve been meaning to talk to you,” right before they say, “You’re important to me, but not in the way you think.” I can hear Eli’s imagined voice already, gentle and pained. They always sound gentle in my head when they’re rejecting me. It makes it worse. “I care about you so much,” Imaginary Eli says. “Then why did you sleep with me?” I ask, not yelling. I’m calm in this version. Devastatingly calm. “That isn’t fair.” “No, it isn’t,” I say. “None of this has been fair.” “I didn’t mean to lead you on.” “You didn’t lead me on. You walked me directly into a wall and acted surprised when I got a concussion.” That is good. I should remember that one. Not use it, necessarily. But remember it, in case I need it. The phone flickers from 6% to 5%. I stare at it. My chest tightens as if the number has accused me of something. I could stay home. I could send a message: *Actually I’m feeling kind of tired, rain check?* Then I would be in control. Eli couldn’t abandon me if I had already stepped away. I would lie in bed with the phone charging beside me and wait for their response, which would either be relief—proof—or concern, which would also somehow be proof. If they say, *Of course, rest up,* that means they don’t care. If they say, *Are you okay?* that means they know something is wrong with me and are preparing to manage it. If they call, it means I’ve become a problem. There is no response that does not contain a trap. My reflection in the dark window looks tired. I try on a smile. It looks like an apology. What if Eli says I’m intense? They haven’t said that. Not exactly. Once, last month, they said, “You feel things very fully, don’t you?” We were in bed, and I had been crying because they had kissed my forehead after sex and the tenderness of it had felt unbearable, like being handed something breakable I was certain I would drop. They’d wiped under my eye with their thumb. “I like that about you,” they had said. I had said, “You shouldn’t.” They had looked genuinely confused. “Why not?” Because people like intensity until it asks them to stay. Because people love being loved until love has needs. Because I know what happens next. I put on eyeliner, wipe it off, put it on again. Too much makes me look like I’m trying. Too little makes me look like I don’t care. I settle on something in between, which probably looks like exactly what it is: someone trying very hard to look like they are not trying at all. My phone buzzes. My body reacts before I read it. Heat in my face. Nausea. The bright rush of certainty: here it is. 4%. It’s Eli. *On my way now. Also, I got us a table somewhere with outlets because I know your phone is always fighting for its life lol.* For a second, something inside me goes quiet. They noticed. Noticed the phone. Noticed me. Made a plan around a small fact about me. An outlet. A table. A place for me to plug in. I press the phone against my chest, ridiculous and immediate. I want to cry. I want to text back a paragraph. *You remembered? That’s so sweet. I’m sorry I’m like this. I love you. I don’t mean—* No. Too much. Too revealing. Too much pressure. I type: *haha you know me too well. leaving now* Then I stare at the message, because *you know me too well* can sound accusing. Like, why do you know I’m always at 4%? Why are you cataloguing my defects? Why did you say “fighting for its life,” like I’m incompetent? Like I can’t take care of anything? No. Stop. They are being kind. They are being kind, and it feels like oxygen, and that is exactly why I can’t trust it. I imagine Eli at dinner, their hands folded around a glass. “I do love you,” they say. My imaginary self flinches. “But?” “There’s no but.” “There’s always a but.” “You make there be one.” That lands hard enough that I stop moving. The apartment is silent except for the refrigerator and the faint electric hum from the charger. I can see the pattern for a moment, almost clinically. The mind building a courtroom before anyone has filed a charge. The rehearsed anger, the preemptive verdict. If I can be furious first, then I won’t have to be surprised. If I can make Eli cruel in advance, then their kindness can’t disarm me. But recognizing it is not the same as stopping. My body doesn’t care that I have named it. My body has already decided we are in danger. 3%. I grab my coat. I make sure the charger is in my bag, then take it out, then put it back. I check the outlet adapter. I check my keys three times. My hands are shaking, but only a little. Enough that I can pretend it’s the cold. On the walk to the station, I rehearse a different version. Eli says, “I’ve missed you.” I say, “Have you?” They look hurt. “Yes.” “Because it hasn’t felt like it.” There. Honest. Not cruel. Then another version: Eli says, “I’ve missed you.” I say, “Me too,” and nothing else. I let the warmth happen. I let myself believe them for one whole second. This version is terrifying. At the station, my phone drops to 2%. I consider sending a message to say I’m there, but if it dies after that, Eli will worry. If I don’t send it, they’ll think I’m flaky. If I explain the battery, I’ll sound incapable. If I don’t explain it, I’ll look careless. I send: *at the station! phone may die but I’m coming* The typing bubble appears immediately. *I’ll keep an eye out for you. No rush. I’m here.* No rush. I read it until the screen dims. No rush means no anger. No rush means they are waiting. No rush means they want me to arrive. And then, because my mind is fast and practiced and merciless: no rush means they expect me to be difficult. They’ve already adjusted for me. They’ve already made room for the fact that I am too much trouble to meet on ordinary terms. The train arrives with a scream of brakes. I step inside, gripping my dying phone, carrying the message like a match cupped between my hands. *I’m here.* For tonight, I try to let that mean what it says.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of borderline personality disorder and the internal experience of anticipatory rejection. The narrative successfully captures the exhausting mental gymnastics of someone who transforms neutral or positive interactions into evidence of impending abandonment. The writing excels in several key areas. The voice is remarkably consistent throughout - anxious, hypervigilant, and caught in recursive thought patterns that feel authentic to the condition being portrayed. The author skillfully uses concrete details (the phone battery percentage, the loose charger, eyeliner application) as anchors for abstract emotional states, creating vivid imagery that grounds the reader in the protagonist's experience. The structure is particularly effective, moving between imagined conversations and real interactions while showing how the protagonist reframes Eli's genuine care as potential manipulation. The cognitive dissonance is well-executed - moments where the protagonist recognizes their pattern ("I can see the pattern for a moment, almost clinically") but cannot escape it demonstrate sophisticated psychological insight. The prose is elegant without being overwrought. Lines like "I carry the message like a match cupped between my hands" and "My body has already decided we are in danger" are poetic but serve the emotional narrative rather than existing for their own sake. The internal monologue feels natural and unforced. However, there are some minor weaknesses. Certain metaphors occasionally feel slightly forced ("measuring time between texts like a seismograph"), and some of the imagined dialogue, while psychologically accurate, lacks the distinct voice that would make Eli feel like a separate person rather than a projection. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - neither falsely optimistic nor gratuitously dark. The protagonist's attempt to "let that mean what it says" feels earned given the preceding struggle, representing a small but realistic moment of choosing trust over fear. The piece adheres closely to the prompt requirements, delivering nearly exactly 1000 words of primarily internal monologue that captures the specific psychological patterns requested. The writing demonstrates maturity and avoids common pitfalls of mental health fiction by focusing on the experience rather than explaining it. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 19 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 17.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18.0, Elegant Prose: 17.0, Emotionally Engaging: 18.0, Emotionally Complex: 19.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 4.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 2.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4.0, Amateurish: 2.0, Purple Prose: 3.0, Overwrought: 4.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 18.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 18.0, Overall Impression: 18.0
+ Physical-spatial: Blood Debt — Score: 77.8
Prompt:
"Blood Debt" Write a vivid third-person narrative depicting an unsanctioned underground duel between two talented but hot-headed martial artists. Their schools have been embroiled in a generations-old rivalry, and despite strict prohibitions from their masters, these two younger fighters arrange a secret confrontation fueled by personal pride and simmering animosity. - **One fighter** represents **Jigen-ryū**, a traditional, aggressive style practiced historically by elite samurai clans. Its practitioners emphasize explosive offense, decisive strikes, and overwhelming speed. Known historically for their arrogance and disdain toward outsiders, practitioners consider their art superior and untouchable. - **The opposing fighter** employs a form of historical **Piguaquan**, a Chinese martial art traditionally practiced by commoners, bodyguards, and street fighters. This style emphasizes whipping, circular strikes, rapid footwork, unpredictable angles of attack, and evasive maneuvers. Practitioners often come from lower social classes and view the traditional elite as pretentious and detached from true combat effectiveness. The fight takes place in the crumbling ruins of an abandoned temple, now functioning as a hidden arena for illicit duels. Passionate supporters from both factions surround them, wagering money, pride, and reputation on the outcome. **Essential Instructions:** Maintain absolute physical coherence throughout—clearly track fighters' positions relative to each other and their environment. Precisely depict strikes, counters, momentum shifts, and spatial dynamics, allowing readers to clearly visualize the mechanics and choreography of the fight at every moment. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The abandoned temple had no gods left in it. Its roof had collapsed years ago, leaving a jagged black opening above the central hall. Rain had eaten grooves into the flagstones. Moss climbed the cracked altar at the far end, where a headless stone guardian leaned beneath a curtain of roots. The only light came from a single torch wedged in an iron bracket on the east wall. Its flame guttered in the drafts, throwing the room into restless bands of orange and black. Around the square of open floor, men and women pressed between broken pillars. Silk sleeves brushed patched wool. Samurai retainers stood shoulder to shoulder with dockhands, caravan guards, gamblers, and apprentices. Coins changed hands in cupped palms. Nobody spoke above a whisper. At the north side of the hall stood Arata Kenzō of Jigen-ryū. He wore a dark training jacket tied tightly at the waist, sleeves rolled above his wrists. A wooden sword—hard oak, scarred from use—rested in both hands, its tip angled toward the floor. He stood just inside the edge of the torchlight, broad-shouldered and motionless, his left foot forward, knees slightly bent. His eyes were fixed on the opposite side of the flagstones. Across from him, near the south wall where a fallen beam lay half-buried under plaster, waited Liang Wei. Liang had no weapon. His loose gray jacket had been repaired at both elbows, and a red cord bound his hair at the nape. His hands hung low and open, palms facing inward. He shifted lightly on the balls of his feet, never settling. His stance was narrower than Kenzō’s, body turned sideways—left shoulder forward, right hip pulled back—making a smaller target. Between them lay six paces of cracked stone. “Your master should have taught you to bow,” Kenzō said. Liang smiled without warmth. “Your master should have taught you that a sword does not make a man important.” A mutter rippled through the southern crowd. Kenzō’s jaw tightened. “A commoner’s art. Spinning arms and gutter tricks.” “And yours?” Liang’s gaze flicked to the bokken. “One swing, one prayer. You call that skill because no one ever told your ancestors no.” The torch snapped. Shadows leapt across Kenzō’s face. No referee stood between them. No formal signal was given. Kenzō moved first. He did not advance cautiously. Jigen-ryū’s answer to distance was to erase it. His rear foot drove off the stone, and he crossed nearly four paces in a blur. The bokken rose from low right to high left, then descended in a diagonal cut aimed at Liang’s left collarbone. Liang had been drifting to his own right—toward the west wall—and the first step carried him outside the line of the blade. He did not retreat straight back. Instead, he pivoted on his right foot, left foot sliding in an arc behind him. The wooden sword hissed past the front of his jacket, close enough to stir the cloth. As Kenzō’s cut finished low to Liang’s right, Liang lashed his left arm outward in a wide Piguaquan whip. His shoulder rolled, elbow loose, forearm snapping toward Kenzō’s temple. Kenzō’s left hand released the sword hilt for an instant. He raised his forearm, catching Liang’s strike near the wrist. The blow slapped against bone. Kenzō’s head turned with the impact, but he used the contact to shove Liang’s arm across Liang’s own centerline. Liang’s torso twisted left. Kenzō stepped in hard, right foot landing between Liang’s feet. He drove the butt end of the bokken toward Liang’s ribs. Liang folded sideways and backward, spine bending like a bow. The wooden pommel skimmed his jacket instead of crushing his floating ribs. His right hand slapped down on the flagstone to steady the lean, and his left foot skated backward toward the south wall. Before Kenzō could recover the weapon, Liang’s right leg whipped upward from the low lean. His heel struck Kenzō’s left thigh, just above the knee. Kenzō’s leg buckled half a step. Liang sprang away, landing three paces south of him, close now to the fallen beam. His chest rose and fell faster, but his grin had returned. “You’re fast,” he said. Kenzō adjusted his footing. The kick had not injured him badly, but it had disrupted his base. He brought the bokken back to center, tip aimed at Liang’s face. “You’re still alive,” Kenzō replied. They circled. Kenzō edged east, keeping the torch on his right. Liang mirrored him from the south, moving westward along the open floor. The torchlight gave Kenzō’s weapon a bright edge but left Liang’s feet flickering in and out of shadow. Kenzō understood the danger. Liang wanted angles, not exchanges. He wanted Kenzō turning, resetting, striking at air. So Kenzō denied him room. He surged forward again, this time with a short, direct thrust toward Liang’s sternum. Liang knocked the bokken aside with a circular sweep of his right arm, not meeting the thrust head-on but guiding it to his own left. At the same time, he stepped diagonally toward Kenzō’s outside—toward the east wall, nearer the torch. His left hand snapped toward Kenzō’s throat. Kenzō jerked his chin back. Liang’s fingers raked his jaw instead of gripping his windpipe. Kenzō rotated with the deflection, letting the thrust become a horizontal cut. The bokken swept left to right at waist height. Liang was too close to retreat cleanly. He hopped backward, but the tip caught the side of his jacket and tore it open at the hip. A line of blood appeared beneath the cloth. The northern crowd roared. Kenzō pressed him immediately. He stepped after the cut and raised the bokken overhead with both hands. His shoulders narrowed, body pitched forward, every part of him committed to a killing descent. Liang’s back was now only two paces from the east wall. The torch burned above and behind Kenzō’s right shoulder. Broken plaster and old brick blocked Liang’s retreat. Kenzō brought the bokken down. Liang did not flee. He stepped inside. His left foot planted just outside Kenzō’s right foot. His right hand shot up beneath Kenzō’s descending arms, striking the inside of Kenzō’s right elbow. Not enough to stop the blow—nothing could stop that much force—but enough to bend its path. The bokken slammed into the stone beside Liang’s left foot. Wood cracked. Kenzō’s momentum carried him low, arms extended. Liang pivoted around the trapped weapon, circling clockwise behind Kenzō’s right side. His left forearm whipped across Kenzō’s neck from behind, while his right palm struck the back of Kenzō’s right shoulder. Kenzō stumbled forward, but he did not fall. He released the lodged bokken and spun toward Liang with a savage backhand. Liang ducked under it. Kenzō’s knuckles tore through the torchlight above Liang’s head. From the crouch, Liang drove a right palm into Kenzō’s liver beneath the ribs. Kenzō grunted, folding slightly to his left. Liang followed with a whipping left strike toward the ear. Kenzō caught the forearm this time. His grip clamped down near Liang’s elbow. With his other hand he seized Liang’s collar. The elegant circles of Piguaquan vanished under the brutal closeness. Kenzō yanked Liang forward and smashed his forehead into Liang’s nose. Blood sprayed dark across the flagstones. Liang recoiled, but Kenzō maintained the collar grip. He dragged him toward the center, away from the wall, then drove a knee toward Liang’s abdomen. Liang turned his hips. The knee hit his right side instead of the center of his stomach. He exhaled sharply, absorbing what he could, and used the impact to spin. His trapped arm wound over Kenzō’s gripping forearm. His free right hand struck downward across Kenzō’s wrist, peeling the collar grip loose. Then Liang’s body unfurled. His left arm came around in a long, brutal arc. The back of his knuckles struck Kenzō behind the ear. Kenzō’s vision flashed white. He staggered one step north, toward the altar. Liang chased, feet skimming fast over the uneven stone. Another circular strike came high. Kenzō raised both arms to shield his head. Liang’s arm curved around the guard and cracked into Kenzō’s ribs. Kenzō retreated again, one pace, then another. His heel hit the lowest altar step. He could go no farther back without climbing onto crumbling stone. Liang saw the trap and rushed in. He led with a wide right whip toward Kenzō’s left temple, expecting the guard. Kenzō gave it to him, forearm rising. But this time Kenzō did not block merely to survive. He stepped forward off the altar step at the exact moment Liang’s arm met his guard. Kenzō drove his shoulder into Liang’s chest, crashing through the circular strike before it could recoil. The impact knocked the air from Liang’s lungs. Kenzō’s right hand caught the back of Liang’s neck. With his left, he drove a short, straight punch into Liang’s mouth. Liang’s head snapped back. Blood ran over his chin. Kenzō struck again—left fist to the cheekbone. Liang stumbled south, trying to recover distance, but Kenzō followed with the same merciless burst that had defined his school for generations. No flourish. No pause. He seized Liang’s torn jacket, shoved him toward the center, and hammered a final palm strike into Liang’s chest. Liang fell backward. His shoulders hit the flagstones first. His head struck a crack in the stone with a dull, terrible sound. He rolled onto one elbow, dazed, facing the south wall. Kenzō stood over him, breathing through his nose. Behind them, the torch sputtered violently. The broken bokken still jutted from the floor near the east wall. Liang tried to rise. His right hand found the stone. His left arm trembled beneath him. He got one knee under his body. Kenzō looked down at him. “Yield.” Liang spat blood onto the floor between them. Then he laughed—a ragged, furious sound. “You came with a sword,” Liang said. “And you still needed your fists.” The insult struck harder than any blow. Kenzō’s face hardened. He drew back his foot. From the crowd, an older voice cut through the hall. “Enough!” No one moved at first. Then the torch went out. Darkness swallowed the temple whole. In the blackness came the scrape of feet, the clatter of coins overturned, and the sudden understanding of every witness: whatever debt had brought them there had not been paid. It had only begun.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution in several key areas while maintaining consistent quality throughout. The author successfully fulfills the prompt's demanding requirements for spatial coherence and fight choreography, tracking every movement, position, and strike with precision that allows readers to visualize the combat clearly. The setting of the abandoned temple is vividly established and effectively used throughout the fight. The characterization efficiently establishes the cultural and class tensions between the fighters through their dialogue and fighting styles. Kenzō embodies the arrogant samurai tradition while Liang represents the scrappy underdog, and their verbal exchanges feel authentic to their backgrounds. The contrast between Jigen-ryū's explosive directness and Piguaquan's circular evasiveness is well-maintained throughout the combat. The prose demonstrates considerable skill in balancing descriptive detail with narrative momentum. Sentences are generally well-constructed and flow naturally, avoiding both excessive purple prose and overly sparse description. The author shows rather than tells, particularly in conveying the fighters' personalities through their actions and combat choices. However, the piece suffers from some predictable elements. The class-based rivalry and the underdog's resilience follow familiar patterns, and while executed competently, don't offer surprising insights. The ending, while atmospheric with the torch going out, feels somewhat abrupt and doesn't fully resolve the dramatic tension built throughout the fight. The dialogue serves its purpose but occasionally veers toward exposition, particularly in establishing the fighters' backgrounds and motivations. Some exchanges feel slightly stilted, though they remain functional within the narrative. The emotional engagement is solid but not exceptional. While readers can follow the action and understand the stakes, the piece doesn't achieve deep emotional resonance or complexity. The characters remain somewhat archetypal rather than fully nuanced individuals. The technical aspects of the fight choreography are genuinely impressive, showing deep understanding of martial arts mechanics and spatial relationships. This adherence to the prompt's most challenging requirement elevates the overall quality significantly. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 13 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 16 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Emotionally Complex: 12 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 15 Overall Impression: 15
