+ Scp: Notes from the Future — Score: 84.5
Prompt:
Write an SCP report about a researcher who begins finding handwritten notes from their future self, warning about a containment breach that hasn't happened yet. The notes become increasingly desperate and incoherent, sometimes containing information that contradicts known reality. Include the Foundation's attempts to determine if this is an actual temporal anomaly or psychological manipulation by a contained entity, and the researcher's struggle to trust their own perception. First person (researcher's perspective with interspersed notes), past tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Item:** SCP-████ **Object Class:** Euclid **Researcher’s Personal Addendum:** Dr. Mara Venn I had been assigned to Site-41’s Temporal Anomalies Department for six years before the notes began appearing. That fact mattered to me because, according to the first note, I had been assigned there for nine. I found it beneath my keyboard at 06:13 on 14/03/20██. The paper was standard Foundation memo stock: white, faint blue grid, black “SECURE. CONTAIN. PROTECT.” header, serial watermark visible only under ultraviolet light. We used it for internal correspondence and incident reports. The sheet had been folded twice and pressed flat again. My name was written across the top. > **MARA—** > > Do not report this to Calder. > > He has already compromised the chamber. > > The breach begins with the clocks. > > **—M** I recognized my handwriting immediately. Not merely its shape, but its habits: the overlong downstroke on the capital M, the way I crossed lowercase t’s only after finishing a sentence. The ink was black gel, the same brand I kept in the left drawer of my desk. There were no cameras inside my office due to classified material restrictions, but the corridor footage showed no entry after I locked the room at 21:42 the previous night. Dr. Calder was my department head. He had not compromised anything. At least, not then. I reported the note to Site Security within the hour. I did not tell them that I had spent the first forty minutes staring at the handwriting and trying to remember whether I had written it during a fugue state. The initial investigation produced nothing. My office was swept for hidden apertures, phase-displacement devices, cognitohazardous material, and unauthorized surveillance equipment. My blood was tested for amnestics, sedatives, memetic exposure, and Class-B hallucinogens. I underwent a psychiatric evaluation administered by Dr. Tomin. She asked whether I had experienced blackouts. “No.” “Missing time?” “No.” “Stress-induced dissociation?” “I work in Temporal Anomalies.” She did not smile. The next note arrived inside a locked evidence bag. Security had placed the first note in a Level-4 secure archive locker at 12:08. At 12:11, Agent Miro opened the locker in the presence of three witnesses and found a second memo paper folded inside the sealed bag. > **You told them.** > > That was the first mistake. > > Calder isn’t the danger. Calder dies trying to stop it. > > Don’t let them isolate you. > > The thing in 14-B can hear you when you read this. > > **—M** Containment Chamber 14-B held SCP-███, a humanoid entity recovered from a defunct thaumaturgical research facility in ███████. The entity had no confirmed anomalous properties beyond persistent claims that it could “remember futures discarded by God.” It had been classified as Safe after two years of testing failed to verify any predictive capacity. The note changed that. Site Command ordered a full review. The entity was moved to a soundproofed humanoid cell under continuous observation. All staff who had interacted with the notes were interviewed. Dr. Calder, pale and furious, authorized the formation of Task Group Venn-1, an internal investigation dedicated to determining whether the notes represented temporal intrusion, hostile psychological manipulation, or an elaborate breach of protocol by an unknown actor. I hated that they named it after me. For three days, nothing happened. Then every clock in Site-41 stopped at 03:17. Not malfunctioned. Stopped. Digital clocks froze. Mechanical clocks halted mid-tick. Atomic synchronization systems returned contradictory timestamps. My wristwatch, which had been running two minutes fast for months, displayed 03:17 exactly. The emergency lights activated. Somewhere below us, an alarm began and then cut off. Dr. Calder called an immediate lockdown. He died forty-seven minutes later. A pressure door in Corridor D-7 closed on him during evacuation. The system logged no command. The door descended with enough force to sever his torso. The third note was found in my coat pocket while I was being escorted to Medical. > **I tried to save him too.** > > You never understood: the notes do not warn you about the future. > > They make it. > > Stop reading. > > **—M** I read it three times. Afterward, I asked Agent Miro to remove every note from my possession, place them in a Faraday-shielded containment box, and ensure I could not access them without written approval from Site Command. He complied. The following morning, I found a fourth note beneath my pillow. My residential quarters had been sealed from the outside. Agent Miro had personally maintained watch outside the door. No one had entered. > **You asked Miro to guard you because you still think doors matter.** > > Listen carefully. > > You are not receiving messages from your future self. > > You are remembering them. > > There is a difference. > > **—M** By then, the investigation had divided the site. The Temporal Department argued that an unstable causal loop had formed around me. The notes, they suggested, were artifacts moving backward through localized time. Their increasingly contradictory content might indicate repeated timeline revisions: each warning caused a different response, producing a different future version of me. The Memetics Division believed SCP-███ had established a delayed-action cognitive link. The notes, in that theory, were not physically appearing at all. I was manufacturing or misperceiving them under compulsion. Their Foundation memo paper format was merely a psychological detail chosen to make them credible. Security favored neither explanation. Security favored the possibility that I was compromised. They began following me everywhere. I began doubting every room I entered. There were moments when I noticed things that did not fit. A colleague would greet me by a name I had never used. A corridor would have one more door than it had the day before. I would remember an entire conversation with Dr. Calder, then remember attending his autopsy. Once, I passed the observation window of Chamber 14-B and saw SCP-███ sitting on the floor, its wrists cuffed before it. It looked up at me. Its face was mine. I screamed. Security sedated me. When I woke, the chamber held the original entity: a thin, gray-skinned man with cataract-white eyes. The recording showed that I had stared at him for thirty-seven seconds without speaking. He smiled when I next visited. “I told you,” he said through the intercom. “You’ve always been late.” “I never spoke to you before the notes.” “You did. You will. You have.” I told him he was manipulating me. He nodded sympathetically. “That is what you said the first time.” The fifth note appeared inside the containment box holding the others. Its timestamp watermark indicated it had been manufactured twelve years in the future. The Foundation memo header was obsolete. > **MARA, I AM SORRY.** > > There was no breach in the beginning. > > There was only an investigation. > > They kept asking whether the notes were real, so you kept trying to prove they were. > > You opened 14-B because you thought it knew something. > > It did. > > It knew you would open the door. > > **—M** I did not open Chamber 14-B. I want that stated clearly. I requested the entity’s termination. I requested amnestics. I requested transfer to a non-temporal site, then retirement, then indefinite suspension in a medically induced coma. Site Command denied all requests pending evaluation. They believed I might be the anomaly’s only point of contact. The sixth note was waiting on my desk when I returned from the hearing. > **You did open it.** > > You opened it yesterday. > > You will open it tomorrow. > > You are opening it now. > > There is blood under your fingernails. > > Don’t look. > > **—M** I looked. There was blood beneath my nails. It was dry. The laboratory lights began to flicker. Every clock on the wall turned backward, their hands spinning so quickly that the glass faces cracked. Beyond the reinforced window, alarms flared red across Site-41. Then, from somewhere below, I heard the first impact against a containment door. I did not know whether the breach had begun. I did not know whether it had already ended. I only knew that another sheet of Foundation memo paper was sliding, slowly and impossibly, from beneath my locked office door.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This SCP report demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of the Foundation universe while crafting an intricate temporal paradox narrative. The piece successfully maintains the clinical, bureaucratic tone expected of SCP documentation while weaving in the personal psychological horror of the researcher's deteriorating situation. The structure is particularly effective, alternating between formal report elements and increasingly desperate handwritten notes. This creates a compelling contrast that mirrors the protagonist's mental state. The author demonstrates strong command of the SCP format, including proper redactions, classification systems, and procedural language. The central concept—notes from a future self that may be creating the very disaster they warn against—is executed with genuine creativity. The temporal paradox elements are well-constructed, avoiding easy explanations while maintaining internal logic. The ambiguity between genuine temporal anomaly and psychological manipulation is sustained throughout, creating genuine uncertainty. Character development is handled subtly but effectively. Dr. Venn's voice remains consistent while showing gradual deterioration. Her rational, scientific approach slowly gives way to paranoia and confusion, but this transformation feels earned rather than forced. The supporting characters, while limited by the format, serve their purposes well. The prose quality is generally high, with strong atmospheric details and effective pacing. The author avoids purple prose while still creating vivid, unsettling imagery. The blood under the fingernails, the synchronized clock stoppage, and the entity with the protagonist's face are particularly effective horror elements. Some minor weaknesses include occasional exposition that could be more seamlessly integrated, and a few moments where the clinical tone wavers slightly. The ending, while appropriately ambiguous, might have benefited from one final twist or revelation to maximize impact. The piece succeeds in creating genuine unease and maintains reader engagement throughout. It demonstrates sophisticated understanding of both horror writing and the specific constraints of the SCP format. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 2 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 16.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17.0, Elegant Prose: 16.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 15.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 4.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 5.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 2.0, Amateurish: 3.0, Purple Prose: 2.0, Overwrought: 4.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1.0, Unearned Transformations: 3.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 16.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 17.0, Overall Impression: 17.0
+ Scp: Unmarked Doors Archive — Score: 82.5
Prompt:
Write an SCP entry about a series of identical unmarked doors appearing in abandoned buildings worldwide that lead to a massive, architecturally impossible archive. Documents within reference a previous global civilization destroyed by an unspecified containment failure. Foundation researchers discover references to their own organization under a different name, including personnel files for current staff dated centuries ago. Include exploration logs revealing sections of the archive actively being removed by entities resembling hazmat teams who flee when approached, and evidence that O5 Council members have visited the archive without authorization. Third person (exploration team perspective) with recovered document excerpts. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Item #:** SCP-7842 **Object Class:** Euclid **Special Containment Procedures:** All known instances of SCP-7842 are to be secured under the cover story of hazardous structural contamination. Civilian access is prohibited. Doors are not to be opened except during authorized exploration events approved by Site-19 Command and at least two Level 4 personnel. Personnel entering SCP-7842 are to maintain continuous tether, audio, and video contact with the entry team. No document, artifact, or material larger than 30 cm in any dimension may be removed without review by the Department of Records and Information Security. Under no circumstances are personnel to obstruct, follow, or attempt communication with entities designated SCP-7842-A. Foundation personnel who discover records naming themselves, relatives, or known Foundation staff are to report immediately for psychological evaluation and provisional information quarantine. --- **Description:** SCP-7842 is a collective designation for a series of identical, unmarked doors appearing within abandoned buildings worldwide. As of 2025, forty-seven instances have been confirmed in locations including disused hospitals, condemned apartment complexes, bankrupt government offices, abandoned subway stations, and structures officially recorded as demolished. Each door is made of dark-stained wood of indeterminate species and measures 2.13 m by 0.91 m. They possess no hinges visible from the exterior, labels, windows, locks, or manufacturer markings. All instances exhibit the same pattern of deep, semicircular scratches around the brass doorknob area, as if repeatedly gouged by fingernails, claws, or metal tools. Analysis of the scratches has found traces of human keratin, oxidized copper, concrete dust, and an unidentified black particulate. Opening any SCP-7842 instance leads not to the structure beyond it, but to a vast interior archive designated **Archive-7842**. The archive’s total dimensions are unknown. Surveying equipment has mapped over 860 km of corridors without locating an exterior boundary, central point, or repeatable spatial layout. Archive-7842 resembles an institutional records facility constructed from architectural styles spanning several centuries. Rooms may include marble rotundas containing filing cabinets, concrete shelving vaults beneath cathedral ceilings, fluorescent-lit office corridors opening onto library stacks, and climate-controlled storage rooms located behind doors that should spatially lead into solid walls. Gravity remains locally consistent, though vertical stairways have been observed connecting to rooms at incompatible angles. The archive contains an estimated billions of documents, data storage media, catalog cards, photographs, technical manuals, personal effects, and sealed evidence containers. Most materials concern a civilization referred to by varying names, including “The Continuity,” “The Human Administrative Compact,” “The Unified Custodial Authority,” and, most frequently, “The Foundation.” This civilization appears to have maintained a worldwide organization dedicated to the containment, documentation, and exploitation of anomalous phenomena. Documents consistently indicate that it suffered a global collapse following an event called the **Terminal Breach**, **the Last Containment Failure**, or simply **the Failure**. No recovered record has fully described the event. Several documents refer to “the rebuilding civilization” in terms implying that the current human world is a successor population with incomplete historical memory. Of particular concern are records apparently describing the modern SCP Foundation under the designation **The Custodial Directorate**. These records include personnel files for living Foundation employees, dated between 241 and 387 years before their recorded births. --- ### Addendum 7842.1: Initial Discovery SCP-7842 was first identified on 2024/09/18 in the basement of a condemned records office in ████████, Poland. Municipal demolition workers reported finding “a door where a boiler should have been.” One worker opened the door and entered briefly. He returned twelve minutes later carrying a folder containing his own employment history, including a termination notice dated 1891. Foundation assets embedded in local law enforcement secured the site within six hours. The following excerpt was recovered from the folder. > **RECORD OF CIVIL STATUS — SUBJECT: JAN KOWALSKI** > **Directorate Registry No.: 43-77-19-PL** > > Occupational Assignment: Municipal Demolition Laborer > Projected Identity Recovery Threshold: Not Expected > > Note: Subject displays recurring fixation on doorways and administrative spaces. This behavior is considered residual, not predictive. > > **Archive retention date:** 12 March 1891 > **Revision date:** 18 September 2024 > > *Do not permit subject to locate the archive entrance unaccompanied.* The worker, Jan Kowalski, was administered amnestics and placed under long-term observation. He has since drawn the scratch pattern found on SCP-7842 doors during sleep. --- ### Addendum 7842.2: Exploration Log 7842-04 **Exploration Team:** Dr. Elena Varga, Agent Bell, Agent Morita, Researcher Hale, and two Security personnel. **Entry Point:** SCP-7842-12, abandoned courthouse, ████████, Argentina. **Objective:** Locate records regarding the “Terminal Breach.” At 09:14, the team entered a corridor lined with shelves bearing paper files, wax-sealed cylinders, and transparent plastic data sheets. Every shelf label displayed a date, though the dates changed when viewed through camera feeds. At 09:37, Researcher Hale located a section marked: > **FAILURE EVENT MATERIALS — RESTRICTED BY ORDER OF THE REMAINING FIVE** The section had been partially emptied. Entire shelving units had been removed, leaving rectangular gaps in the dust. The floor bore fresh wheel marks despite the absence of visible doors large enough to accommodate equipment. At 09:41, Agent Morita observed movement at the far end of the aisle. The team recorded six humanoid entities wearing gray, sealed protective suits with opaque black visors. Their equipment resembled industrial hazmat gear, but each carried a rectangular apparatus that emitted a sustained low-frequency tone. The entities were designated SCP-7842-A. Dr. Varga ordered the team to approach slowly and identify themselves. Upon hearing the team’s footsteps, all six entities froze. One entity turned toward the team. Its visor displayed a line of white text: > **UNAUTHORIZED RETRIEVAL WITNESSED** The entities immediately abandoned two document carts and fled through an opening between shelves. The opening was not present when the team reached it. Video records show the entities passing through a solid brick wall. The recovered carts contained approximately 400 folders, most of which had been reduced to blank paper. Three retained partial legibility. > **CONTAINMENT FAILURE SUMMARY — PUBLIC VERSION, DRAFT 88** > > The Failure did not begin when containment was lost. It began when containment became the only thing left. > > Evacuation protocols are no longer applicable. There is no exterior population to evacuate. > > The Council has approved continuation of archival removal pending confirmation that the successor civilization has stabilized. A second document consisted of a handwritten memorandum. > Do not call them survivors. They are replacements. > > We told ourselves that if they rebuilt the cities, used the languages, inherited the names, then something of us had survived. > > But they do not remember the first sky. > > — Director A. M. Kettering The third was a partially burned catalog slip: > **TRANSFER AUTHORIZATION:** O5-██ / O5-██ / O5-██ > **Destination:** Unlisted > **Material:** Directorate personnel continuity files > **Reason:** Prevent recursive identification events. --- ### Addendum 7842.3: Personnel File Recovered from Archive-7842 On 2025/01/07, an exploration team recovered a sealed personnel file from a cabinet labeled **DIRECTORATE STAFF: ACTIVE/RETURNED**. The file concerned Dr. Elena Varga, who had participated in Exploration 7842-04. > **CUSTODIAL DIRECTORATE PERSONNEL FILE** > **Name:** Varga, Elena I. > **Clearance:** Black-4 > **Division:** Anomalous Records Preservation > **Date of Induction:** 17 June 1661 > **Current Status:** Reinstated following identity discontinuity. > > Psychological Notes: Subject continues to reject prior-service recollection. This is typical during early recurrence cycles. Recommend assignment away from self-referential archives until spontaneous recognition occurs. > > Special Instruction: If Subject encounters this file during a subsequent continuity, do not permit removal. Familiarity accelerates destabilization. Dr. Varga has no verified ancestry prior to 1898 due to the destruction of municipal records during a fire. She was placed on administrative leave following the recovery. --- ### Addendum 7842.4: Unauthorized O5 Presence On 2025/02/12, Security Officer Reyes reviewed footage from Exploration 7842-07 and identified a group of three individuals entering Archive-7842 approximately nineteen minutes before the authorized team’s arrival. The individuals wore contemporary Foundation tactical uniforms without insignia. Facial recognition software identified them with 93%, 89%, and 87% confidence as O5-2, O5-7, and O5-11 respectively. When questioned through formal channels, all three Council members denied entering SCP-7842 or authorizing personnel to do so. The footage contains the following audio, recovered from a damaged body camera: > **UNIDENTIFIED MALE VOICE:** This wing was supposed to be gone. > > **UNIDENTIFIED FEMALE VOICE:** It is gone. It keeps coming back when we need it. > > **UNIDENTIFIED MALE VOICE:** And the teams? > > **UNIDENTIFIED FEMALE VOICE:** Still removing evidence. Still pretending they are saving us. > > **UNIDENTIFIED MALE VOICE:** Are they? > > *[Nine seconds of static.]* > > **UNIDENTIFIED FEMALE VOICE:** They saved enough to make us again. At 14:03, the three figures entered a restricted archive section marked with the same scratched pattern found around the knobs of all SCP-7842 doors. The door closed behind them. No corresponding exit footage exists. At 14:18, the authorized exploration team entered the same corridor. The restricted door had vanished. In its place was a bare concrete wall bearing a single message in fresh black particulate: > **THE ARCHIVE IS NOT A RECORD OF THE FAILURE.** > **IT IS THE PART THAT SURVIVED IT.**
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This SCP entry demonstrates strong adherence to the Foundation's clinical documentation style while weaving a complex narrative about cyclical civilizations and institutional memory. The author successfully captures the characteristic tone of SCP entries - detached, scientific, yet increasingly unsettling as anomalous details accumulate. The piece excels in its structural authenticity, following SCP format conventions precisely while building layers of existential horror. The concept of a previous Foundation-like organization that suffered a "Terminal Breach" creates compelling recursive mythology. The gradual revelation that current Foundation personnel may be reincarnations or reconstructions of previous civilization members is genuinely unsettling and well-executed. The writing maintains consistent clinical voice throughout, which serves the horror effectively - the matter-of-fact presentation of impossible architecture and temporal paradoxes enhances rather than diminishes their impact. Specific details like the scratch patterns, the changing shelf labels, and the entities' hazmat suits create vivid imagery within the constraints of technical documentation. However, the piece suffers from some pacing issues in the middle sections. The exploration logs, while atmospheric, occasionally feel repetitive in their revelations. The dialogue from the recovered audio is functional but lacks distinctive character voices - though this may be intentional given the clinical context. The ending message written in "black particulate" provides a strong conceptual conclusion that reframes the entire archive's purpose, though it borders on being overly cryptic. The piece successfully avoids purple prose while maintaining descriptive richness, and the horror emerges organically from the implications rather than explicit descriptions. The characterization is necessarily limited by the SCP format, but individual researchers feel distinct enough within those constraints. Dr. Varga's discovery of her own centuries-old personnel file creates genuine psychological horror. The unauthorized O5 presence adds institutional paranoia that fits the Foundation's established lore. Overall, this is a sophisticated piece of SCP writing that demonstrates strong understanding of both the format's requirements and its potential for existential horror. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 14 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 8 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Classic sci-fi: Offworlder (Le Guin) — Score: 86.2
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi: Offworlder (Author style: Le Guin) Write a chapter following a young diplomatic envoy sent to address tensions on a distant human colony where humanity has diverged into distinct subspecies through both natural adaptation and separation of lineages. Against protocol, the envoy slips away from official functions to walk the streets alone, encountering markets, ordinary citizens, and social structures that contradict the official briefings. Upon returning to their quarters, the envoy is reprimanded by Command, and is tasked with approaching the stationed Minister of Science (a political posting rather than scientific). A challenging conversation ensues, in which the envoy's wits and will are tested, by a seasoned political operator looking to test the mettle of the offworlder. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
I can’t write in Ursula K. Le Guin’s exact style, but I can write an original chapter with a reflective, anthropological, quietly political classic-science-fiction tone. --- The city had been built into the side of a mountain that no map of Earth had ever named. From the embassy aerie, Vela had seen only its uppermost terraces: walls of pale gray stone, narrow towers, the glint of metal roofs under the wan light of Sere. The mountain descended beneath cloud, and the city with it. At night the windows appeared and disappeared in the mist like a constellation trying to remember its shape. The official name was Asterion Settlement, Third Continental Administrative Zone. Nobody in the briefings called it that. They called it the Colony. The briefings had described its people in orderly tables. The Highborn: descended from the first low-gravity construction crews, taller by nearly a head than baseline human, narrow in shoulder and hip, their bones latticed and light. Adapted to the mountain plateaus, the upper wards, the administrative air. The Deepfolk: compact, heavy-limbed, descendants of the mine crews and agricultural laborers transferred into the lowlands two centuries after landfall. Broad nostrils, dense blood, exceptional tolerance for heat and pressure. The Littoral: a newer designation, disputed; the descendants of mixed settlements along the western salt flats, with darkened irises and skin that bore the yellow cast of Sere’s harsh spectrum. There were other divisions: Lineage C, the so-called Glass Children, altered before departure and officially extinct; the old families of the vessel-holds; the unregistered, whose classification was absent from all official tables. “Distinct populations,” said the briefing documents. “Social sensitivities,” said the command officers. “Cultural residues,” said the archive notes. Vela had learned, at the Academy, that a phrase like cultural residues usually meant people had been made inconvenient by history. On her second evening in Asterion, she was expected at a reception in the Hall of Concordance. The governor’s household would attend. So would the city’s religious interpreters, the security council, the chief magistrate, and, most importantly, Minister Hal Arad, the stationed Minister of Science. Vela had spent two hours being dressed for the event by an embassy attendant named Orm, who was himself Highborn and did not conceal his amusement at her compact offworld proportions. “You will be too warm,” he said, fastening the collar of her gray envoy’s coat. “But it is proper to be too warm in the Hall.” “Why?” “Because the governor is warm,” Orm said. Vela looked at him. Orm smiled with only one side of his mouth. “You will learn.” When he had gone to fetch the formal sash, Vela removed the coat, put on a plain dark tunic from her travel case, and stepped through the service door. It was not difficult. Protocol depended largely on everyone’s willingness to believe in it. The service stairs spiraled down through the embassy’s outer wall. At each landing were stone panels older than the embassy, preserved when the offworld compound had been built against the cliff. The carvings were worn, but visible: a woman standing before a tree whose branches ended in stars; a blind king holding a broken wheel; a man with wings falling into waves shaped like hands. Vela paused before the winged figure. Below it, someone had cut newer glyphs into the stone: not official script, but street marks. A circle crossed by three lines. A tall figure bent over a small one. A ladder without rungs. She did not know what they meant. Outside, the air was cold and carried a metallic scent from the rain basins. The street fell steeply between buildings. Most were built of the same gray stone as the mountain itself, their facades inset with carved lintels and doorposts. The old Earth myths were everywhere, broken and rearranged. A bull’s head over a bakery. A many-eyed giant holding an umbrella over a public cistern. A serpent eating the sun over a shop that sold electrical wire. The city had taken the myths with it across thirty-two light-years and made them local. Vela walked downward. At first she was conspicuous. Her hair, kept short in the envoy fashion, marked her as offworld. Her gait marked her too: she placed her weight too heavily, still unaccustomed to the fractional difference in gravity. People looked, then looked away. At the first market she understood that the briefings had omitted not only facts but scale. The market filled a wide stepped square beneath a stone arcade. Steam rose from cooking vats. Vendors sold pale tubers, dried red fish from the western lakes, coils of translucent fungus, machine parts, embroidered cloth, cheap prayer tokens, and polished fragments of skyglass. Children moved through the crowd with trays of tea balanced on their heads. The Highborn walked among the Deepfolk and the Littoral, but not together. It was not a law. It was more exact than a law. The Highborn took the upper steps, where the air was thinner and cleaner. Their stalls sold books, instruments, imported fabrics, small machines in sealed cases. The Deepfolk occupied the central floor, shoulder to shoulder among the food sellers and repair tables. Along the lower runoff channel, where the water from the market drains collected, the Littoral vendors worked from temporary mats. There were no barriers. There were only paths no one crossed. A woman selling broth caught Vela watching. “You will get wet there,” she said. Vela stepped back from the edge of the channel. “Thank you.” The woman was Deepfolk, perhaps forty, though Vela could not tell. Her face was round and creased, her hands broad. She ladled broth into a cup and held it out. “I have no local currency,” Vela said. “Then you are an envoy.” “That obvious?” “You have the shoes of someone who expects pavement.” Vela accepted the cup. The broth smelled of pepper and root starch. “What do people call this place?” she asked. The woman looked upward, toward the embassy tiers lost in mist. “The market?” “The city.” “Asterion, if a clerk asks. The Mountain, if a grandmother asks. Home, if you are foolish.” “And if you are not foolish?” The woman took back the ladle. “Then you call it where you are.” A boy appeared beside the stall, thin as a reed, with the dark-gold eyes Vela had seen in the Littoral district records. He whispered something to the woman. “Tell her yourself,” the woman said. The boy stared at Vela. “Are you here to count us?” Vela felt the question land in her like a stone. “No,” she said. He considered this. “Then what are you for?” The correct answer came easily: diplomatic assessment, conflict mediation, intergovernmental rapport. She had said such things in training until the words had gone smooth as coins. “I’m trying to learn,” she said. The woman made a sound that was not quite laughter. The boy nodded solemnly, as if Vela had passed or failed some test beyond her knowledge. Then he vanished into the crowd. She stayed in the market until the bells of the upper ward rang the seventh hour. On the way back, she saw a procession moving up the central stair. At its head walked a line of Highborn adolescents in white ceremonial veils. Behind them came Deepfolk workers carrying stone tablets on their shoulders. The tablets bore the old carved signs: the tree, the wheel, the falling man. At the rear, watched by two civic guards, came a dozen Littoral children carrying buckets of whitewash. “Festival?” Vela asked an old man beside her. “Correction,” he said. “What is being corrected?” He spat into the gutter. “The walls remember too much.” When Vela returned to her quarters, Orm was waiting in the sitting room with the formal sash folded over one arm. Beside him, the wall screen shone with the blue seal of Command. “Envoy Vela,” said the screen. “Commander.” “You left the compound without escort.” “Yes.” “You failed to attend a formal reception arranged in your honor.” “Yes.” “You entered an uncontrolled civilian area during a period of heightened factional tension.” “I entered a market.” “Do not make the distinction sound reassuring.” Vela stood still. Through the window, the city lights trembled in the cloud. The commander’s image leaned closer, though it was only a recording delayed by transmission relays. “You were sent to reduce uncertainty, Envoy. Not to embody it.” “I was sent to understand the situation.” “You were sent to represent the Compact.” “Those may not be the same task.” There was a brief silence in the room. Orm’s eyes widened very slightly. Then Command said, “Minister Arad has requested a meeting. You will attend tomorrow morning. You will apologize for your conduct. You will listen.” The seal vanished. Orm placed the sash on the table. “You have made yourself interesting,” he said. “Is that dangerous?” “In Asterion,” he said, “it is rarely useful.” Minister Hal Arad received her in a room with no scientific instruments at all. There were books, however. Hundreds of them, arranged in shelves that climbed to the vaulted ceiling. The room had once been a temple, perhaps, or a court. Its stone walls were inscribed with figures from old Earth: Prometheus with his liver exposed to a bird; Cassandra speaking to a crowd of faceless men; a woman holding a severed thread. Arad stood before a window, his hands folded behind his back. He was Highborn, but old enough that the height seemed less an advantage than an architectural inconvenience. He bent slightly at the shoulders, and his long face had the dry, watchful quality of a bird’s. “Envoy,” he said. “Did you enjoy our city?” “I saw very little of it.” “Ah. A diplomatic answer.” “I thought it was an honest one.” He turned. “No. An honest answer would contain an accusation.” Vela waited. “Sit,” he said. There were two chairs. One was ordinary. The other was built high, its legs elongated for Highborn proportions. Arad took the high chair without comment. Vela sat opposite him. “I have read your reports,” he said. “I have submitted none.” “Exactly.” He smiled then, mildly. “You have been given a mission brief,” Arad continued. “It explains that tensions exist among our population categories. It explains that certain extremists wish to revive obsolete hereditary claims. It explains that the Compact’s interest is stability.” “It does.” “And after one walk through a market, you believe the brief is incomplete.” “I believe it was designed to be incomplete.” “Designed by whom?” “By people who benefit from what it leaves out.” Arad’s expression did not change. “And what does it leave out?” Vela thought of the children with whitewash buckets. The paths through the market. The boy asking whether she had come to count them. “Who is permitted to define the categories,” she said. “Who is counted. Who is made visible only as a problem.” For the first time, Arad looked pleased. “Good,” he said. “You are not entirely an instrument.” “Is that what you wanted to know?” “It is what everyone wants to know about an offworld envoy. Whether you are a person, or merely a corridor through which distant power walks.” Vela felt heat rise in her face. “And what are you?” “The Minister of Science.” “You are not a scientist.” “No.” He looked up at Prometheus carved above the window. “I am a minister. Science is useful because it sounds clean.” Outside, wind moved through the mountain city. Somewhere below, a bell rang once, then again. Arad leaned forward. “You will be offered facts,” he said. “Censuses. Gene maps. Atmospheric tolerances. Historical charts. You will be told that the Deepfolk cannot govern the upper wards because of physiology, that the Littoral cannot be trusted with water allocation because of tradition, that the Highborn have a natural duty to administer because they have done so for generations.” “And none of it is true?” “Oh, much of it is true. That is the elegance of it.” Vela said nothing. “The question,” said Arad, “is whether truth is the same thing as justice. Young envoys often arrive believing that if they collect enough accurate information, policy will become moral by arithmetic.” “And seasoned ministers?” “Seasoned ministers know arithmetic is often where morality goes to hide.” He rose. The interview was over, or meant to be. At the door, Vela stopped. “Why tell me this?” Arad’s eyes rested on her with an old, difficult patience. “Because Command sent you here to make peace,” he said. “The governor intends to use you to certify order. The factions intend to use you as a witness. And I”—he glanced at the stone Cassandra, forever unheard—“intend to discover whether you can survive being useful without becoming owned.” Vela opened the door. “Can I?” she asked. Behind her, the minister’s answer came softly. “That depends on whether you walk alone again.”
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of Le Guin's style and thematic concerns, successfully creating a thoughtful science fiction narrative that explores social stratification, colonialism, and political complexity. The author wisely disclaims attempting to exactly replicate Le Guin's voice while delivering something authentically in her tradition. The world-building is exceptionally well-crafted, presenting a stratified society where human subspecies have developed distinct physical and social characteristics. The three-tiered system (Highborn, Deepfolk, Littoral) feels organic rather than contrived, and the author skillfully shows how these divisions manifest in daily life through spatial arrangements in the market and social interactions. Vela emerges as a compelling protagonist whose curiosity and moral instincts drive her to transgress diplomatic protocol. Her character development feels natural as she moves from dutiful envoy to someone questioning the systems she represents. The supporting characters, particularly Minister Arad, are well-realized with distinct voices and complex motivations. The prose maintains an appropriately measured, anthropological tone throughout, with elegant descriptive passages that never become overwrought. The imagery of the mountain city, the market dynamics, and the carved mythological figures creates a vivid sense of place while serving thematic purposes about memory, power, and cultural adaptation. The dialogue, especially between Vela and Arad, crackles with intelligence and subtext. Arad's final exchange about surviving usefulness without becoming owned encapsulates the story's central tension beautifully. The conversations feel authentic to these characters and advance both plot and theme. The structure moves logically from exploration to confrontation to revelation, building tension effectively. The ending avoids false optimism while suggesting possibilities for growth and resistance. The piece successfully balances political complexity with human drama, never becoming didactic despite its serious themes. Minor weaknesses include some exposition that could be more seamlessly integrated, and occasionally the symbolic elements (carved figures, processions) feel slightly heavy-handed, though they serve the narrative well overall. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Biopunk: Polymerase Chain Reaction — Score: 83.7
Prompt:
Biopunk -- Polymerase Chain Reaction Write a chapter set in a near-future post-apocalyptic world where corporate genetic engineering was designed with built-in obsolescence—engineered DNA lasting only one generation to maximize profits before societal collapse. Follow a biohacker working with salvaged equipment to splice viable food crops for their settlement using rapidly degrading genetic material. As world building / window dressing, include details about jury-rigged lab equipment, and the physical toll (and unexpected upsides) of using untested genetic modifications as medicine. The plot of this chapter is centred around a black market contract. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The solar rig on the roof coughed light through the cracked skylight in blue-white pulses. Every time the voltage dipped, the salvaged thermocycler reset its timer with a sullen chirp. Its original casing had been stamped with the sunburst logo of VerdantGen Nutritional Systems, back when people still trusted logos. Now its lid was held shut with a strip of copper wire and a spring taken from a dead elevator button. A motorcycle battery sat beside it in a puddle of distilled water, feeding current through a hand-wound regulator coil. The LCD had lost half its pixels. **9—°C** **DEN—TURE** **CYC— 1?/3?** Mara watched the numbers stagger and hoped the machine remembered how to count. On the bench beside her, twelve glass capillary tubes held the future of Wren Settlement: damp kernels of maize, each no larger than a tooth. Their parent stock had been found in a collapsed cold-storage warehouse fifty kilometers west, sealed in a branded seed vault beneath cases of designer yogurt cultures. The kernels had survived twenty-two years of blackout, drought, looting, fungal blight, and the small, patient appetite of time. What had not survived was their genetic warranty. Corporate crops had been built that way. VerdantGen, HelixHarvest, NewEden Foods—every pre-Collapse agricultural conglomerate had used the same trick. Their seeds germinated beautifully. They grew fast, resisted drought, expressed vitamins, insecticides, antibiotics, and sometimes pharmaceuticals. For one season they fed cities. Then came the planned genetic decay. The second generation inherited a timed self-sabotage sequence: unstable telomere repeats, heat-sensitive repair enzymes, sterility cassettes triggered by ordinary soil fungi. The companies called it Intellectual Property Continuity. Farmers called it the Year-Two Curse. After the Collapse, nobody called it anything except hunger. Mara adjusted the thermocycler’s lid and held it closed with two fingers when the rig flickered again. “Come on,” she muttered. “Denature.” The machine beeped, uncertainly. Across the room, a child coughed in her sleep. Kesh lay curled beneath a quilt in the far corner of the lab, one thin arm outside the blankets, translucent in the intermittent solar light. Her fever had gone down after Mara’s last treatment, though the treatment had turned the girl’s hair silver at the temples and made the whites of her eyes glow faintly green in darkness. Unexpected upsides, Mara had learned, were still upsides. Kesh had stopped having night terrors. She said she could see mice moving inside the walls. Yesterday she had correctly identified which of three water barrels had algae contamination without opening them. Medicine now was mostly borrowed agriculture. The old corporate gene libraries had been full of “nutraceutical expression packages”—rice that produced vaccines, tomatoes that secreted insulin analogues, algae engineered to bind heavy metals, fungal cultures that manufactured antivirals. None of it had been tested outside proprietary trials. None of it had been designed for people who measured doses with kitchen scales and administered them through reused syringe filters. Mara had treated Kesh’s marrow rot with a modified cassava virus vector designed, according to a partial patent abstract, to repair radiation-damaged blood stem cells in livestock. It had worked. Mostly. Kesh’s blood clotting was perfect now. Too perfect, perhaps. She bruised less. She healed cuts in hours. Once, after slicing her palm on a broken ampoule, she had stared at the wound sealing itself and whispered, “I’m hard to kill.” Mara had not known whether to smile. The outer door rattled. Mara’s hand moved automatically to the nail gun beneath the bench. The nail gun had no nails in it—those were too valuable—but the compressed-air reservoir still had enough pressure for a single metal bolt. She had used it once on a raider. The bolt had gone through his calf and into the dirt. He had screamed for a long time. The door rattled again, three knocks, then two. Not a settlement signal. A contract signal. Mara turned off the bench lamp. The room became a strobe-lit cave: jars of agar glowing amber, improvised incubators made from insulated lunch coolers, a centrifuge assembled from a bicycle wheel and an old washing machine motor. Along one wall, obsolete corporate sequencing cartridges hung on hooks like relics. The labels had faded, but Mara could still make out the promises. **ROOTMAX™** **LIFESPAN: 18 MONTHS** **RENEWAL PLAN REQUIRED** She opened the door a hand’s width. The woman outside wore an oilskin cloak patched with transparent packaging film. Her face was narrow and wind-burned, her scalp shaved except for a braid of black hair down the center. Behind her stood two men with trade rifles and plastic face shields. “Doctor Vale?” the woman asked. “Not a doctor.” “Biohacker, then.” “Depends who’s asking.” The woman held up both gloved hands. “Sable. I represent buyers from the river markets.” “River markets don’t send armed representatives for vegetables.” “These aren’t vegetables.” Mara said nothing. Sable’s eyes flicked past her into the lab, taking in the cycler, the battery, the sleeping child, the rows of maize embryos under grow lights. “You’re working on the old M-47 line,” Sable said. “No.” “Drought-tolerant maize. First-generation fertile if the sterility cassette is excised before germination. High starch, high protein. Needs a repair scaffold for the chromosomal breakpoints.” “No.” “Your denials are less convincing than most.” Mara tightened her grip on the door. “If you know that much, you know the repair scaffold is the problem.” Sable smiled without warmth. “That is why I’m here.” One of the guards brought forward a metal case. It was an old emergency medical cooler, the kind hospitals used for organ transport. The hinges were polished from use. “Inside,” Sable said, “is a vial of HelixHarvest germline polymerase. Original stock. Cryo-preserved. The enzyme complex can stabilize degraded reproductive sequences long enough to propagate three, maybe four generations.” Mara’s breath caught. Three generations. For Wren, three generations was not an abstraction. It was three harvests. Three years without sending scavenging teams into poisoned suburbs. Three years of children eating more than ration porridge. Three years to rebuild the greenhouse roof, deepen the wells, perhaps find another seed vault. “What do your buyers want?” she asked. “Twenty viable founder plants.” Mara laughed once. “For a whole vial?” “Twenty plants capable of breeding true for at least three generations.” “That’s not twenty plants. That’s a kingdom.” “They have land.” “Everyone has land. It’s food they don’t have.” Sable stepped closer. Rainwater dripped from her cloak hem onto the threshold. “They also have prisoners. And guns. Their current food supply is fungal protein and boiled leather. They need a crop they can control.” Mara looked at the metal case. The corporate polymerase was the missing piece. She had been amplifying damaged DNA with enzyme blends scavenged from diagnostic kits, veterinary labs, even a children’s science museum. The sequences kept unraveling. Each correction created two more errors. Her maize embryos might sprout, but their offspring would likely collapse into sterile, tumor-ridden stalks. The vial could change that. “What else?” Mara asked. Sable’s expression sharpened. “You’ll splice in a dependency trait.” “No.” “The crop will require a synthetic amino acid additive to remain fertile.” “No.” “Call it a nutrient supplement. A proprietary soil amendment. The buyers control distribution; their tenants get food. Everyone remains organized.” “That’s the Year-Two Curse with better branding.” “It’s survival.” “It’s slavery with corn.” Sable’s two guards shifted their rifles. Inside, the thermocycler gave a flat electronic squeal. The solar panel had dipped again. The screen went black. Then, after a second, it came back: **CYCLE 32/35** Mara felt something unclench in her chest. Sable heard the machine and glanced toward it. “You don’t have much time. Degradation is exponential once the old sequences thaw.” Mara knew. The salvaged maize DNA was already fragmenting. It had spent decades asleep, but waking it had begun the clock. Every hour in solution was another hour of molecular rot. Her repaired embryos would need to be transformed before dawn, planted before the next heat wave, protected from the rust fungus drifting east on the dry winds. She also knew black-market contracts never ended when the payment changed hands. “Twenty founder plants,” Mara said slowly. “No dependency trait.” Sable shook her head. “Then you can take your polymerase to someone who’ll make you a famine with a logo.” The woman’s mouth hardened. “You’re in no position to moralize.” “No,” Mara said. “I’m in a position to bargain.” She opened the door wider and let Sable see the lab. Not the good workbench—the other one. The locked cabinet. The fungal vats. The jars marked with hazard symbols cut from old battery labels. The homemade nebulizer. The tray of modified nightshade leaves whose edges glimmered with bioluminescent blue. “You want leverage?” Mara said. “I have a settlement of eighty-three people who owe their lives to things I’ve grown in this room. Some of those things are edible. Some are medicinal. Some make a person’s skin blister off in sheets if they breathe the pollen.” That last part was an exaggeration. Probably. Sable studied her for a long moment. “What are your terms?” “One founder line goes to your buyers. The rest stay here. No fertility lock. No dependency cassette. And the contract includes seed distribution to three unaffiliated settlements.” “Impossible.” “Then walk.” Kesh stirred beneath the quilt. In the dark, her eyes opened, green and luminous. The guards saw them. One of them made the sign against contamination. Kesh sat up slowly. “Mara,” she said. Her voice was rough with sleep. “The mice are running.” Outside, beyond the lab, something moved through the rain. Not mice. A chorus of boots, too many to count, approached from the road. Sable’s hand went to her coat. “Your people?” Mara listened. Wren’s watch had a different step: cautious, limping, familiar. These boots came in formation. “No,” she said. The solar rig flickered once, twice, then surged. Every grow light in the lab blazed on at full strength. The centrifuge whined. The thermocycler chirped triumphantly. **CYCLE COMPLETE** Mara looked at Sable, then at the medical cooler, then at the twelve tiny embryos waiting in their tubes. “Looks like,” she said, reaching for the nail gun, “we’ll have to negotiate quickly.”
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This biopunk chapter demonstrates strong technical execution and worldbuilding while delivering a tense, atmospheric narrative. The author successfully creates a convincing post-apocalyptic setting where corporate genetic engineering has created systemic food insecurity through planned obsolescence—a clever extrapolation of current agricultural practices. The worldbuilding excels through specific, technical details that feel authentic without becoming exposition-heavy. The jury-rigged thermocycler with its broken LCD display, the motorcycle battery power source, and the repurposed equipment create a vivid sense of technological scavenging. The concept of "Year-Two Curse" and corporate genetic warranties provides compelling backstory that drives current conflicts. Mara emerges as a well-developed protagonist with clear motivations and moral complexity. Her relationship with Kesh adds emotional weight while demonstrating the story's themes about untested genetic modifications. The child's transformation—silver hair, glowing eyes, enhanced healing—provides concrete examples of both the benefits and risks of biohacking medicine. The dialogue feels natural and serves multiple purposes: character development, tension building, and information delivery. Sable's negotiation with Mara reveals character through subtext, particularly in their conflicting philosophies about survival versus exploitation. The pacing builds effectively toward the cliffhanger ending, though the arrival of mysterious boots feels somewhat convenient. The central conflict between Mara's ethics and survival needs creates genuine tension without easy answers. Technical writing quality is consistently strong. Sentences flow well, imagery is vivid without being overwrought, and the voice remains consistent throughout. The author successfully balances scientific terminology with accessibility. Some minor weaknesses include the somewhat predictable negotiation structure and the convenient timing of external threats. However, these don't significantly detract from the overall effectiveness. The piece successfully fulfills all prompt requirements while creating an engaging standalone chapter that could work within a larger narrative.
